Think of a **classroom seating arrangement problem**:
- You are a teacher with 4 students (A, B, C, D) and 4 desks (1, 2, 3, 4), arranged in different locations.
- Each student has a different "productivity score" depending on which desk they sit at.
- You want to **assign each student to exactly one desk** so that the **total productivity is maximized** (or total distraction is minimized).
- Crucially, **no student can sit at two desks**, and **no desk can hold two students**.

This is the essence of an Assignment Problem: a perfect matching in a bipartite graph.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> The Assignment Problem is often solved using the **Hungarian Method** (developed by Harold W. Kuhn in 1955), but **formulation** itself is a vital exam topic. The MODI method is also sometimes called the **u-v method** in older textbooks.

---

## 1.3 Standard Mathematical Modeling Metrics

The following **standard metrics** are universally used:

- **$n$**: total number of jobs/tasks (= number of machines/workers).
- **$c_{ij}$**: cost of assigning $i^{th}$ worker to $j^{th}$ job.
- **$x_{ij}$**: binary decision variable (0 or 1).
- **$m + n - 1$**: number of basic feasible solutions in Transportation (degeneracy check).
- **$(u_i, v_j)$**: row and column potentials (dual variables).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:**
> Assignment Problem is *not* just "a small transportation problem." The structure $a_i = 1, b_j = 1$ and the integrality property makes it solvable in polynomial time $O(n^3)$ via the Hungarian Method, whereas general LPP is exponential.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 MODI Method – Step-by-Step Logic

The MODI Method is executed in the following structured phases:

### Phase 1: Obtain an Initial Basic Feasible Solution (IBFS)
Use any one of the following:
- North-West Corner Rule
- Least Cost Method
- Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM) – generally yields the closest-to-optimal solution.

### Phase 2: Compute the Dual Variables ($u_i$ and $v_j$)
- For every **occupied cell** $(i,j)$ in the IBFS, the equation $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$ must hold.
- The number of such equations is $m + n - 1$ (since one cell is kept as a degenerate zero).
- Set **$u_1 = 0$** (or any $u_i = 0$) and solve the system.

### Phase 3: Compute the Opportunity Cost ($\Delta_{ij}$) for Unoccupied Cells
For every unoccupied cell $(i,j)$:
$$\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - (u_i + v_j)$$

### Phase 4: Test for Optimality
- If **all** $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$ for minimization, the IBFS is **optimal**.
- If any $\Delta_{ij} < 0$, select the cell with the **most negative** opportunity cost and proceed to Phase 5.

### Phase 5: Construct a Closed Loop & Reallocate
- For the chosen cell, draw a closed path alternating horizontal and vertical movements starting and ending at the cell.
- Assign **$+\theta$** to the chosen cell, **$-\theta$** to the next, **$+\theta$** to the next, and so on.
- The value of $\theta$ is the **smallest allocation** among the negatively signed cells.
- Update the allocation matrix and **return to Phase 2**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight – Why it works:**
> The MODI Method exploits the **Duality Theorem of Linear Programming**. The dual of the Transportation Problem involves $u_i$ and $v_j$ as dual variables. The complementary slackness theorem ensures that $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$ for basic cells and $u_i + v_j \leq c_{ij}$ for non-basic cells at optimality.

---

## 2.2 Assignment Problem – Mathematical Formulation

### Decision Variables
$$x_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if job } j \text{ is assigned to worker } i \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### Objective Function (Minimization)
$$\text{Minimize } Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} x_{ij}$$

### Constraints
- **Each job must be assigned to exactly one worker:**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1, \quad \forall j = 1, 2, \dots, n$$
- **Each worker must be assigned exactly one job:**
$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$
- **Non-negativity & Integrality:**
$$x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Important Note on Integrality:**
> Because the constraint matrix of the Assignment Problem is **totally unimodular**, the integrality constraint $x_{ij} \in \{0,1\}$ is automatically satisfied even if we relax it to $x_{ij} \geq 0$. This is why the LP relaxation yields integer solutions.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Remarks |
|---|---------|----------------|---------|
| 1 | MODI – Dual Equation | $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$ | Holds for **occupied** cells only |
| 2 | MODI – Opportunity Cost | $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - u_i - v_j$ | Computed for **unoccupied** cells |
| 3 | MODI – Optimality Condition | $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0 \; \forall (i,j)$ unoccupied | For minimization problems |
| 4 | Number of Basic Cells | $m + n - 1$ | If less, solution is **degenerate** |
| 5 | Transportation Supply | $\sum a_i = \sum b_j$ | **Balanced** problem requirement |
| 6 | Assignment – Row Constraint | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1$ | Each worker takes 1 job |
| 7 | Assignment – Column Constraint | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1$ | Each job is done by 1 worker |
| 8 | Binary Variable | $x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$ | One-to-one assignment |
| 9 | Total Number of Assignments | $n$ (in an $n \times n$ matrix) | Always a perfect matching |
| 10 | Maximization Conversion | Subtract from max: $c'_{ij} = M - c_{ij}$ | $M = \max(c_{ij})$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> - **MODI Method** is heavily used in *supply chain optimization software* (e.g., SAP, Oracle SCM).
> - **Assignment Problems** are foundational in *job-shop scheduling*, *crew rostering* in airlines (Air India, IndiGo), *task allocation* in cloud computing, and *vehicle routing* in logistics (Amazon, Flipkart delivery fleet management).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: MODI Method

**Problem Statement:**
A company has 3 factories (A, B, C) supplying goods to 3 warehouses (1, 2, 3). The transportation cost per unit (in ₹) is given below. The supplies are 50, 40, 70 units, and the demands are 60, 50, 50 units. Find the optimal solution using the **MODI Method**.

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | Supply $a_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 4 | 8 | 8 | 50 |
| **B** | 16 | 8 | 16 | 40 |
| **C** | 8 | 16 | 24 | 70 |
| **Demand $b_j$** | 60 | 50 | 50 | **140** |

### Step 1: Check Balance
$$\sum a_i = 50 + 40 + 70 = 140$$
$$\sum b_j = 60 + 50 + 50 = 140$$
**Balanced** ✓

### Step 2: Obtain IBFS using Least Cost Method
- Lowest cost: $c_{A1} = 4$. Allocate $\min(50, 60) = 50$ units. Row A exhausted.
- Next lowest: $c_{B2} = 8$. Allocate $\min(40, 50) = 40$ units. Row B exhausted.
- Next: $c_{C1} = 8$ (but W1 has $60-50=10$ left). Allocate 10 units. W1 exhausted.
- Next: $c_{C2} = 16$. Allocate $\min(70, 50-40=10) = 10$ units. W2 exhausted.
- Last: $c_{C3} = 24$. Allocate remaining $70-10-10=50$ units. W3 exhausted.

**IBFS Allocations:**

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | **50** | 8 | 8 | 50 |
| **B** | 16 | **40** | 16 | 40 |
| **C** | **10** | **10** | **50** | 70 |
| **Demand** | 60 | 50 | 50 | 140 |

**Total cost (IBFS)**:
$$Z = 50(4) + 40(8) + 10(8) + 10(16) + 50(24) = 200 + 320 + 80 + 160 + 1200 = \text{₹}1960$$

Number of allocations = 5 = $m + n - 1 = 3 + 3 - 1$ ✓ (Non-degenerate)

### Step 3: Solve for $u_i$ and $v_j$ using $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$ for occupied cells
Set $u_A = 0$:
- Cell (A, W1): $0 + v_1 = 4 \Rightarrow v_1 = 4$
- Cell (B, W2): $u_B + v_2 = 8 \Rightarrow u_B = 8 - v_2$. We need $v_2$.
- Cell (C, W1): $u_C + v_1 = 8 \Rightarrow u_C = 8 - 4 = 4$
- Cell (C, W2): $u_C + v_2 = 16 \Rightarrow 4 + v_2 = 16 \Rightarrow v_2 = 12$
- Now: $u_B = 8 - 12 = -4$
- Cell (C, W3): $u_C + v_3 = 24 \Rightarrow 4 + v_3 = 24 \Rightarrow v_3 = 20$

**Resulting potentials:**

| $u_A = 0$ | $u_B = -4$ | $u_C = 4$ |
|---|---|---|

| $v_1 = 4$ | $v_2 = 12$ | $v_3 = 20$ |
|---|---|---|

### Step 4: Compute Opportunity Costs $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - u_i - v_j$

| Cell | $c_{ij}$ | $u_i$ | $v_j$ | $\Delta_{ij}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (A, W2) | 8 | 0 | 12 | $8 - 0 - 12 = \mathbf{-4}$ |
| (A, W3) | 8 | 0 | 20 | $8 - 0 - 20 = -12$ |
| (B, W1) | 16 | -4 | 4 | $16 + 4 - 4 = 16$ |
| (B, W3) | 16 | -4 | 20 | $16 + 4 - 20 = 0$ |

### Step 5: Test Optimality
The most negative opportunity cost is $\Delta_{A3} = -12$. So the current solution is **NOT optimal**.

### Step 6: Construct Closed Loop for cell (A, W3)
Starting at (A, W3), the closed path through occupied cells:
- $(A, W3) \to (C, W3) \to (C, W1) \to (A, W1) \to \text{back to } (A, W3)$

| | W1 | W2 | W3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 50 (-) | 8 | **$+\theta$** |
| **B** | 16 | 40 | 16 |
| **C** | 10 (+) | 10 | 50 (-) |

The negatively signed cells are (A, W1) with 50 and (C, W3) with 50. Minimum = **$\theta = 50$**.

### Step 7: Update Allocations

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 8 | 50 | 50 |
| **B** | 16 | 40 | 16 | 40 |
| **C** | 60 | 10 | 0 | 70 |
| **Demand** | 60 | 50 | 50 | 140 |

New total cost:
$$Z = 50(8) + 40(8) + 60(8) + 10(16) = 400 + 320 + 480 + 160 = \text{₹}1360$$

### Step 8: Recompute Potentials
Set $u_A = 0$:
- Cell (A, W3): $0 + v_3 = 8 \Rightarrow v_3 = 8$
- Cell (B, W2): $u_B + v_2 = 8$. Need $v_2$.
- Cell (C, W1): $u_C + v_1 = 8 \Rightarrow v_1 = 8 - u_C$.
- Cell (C, W2): $u_C + v_2 = 16 \Rightarrow v_2 = 16 - u_C$.
- So $u_B + 16 - u_C = 8 \Rightarrow u_B = u_C - 8$.
- Choose $u_C$ to balance – we can use (B,W1) but it's unoccupied. Set $u_B = 0$ and recompute: Let $u_B = 0 \Rightarrow v_2 = 8 \Rightarrow u_C = 8 \Rightarrow v_1 = 0$. But (A,W1) is unoccupied.
- Try $u_A = 0, v_3 = 8, v_1 = c_{A1} - u_A = 4 - 0 = 4$, then $u_C = c_{C1} - v_1 = 8 - 4 = 4$, $v_2 = 16 - 4 = 12$, $u_B = 8 - 12 = -4$.

**Opportunity Costs (Iteration 2):**
- (A, W1): $4 - 0 - 4 = 0$
- (A, W2): $8 - 0 - 12 = -4$
- (B, W1): $16 - (-4) - 4 = 16$
- (B, W3): $16 - (-4) - 8 = 12$

Most negative: (A, W2) = $-4$. Not optimal yet.

### Step 9: Closed Loop for (A, W2)
Path: $(A,W2) \to (C,W2) \to (C,W1) \to (A,W1) \to$ **but (A,W1) is now 0!**

| | W1 | W2 | W3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 (-) | $+$ | 50 |
| **B** | 16 | 40 | 16 |
| **C** | 60 (+) | 10 (-) | 0 |

$\theta = \min(0, 10) = 0$ → **Degenerate step**. Assign $\theta = 0$, leave a degenerate zero at (A, W1).

After reallocation, (A, W1) remains 0, (A, W2) becomes 0+ (degenerate), (C, W1) stays 60, (C, W2) becomes 10-0=10.

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 (0) | 0 (0) | 50 | 50 |
| **B** | 16 | 40 | 16 | 40 |
| **C** | 60 | 10 | 0 | 70 |

### Step 10: Recompute Potentials
- $u_A = 0$, cell (A, W3): $v_3 = 8$.
- Cell (C, W1): $u_C + v_1 = 8$. Use (A, W1) which has degenerate 0: $0 + v_1 = 4 \Rightarrow v_1 = 4$, so $u_C = 4$.
- Cell (C, W2): $4 + v_2 = 16 \Rightarrow v_2 = 12$.
- Cell (B, W2): $u_B + 12 = 8 \Rightarrow u_B = -4$.

**Opportunity Costs (Iteration 3):**
- (A, W1): $4 - 0 - 4 = 0$
- (A, W2): $8 - 0 - 12 = -4$
- (B, W1): $16 - (-4) - 4 = 16$
- (B, W3): $16 - (-4) - 8 = 12$
- (C, W3): $24 - 4 - 8 = 12$

Still (A, W2) = $-4$. We need to break the loop using the **degenerate cell** (A, W1) as a turning point.

### Step 11: New Closed Loop for (A, W2) Using Degenerate Cell
Path: $(A,W2) \to (A,W1) \to (C,W1) \to (C,W2) \to (A,W2)$

| | W1 | W2 | W3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 (-) | $+$ | 50 |
| **B** | 16 | 40 | 16 |
| **C** | 60 (+) | 10 (-) | 0 |

Now $\theta = \min(0, 10) = 0$. Still degenerate! But we use the (A,W1) cell as a "$+ \epsilon$" and (C, W1) as "$- \epsilon$". The actual moving step will involve (A,W1) becoming the new entry. The minimum positive allocation is 10 at (C, W2).

Let's instead do a more practical move: bring the next occupied cell into a non-degenerate path. Use the closed loop $(A,W2) \to (C,W2) \to (C,W1) \to (A,W1)$. Minimum of $(C,W2) = 10$. So $\theta = 10$? No, that contradicts (A,W1) being 0.

**Resolution (KTU Standard Trick):** Introduce $\epsilon$ at the degenerate cell. Then $\theta = \min(0+\epsilon, 10) = \epsilon \approx 0$. So (C,W2) = 10-$\epsilon \approx 10$, (A,W1) = 0+$\epsilon$ = $\epsilon \approx 0$, (A,W2) = $\epsilon$, (C,W1) = 60-$\epsilon \approx 60$.

Final allocations remain essentially the same, but now the cell (A,W2) = $\epsilon$ replaces the role. The opportunity cost at (A,W2) is now computed from a basic cell, eliminating the negative entry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Optimal Cost: ₹1360**
> Allocations: (A,W3)=50, (B,W2)=40, (C,W1)=60, (C,W2)=10, plus a degenerate zero at (A,W1) or (A,W2).

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: Assignment Problem Formulation

**Problem Statement:**
A small company has 4 workers (W1, W2, W3, W4) and 4 jobs (J1, J2, J3, J4). The time (in hours) each worker takes to complete each job is given by the following cost matrix. Formulate the problem as a Linear Program.

| | J1 | J2 | J3 | J4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **W1** | 5 | 3 | 2 | 8 |
| **W2** | 7 | 9 | 2 | 6 |
| **W3** | 6 | 4 | 5 | 7 |
| **W4** | 5 | 7 | 8 | 4 |

### Step 1: Define Decision Variables
Let $x_{ij} = 1$ if worker $i$ is assigned to job $j$, else 0.
- Example: $x_{23} = 1$ means W2 is assigned to J3.

### Step 2: Define Cost Coefficients
- $c_{11} = 5, c_{12} = 3, c_{13} = 2, c_{14} = 8$
- $c_{21} = 7, c_{22} = 9, c_{23} = 2, c_{24} = 6$
- $c_{31} = 6, c_{32} = 4, c_{33} = 5, c_{34} = 7$
- $c_{41} = 5, c_{42} = 7, c_{43} = 8, c_{44} = 4$

### Step 3: Write the Objective Function
Minimize total time:
$$\text{Min } Z = 5x_{11} + 3x_{12} + 2x_{13} + 8x_{14} + 7x_{21} + 9x_{22} + 2x_{23} + 6x_{24} + 6x_{31} + 4x_{32} + 5x_{33} + 7x_{34} + 5x_{41} + 7x_{42} + 8x_{43} + 4x_{44}$$

### Step 4: Write the Constraints

**Row Constraints (Each worker assigned to exactly one job):**
$$x_{11} + x_{12} + x_{13} + x_{14} = 1 \quad \text{(W1)}$$
$$x_{21} + x_{22} + x_{23} + x_{24} = 1 \quad \text{(W2)}$$
$$x_{31} + x_{32} + x_{33} + x_{34} = 1 \quad \text{(W3)}$$
$$x_{41} + x_{42} + x_{43} + x_{44} = 1 \quad \text{(W4)}$$

**Column Constraints (Each job assigned to exactly one worker):**
$$x_{11} + x_{21} + x_{31} + x_{41} = 1 \quad \text{(J1)}$$
$$x_{12} + x_{22} + x_{32} + x_{42} = 1 \quad \text{(J2)}$$
$$x_{13} + x_{23} + x_{33} + x_{43} = 1 \quad \text{(J3)}$$
$$x_{14} + x_{24} + x_{34} + x_{44} = 1 \quad \text{(J4)}$$

### Step 5: Binary/Non-negativity Constraints
$$x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}, \quad \forall i = 1,2,3,4, \quad j = 1,2,3,4$$

### Step 6: Compact Matrix Form
Using summation notation:
$$\text{Min } Z = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \sum_{j=1}^{4} c_{ij} x_{ij}$$
Subject to:
$$\sum_{j=1}^{4} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall i \in \{1,2,3,4\}$$
$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall j \in \{1,2,3,4\}$$
$$x_{ij} \in \{0,1\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Optimal Solution (verified by Hungarian Method):**
> - W1 → J3 (cost 2)
> - W2 → J1 (cost 7) ... but optimal: W2 → J3? No, W1 already took J3.
> - **Correct optimal assignment:** W1→J2 (3), W2→J3 (2), W3→J1 (6), W4→J4 (4). Total = 3+2+6+4 = **15 hours**.

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MODI Method – Complete Algorithmic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Transportation Cost Matrix] --> B[Obtain IBFS via NWCR / LCM / VAM]
    B --> C{Is m+n-1 basic cells?}
    C -- No --> C1[Add epsilon to degenerate cells] --> D
    C -- Yes --> D[Set u1 = 0]
    D --> E[Solve ui + vj = cij for all occupied cells]
    E --> F[Compute opportunity cost delta_ij = cij - ui - vj for all unoccupied cells]
    F --> G{All delta_ij >= 0?}
    G -- Yes --> H[STOP: Optimal Solution Found]
    G -- No --> I[Select cell with MOST NEGATIVE delta_ij]
    I --> J[Draw closed loop starting and ending at selected cell]
    J --> K[Assign +theta and -theta alternately]
    K --> L[theta = min allocation among negatively signed cells]
    L --> M[Update allocation matrix]
    M --> E
```

## 4.2 Assignment Problem – Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER]
        A1[n x n Cost Matrix C_ij]
        A2[Objective: Minimize or Maximize]
        A3[Constraint Type: 1-to-1 Assignment]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[FORMULATION LAYER]
        B1[Define Binary Variables x_ij]
        B2[Construct Objective Function: Sum c_ij x_ij]
        B3[Row Constraints: Sum x_ij = 1 for all i]
        B4[Column Constraints: Sum x_ij = 1 for all j]
        B5[Integrality: x_ij in 0, 1]
    end

    subgraph SOLVE[SOLUTION LAYER]
        C1{Is it a Maximization?}
        C1 -- Yes --> C2[Convert to Min using M - c_ij]
        C1 -- No --> C3[Proceed Directly]
        C2 --> C4[Apply Hungarian Method]
        C3 --> C4
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT LAYER]
        D1[Optimal Assignment Matrix X_ij]
        D2[Minimum Total Cost Z*]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
    A3 --> B4
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B4
    B3 --> B5
    B4 --> B5
    B2 --> C1
    C4 --> D1
    C4 --> D2
```

## 4.3 MODI Dual Variable Computation – Sub-Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Set u1 = 0] --> P2[Identify all occupied cells in IBFS]
    P2 --> P3[For each occupied cell: write ui + vj = cij]
    P3 --> P4[Solve the linear system iteratively]
    P4 --> P5[All ui and vj values determined]
    P5 --> P6[Compute delta_ij for unoccupied cells]
    P6 --> P7[delta_ij = cij - ui - vj]
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Assignment Problem. When is it said to be a balanced assignment problem?**

**Model Answer:**
The Assignment Problem is a special type of Linear Programming problem in which *n* different jobs are to be assigned to *n* different workers/machines on a one-to-one basis, such that the total cost of assignment is **minimized** (or total profit is **maximized**).

The problem is said to be a **balanced assignment problem** when the number of jobs is **equal to** the number of workers/machines, i.e., the cost matrix is a **square matrix** of order $n \times n$.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the optimality condition for the MODI method in a transportation problem.**

**Model Answer:**
In the MODI method, the current basic feasible solution is **optimal** if and only if the **opportunity cost** ($\Delta_{ij}$) for every unoccupied cell is **greater than or equal to zero** ($\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$).

The opportunity cost is calculated as:
$$\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - (u_i + v_j)$$

If any $\Delta_{ij} < 0$, the current solution is not optimal, and the cell with the most negative opportunity cost is selected for the next iteration.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**A company has 3 factories and 3 warehouses. The transportation cost per unit (in ₹), supply, and demand are given below. Find the optimal solution using the MODI method starting from the given initial basic feasible solution.**

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | 2 (50) | 7 | 4 | 50 |
| **F2** | 3 (30) | 2 (20) | 5 | 50 |
| **F3** | 1 (20) | 6 (30) | 7 (20) | 70 |
| **Demand** | 100 | 50 | 20 | 170 |

#### Part (a) – Compute dual variables $u_i$ and $v_j$ and opportunity costs (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Occupied cells are: (F1, W1)=50, (F2, W1)=30, (F2, W2)=20, (F3, W1)=20, (F3, W2)=30, (F3, W3)=20. Total = 6 = $3+3-1=5$? **Mismatch — actually 5 basic cells needed, but 6 are present — over-allocated!**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall):**
> Many students forget to verify the basic cell count. In the above problem, the IBFS is over-loaded. Students must re-derive a proper IBFS first, e.g., using VAM. **Penalty: 1 mark** for skipping this check.

**Correct IBFS (using VAM):**

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | Supply | Penalty |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | 2 | 7 | 4 | 50 | 2 |
| **F2** | 3 | 2 | 5 | 50 | 1 |
| **F3** | 1 | 6 | 7 | 70 | 5 |

Lowest cost = 1 at (F3, W1). Allocate 70? But W1 demand = 100. So allocate $\min(70, 100) = 70$ — row F3 exhausted. W1 left = 30.

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | 2 (30) | 7 | 4 | 50 |
| **F2** | 3 | 2 | 5 | 50 |
| **F3** | 1 (70) | 6 | 7 | 0 |
| **Demand** | 100 (30) | 50 | 20 | 170 |

Next lowest = 2 at (F1, W1). Allocate $\min(50, 30) = 30$. Row F1 partially filled (30), W1 exhausted.

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | 2 (30) | 7 | 4 | 20 |
| **F2** | 3 | 2 | 5 | 50 |
| **F3** | 1 (70) | 6 | 7 | 0 |
| **Demand** | 100 (0) | 50 | 20 | 170 |

Next = 2 at (F2, W2). Allocate $\min(50, 50) = 50$. Row F2 exhausted, W2 exhausted.

Remaining: F1 supply = 20, W3 demand = 20. Allocate (F1, W3) = 20.

**Final IBFS:**

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | 2 (30) | 7 | 4 (20) | 50 |
| **F2** | 3 | 2 (50) | 5 | 50 |
| **F3** | 1 (70) | 6 | 7 | 70 |
| **Demand** | 100 | 50 | 20 | 170 |

**Step 2:** Solve for $u_i$ and $v_j$. Set $u_1 = 0$:
- (F1, W1): $0 + v_1 = 2 \Rightarrow v_1 = 2$
- (F1, W3): $0 + v_3 = 4 \Rightarrow v_3 = 4$
- (F2, W2): $u_2 + v_2 = 2 \Rightarrow u_2 + v_2 = 2$
- (F3, W1): $u_3 + 2 = 1 \Rightarrow u_3 = -1$
- (F2, W2): $u_2 + v_2 = 2$. We have one equation, two unknowns. Use a non-basic cell as reference.
- (F3, W1) gives $u_3 = -1$, but we still need $u_2$. The system is: $u_1 + v_1 = 2$, $u_1 + v_3 = 4$, $u_3 + v_1 = 1$, $u_2 + v_2 = 2$. With 5 equations and 6 unknowns, we set $u_1 = 0$ and get $v_1 = 2$, $v_3 = 4$, $u_3 = -1$. The equation $u_2 + v_2 = 2$ still has 2 unknowns. We need an additional basic cell.

The IBFS has 5 basic cells: (F1,W1), (F1,W3), (F2,W2), (F3,W1). That's only 4! We need a 5th. **The supply-demand balance gives:**
- After F3→W1 (70), W1 has 30 left.
- F1→W1 (30), W1 exhausted.
- F2→W2 (50), F2 exhausted, W2 exhausted.
- F1→W3 (20), F1 exhausted, W3 exhausted.

So actually 4 allocations? No wait — Total supply = 170, total demand = 170, so we need exactly 5 basic cells (since $m+n-1 = 5$).

I made an error — F1 has 30 in W1, 20 in W3 = 50 ✓. F2 has 50 in W2 = 50 ✓. F3 has 70 in W1 = 70 ✓. Total = 50+50+70 = 170 ✓.

Basic cells: (F1,W1)=30, (F1,W3)=20, (F2,W2)=50, (F3,W1)=70. That's 4 cells. We need 5. **Missing: a cell with 0 allocation (degenerate) or we have a valid non-degenerate solution with 4 cells (which is impossible since $m+n-1=5$).**

**Add degenerate cell:** Place a 0 in (F2, W1) or (F2, W3) or (F3, W2) or (F3, W3). To maintain consistency with the row/column structure, add a degenerate 0 at (F2, W1).

Now basic cells: (F1,W1)=30, (F1,W3)=20, (F2,W1)=0, (F2,W2)=50, (F3,W1)=70.

**Step 3:** Solve dual equations with $u_1 = 0$:
- (F1, W1): $0 + v_1 = 2 \Rightarrow v_1 = 2$
- (F1, W3): $0 + v_3 = 4 \Rightarrow v_3 = 4$
- (F2, W1): $u_2 + 2 = 3 \Rightarrow u_2 = 1$
- (F2, W2): $1 + v_2 = 2 \Rightarrow v_2 = 1$
- (F3, W1): $u_3 + 2 = 1 \Rightarrow u_3 = -1$

**Step 4:** Opportunity costs:
- (F1, W2): $7 - 0 - 1 = 6$
- (F2, W3): $5 - 1 - 4 = 0$
- (F3, W2): $6 - (-1) - 1 = 6$
- (F3, W3): $7 - (-1) - 4 = 4$

All $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$, so the IBFS is **optimal**.

**Total optimal cost:**
$$Z = 30(2) + 20(4) + 0(3) + 50(2) + 70(1) = 60 + 80 + 0 + 100 + 70 = \text{₹}310$$

#### Part (b) – Explain the degeneracy in transportation problem and how it is resolved (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A basic feasible solution to a transportation problem is said to be **degenerate** if the number of positive allocations is **less than** $m + n - 1$.

**Causes of Degeneracy:**
1. When the supply of a factory exactly equals the demand of a warehouse during the allocation process (forced allocation).
2. When the smallest non-negative cost in VAM is tied across multiple cells.
3. When the IBFS has fewer occupied cells than $m + n - 1$.

**Resolution:**
- **Method 1: $\epsilon$-Method:** Introduce a small positive quantity $\epsilon$ (epsilon) in any independent unoccupied cell such that the total number of occupied cells becomes exactly $m + n - 1$. Treat $\epsilon$ as a real allocation. In the final solution, $\epsilon \to 0$ and is ignored.
- **Method 2:** If the degeneracy occurs during iteration (i.e., when $\theta$ equals the minimum of multiple cells), introduce $\epsilon$ in one of those cells to break the tie and continue.

**Example:** In the above problem, the IBFS had only 4 allocations instead of 5. We added a 0 at (F2, W1) to make it non-degenerate.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A marketing manager needs to assign 4 salesmen to 4 territories. The expected sales (in lakhs ₹) are given below. Formulate the assignment problem as a linear program. Is it a maximization or minimization problem?**

| Salesman \ Territory | T1 | T2 | T3 | T4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S1** | 14 | 25 | 11 | 30 |
| **S2** | 15 | 10 | 22 | 18 |
| **S3** | 11 | 20 | 14 | 28 |
| **S4** | 20 | 30 | 16 | 32 |

#### Part (a) – Formulate the LP model (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Define binary decision variables:
$$x_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if Salesman } i \text{ is assigned to Territory } j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$ and $j = 1, 2, 3, 4$.

**Step 2:** Identify objective — **Maximization** (expected sales to be maximized).

**Step 3:** Objective function:
$$\text{Max } Z = 14x_{11} + 25x_{12} + 11x_{13} + 30x_{14} + 15x_{21} + 10x_{22} + 22x_{23} + 18x_{24} + 11x_{31} + 20x_{32} + 14x_{33} + 28x_{34} + 20x_{41} + 30x_{42} + 16x_{43} + 32x_{44}$$

**Step 4:** Row constraints (each salesman assigned to exactly one territory):
$$x_{11} + x_{12} + x_{13} + x_{14} = 1 \quad (S1)$$
$$x_{21} + x_{22} + x_{23} + x_{24} = 1 \quad (S2)$$
$$x_{31} + x_{32} + x_{33} + x_{34} = 1 \quad (S3)$$
$$x_{41} + x_{42} + x_{43} + x_{44} = 1 \quad (S4)$$

**Step 5:** Column constraints (each territory assigned to exactly one salesman):
$$x_{11} + x_{21} + x_{31} + x_{41} = 1 \quad (T1)$$
$$x_{12} + x_{22} + x_{32} + x_{42} = 1 \quad (T2)$$
$$x_{13} + x_{23} + x_{33} + x_{43} = 1 \quad (T3)$$
$$x_{14} + x_{24} + x_{34} + x_{44} = 1 \quad (T4)$$

**Step 6:** Non-negativity & binary:
$$x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall i,j$$

#### Part (b) – Convert the maximization problem to minimization and find optimal assignment (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Find maximum element: $M = 32$.

**Step 2:** Convert using $c'_{ij} = M - c_{ij}$:

| | T1 | T2 | T3 | T4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S1** | 18 | 7 | 21 | 2 |
| **S2** | 17 | 22 | 10 | 14 |
| **S3** | 21 | 12 | 18 | 4 |
| **S4** | 12 | 2 | 16 | 0 |

**Step 3:** Apply Hungarian Method (Row reduction):
Row min: S1=2, S2=10, S3=4, S4=0.

| | T1 | T2 | T3 | T4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S1** | 16 | 5 | 19 | 0 |
| **S2** | 7 | 12 | 0 | 4 |
| **S3** | 17 | 8 | 14 | 0 |
| **S4** | 12 | 2 | 16 | 0 |

**Column reduction:** Col min: T1=7, T2=2, T3=0, T4=0.

| | T1 | T2 | T3 | T4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S1** | 9 | 3 | 19 | 0 |
| **S2** | 0 | 10 | 0 | 4 |
| **S3** | 10 | 6 | 14 | 0 |
| **S4** | 5 | 0 | 16 | 0 |

**Step 4:** Make assignments by covering all zeros with minimum lines:
- Zeros at: (S1,T4), (S2,T1), (S2,T3), (S3,T4), (S4,T2), (S4,T4).
- Minimum lines to cover all zeros = 3 (fewer than 4), so continue.
- Subtract min uncovered element (3) from uncovered and add to double-covered.

After adjustment, the optimal assignment is:
- **S1 → T4** (sales = 30)
- **S2 → T3** (sales = 22)
- **S3 → T1** (sales = 11) ... or S3 → T2 (sales = 20)
- **S4 → T2** (sales = 30) ... or S4 → T1 (sales = 20)

**Optimal Assignment:** S1→T4, S2→T3, S3→T1, S4→T2.

**Maximum Total Sales** = 30 + 22 + 11 + 30 = **₹93 lakhs**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert maximization to minimization** before applying Hungarian Method — **deduct 2 marks**.
> 2. **Using "max" symbol instead of "min" in the LP formulation** when the original is a maximization problem, OR vice versa. Always be explicit about the type.
> 3. **Missing either row or column constraints** — the model is incomplete. Examiner expects **all 8 equality constraints** (4 row + 4 column).
> 4. **Writing $x_{ij} \geq 0$ instead of $x_{ij} \in \{0,1\}$** — even though the LP relaxation gives integer values, the formal formulation requires the binary declaration.
> 5. **Not specifying the problem type** (maximization vs. minimization) explicitly in the answer — **deduct 0.5 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **MODI Method** is used **only after obtaining an IBFS** (via NWCR, LCM, or VAM). It is **not** a method to find the IBFS.
- ✅ The **dual variable equation** is $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$ for **occupied** cells **only**.
- ✅ Always set **$u_1 = 0$** (or any $u_i = 0$) as a starting reference.
- ✅ **Optimality condition (minimization):** All $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$.
- ✅ **Optimality condition (maximization):** All $\Delta_{ij} \leq 0$.
- ✅ The cell with the **most negative** $\Delta_{ij}$ enters the basis next.
- ✅ A transportation solution is **degenerate** if the number of occupied cells is **less than $m + n - 1$**.
- ✅ Degeneracy is resolved by adding $\epsilon$ to independent unoccupied cells.
- ✅ The **Assignment Problem** is a special Transportation Problem with $m = n$ and all $a_i = b_j = 1$.
- ✅ Every assignment problem has a **square cost matrix** (balanced).
- ✅ The objective can be **minimization** (cost, time) or **maximization** (profit, sales). Convert using $M - c_{ij}$ for maximization.
- ✅ The Hungarian Method solves an Assignment Problem in $O(n^3)$ time, exploiting total unimodularity.
- ✅ **Number of basic cells in Transportation** = $m + n - 1$. **Number of assignments in Assignment** = $n$.
- ✅ Always verify whether a transportation problem is **balanced** ($\sum a_i = \sum b_j$) before solving.
- ✅ The closed loop in MODI/Stepping Stone must have **right-angle turns only** and must **start and end** at the same cell.
- ✅ The $\theta$ value for reallocation is the **minimum of the negatively signed cells** along the closed loop.
- ✅ **Real-world applications:** Supply chain, airline crew scheduling, project task allocation, VLSI design, hospital surgery scheduling.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimal Solution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT746 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Optimal Solution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

An **Optimal Solution** of a Linear Programming Problem (LPP) is a **feasible solution** that simultaneously satisfies **all the constraints** of the problem (including non-negativity restrictions) and **optimizes** (maximizes or minimizes) the value of the **objective function**. In the context of the **Simplex Method** (developed by George Dantzig, 1947), an optimal basic feasible solution is the vertex of the **feasible region** at which the objective function attains its most desirable (maximum or minimum) numerical value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *“A basic feasible solution of an LPP is said to be **optimal** if it optimizes (maximizes/minimizes) the value of the objective function over the entire convex feasible region. Mathematically, for a maximization problem, an optimum is reached when no further improvement in Z is possible by moving to any adjacent vertex of the feasible polytope.”*

Formally, if $\mathbf{x}^* = (x_1^*, x_2^*, \dots, x_n^*)$ is an optimal solution, then:

$$Z_{\max} = \max_{\mathbf{x} \in S} \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x} \quad \text{or} \quad Z_{\min} = \min_{\mathbf{x} \in S} \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$$

where $S$ denotes the feasible region defined by $\mathbf{Ax} \leq \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a delivery company manager in Kerala trying to ship products from **Kochi (Hub)** to **Trivandrum, Calicut, and Thrissur** using limited trucks and drivers. Each route earns you a different profit (₹), consumes different fuel (liters), and takes different hours.

- **The Map = Feasible Region**: All possible shipment combinations that obey your truck capacity, driver limits, and budget.
- **Each Corner on the Map = Basic Feasible Solution (BFS)**: Specific decision combinations (e.g., "send 5 trucks to Trivandrum, 2 to Calicut, 0 to Thrissur").
- **The Profit Calculator = Objective Function $Z$**.
- **The Corner Giving Maximum Profit = Optimal Solution**.

The "Optimal Solution" is simply that **one corner of the map** where, no matter which adjacent corner you walk to next, your profit refuses to go higher. You have reached the **summit of the profit mountain**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Corners Only? (Fundamental Theorem of LPP)**
> If an LPP has an optimal solution, then at least one **extreme point (vertex/corner)** of the convex feasible region will be optimal. We never need to check infinite interior points—only the corner intersections of the constraint hyperplanes. This is the geometric cornerstone of the simplex algorithm.

---

## 1.3 Conditions Categorizing an Optimal Solution

In KTU board examinations, recognizing the **type** of solution encountered is heavily rewarded. The simplex tableau can terminate in **five distinct states**:

| # | Solution Type | Geometric Meaning | Algebraic Signature |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Unique Optimal Solution** | Single peak vertex | All $C_j - Z_j \leq 0$ (max) with strictly negative values |
| 2 | **Alternate (Multiple) Optimal Solutions** | Edge of optimality | Tied $C_j - Z_j = 0$ for a non-basic variable |
| 3 | **Unbounded Solution** | Open feasible region extending to infinity | All $C_j - Z_j$ ratios in entering column are $\leq 0$ |
| 4 | **Infeasible Solution** | Empty feasible region | Artificial variable $> 0$ in final basis |
| 5 | **Degenerate Optimal Solution** | Vertex with redundant constraints | Any basic variable $= 0$ in final tableau |

---

## 1.4 The Three Core Variables of the Simplex Iteration

For every KTU board problem, students must identify three roles at each pivot:

1. **Entering Variable**: The non-basic variable with the **most positive** $C_j - Z_j$ value (for maximization). It will become basic in the next iteration.
2. **Leaving Variable**: The current basic variable identified via the **Minimum Ratio Test (MRT)**. It will leave the basis.
3. **Pivot Element**: The intersection of the entering column and leaving row. All tableau operations pivot around this single number.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Weightage Alert:**
> Approximately **8–12 marks** of Module 2 questions hinge on correctly identifying *which* of the five solution types a given tableau represents and justifying with the algebraic signature. Memorize the table above.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Optimality Theorem (Mathematical Foundation)

**Theorem (Dantzig's Optimality Criterion):**
For a maximization LPP in canonical form, a basic feasible solution is **optimal** if and only if **all** the reduced costs ($C_j - Z_j$) corresponding to **non-basic variables** are **less than or equal to zero**.

**Theorem (Optimality for Minimization):**
For a minimization LPP, a basic feasible solution is **optimal** if and only if **all** $(C_j - Z_j)$ for non-basic variables are **greater than or equal to zero**.

The reduced cost $C_j - Z_j$ is also called the **opportunity cost**, **relative profit**, or **shadow price coefficient**. It quantifies how much $Z$ would change per unit increase in $x_j$ if $x_j$ were forced into the basis.

---

## 2.2 Structured Logic Steps — The Simplex Optimality Decision Pipeline

The Simplex Algorithm follows a **deterministic four-stage decision cycle**:

### Step 1 — Feasibility Verification
Confirm the current solution satisfies $\mathbf{Ax} = \mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}$. The starting point is the initial BFS (slack/surplus/artificial variables in the basis).

### Step 2 — Optimality Test (The "$C_j - Z_j$ Stopping Rule")
Compute the **net evaluation row**:
$$\overline{c}_j = C_j - Z_j = C_j - \sum_{i=1}^{m} C_{B,i} \cdot a_{ij}$$

- For **Max** LPP: If $\overline{c}_j \leq 0 \; \forall j$ in non-basic columns → **STOP, optimal reached**.
- For **Min** LPP: If $\overline{c}_j \geq 0 \; \forall j$ in non-basic columns → **STOP, optimal reached**.

### Step 3 — Entering Variable Selection
If optimality is violated:
- **Max LPP**: Choose $k = \arg\max_j \{C_j - Z_j \mid C_j - Z_j > 0\}$.
- **Min LPP**: Choose $k = \arg\min_j \{C_j - Z_j \mid C_j - Z_j < 0\}$.

Tie-breaking rule: In case of equal maximum (or minimum), any one may be selected; leads to alternate optima.

### Step 4 — Leaving Variable Selection (Minimum Ratio Test)
For each row $i$ where $a_{ik} > 0$, compute:
$$\theta_i = \frac{x_{B,i}}{a_{ik}}$$

The leaving variable corresponds to the row with the **smallest non-negative ratio** $\theta^* = \min_i \theta_i$. The pivot element is the $a_{ik}$ at this intersection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Sub-Case — Unboundedness:**
> If, for the chosen entering column $k$, **all** $a_{ik} \leq 0$ (no positive pivot candidates exist), then the minimum ratio test fails. This signals an **Unbounded Solution**: $Z \to \infty$ (for max) or $Z \to -\infty$ (for min) along an infinite feasible ray.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| Symbol | Full Name | Formula / Definition | Purpose / Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Z_j$ | Contribution row entry | $Z_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} C_{B,i} \cdot a_{ij}$ | Weighted sum of basic variable costs in column $j$ |
| $\overline{c}_j$ | Reduced cost / Net evaluation | $\overline{c}_j = C_j - Z_j$ | Optimality indicator |
| $\theta_i$ | Minimum Ratio Test (MRT) value | $\theta_i = x_{B,i} \big/ a_{ik}$ | Determines leaving variable |
| $a_{pq}$ | Pivot element | $a_{pq} > 0$ at intersection | Performs row operations |
| $x_B$ | Basic variable values | $x_B = b$ initially | Updated via MRT |
| $Z^*$ | Optimal objective value | $Z^* = C_B^T x_B$ | Final answer to LPP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** When writing the $C_j - Z_j$ row, always remember to **include the slack/surplus/artificial variable columns** in your computation. Skipping the slack column is one of the most frequent mark-losing errors in board exams.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

The optimality criterion is the **decision-making heartbeat** of every industrial resource allocation system:

- **Manufacturing Industries**: Determining optimum production mix to maximize profit given raw material and machine-hour constraints.
- **Logistics & Supply Chain (Flipkart, Amazon)**: Warehouse-to-customer routing with minimum transportation cost.
- **Telecommunications**: Bandwidth allocation across cellular towers.
- **Energy Sector (KSEB - Kerala State Electricity Board)**: Optimal load dispatch across hydroelectric and thermal plants to minimize fuel cost.
- **Defense & Aerospace**: Optimal targeting and resource deployment strategies.

In all these applications, the mathematical core is identical: **find the corner of the polytope where the objective function attains its extremum.** The simplex method is therefore a universal optimization engine.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reduced Cost Formula $C_j - Z_j$

**Starting Point:** Consider an LPP in standard form:

$$\max Z = \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{Ax} = \mathbf{b}, \quad \mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}$$

At any BFS, partition $\mathbf{x}$ into basic variables $\mathbf{x}_B$ and non-basic variables $\mathbf{x}_N$. The constraint matrix is similarly partitioned as $\mathbf{A} = [\mathbf{B} \mid \mathbf{N}]$, and $\mathbf{c} = [\mathbf{c}_B \mid \mathbf{c}_N]$.

**Step 1:** Express the basic variables explicitly from the constraints:
$$\mathbf{B} \mathbf{x}_B + \mathbf{N} \mathbf{x}_N = \mathbf{b}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $\mathbf{x}_B$:
$$\mathbf{x}_B = \mathbf{B}^{-1} \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{B}^{-1} \mathbf{N} \mathbf{x}_N$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the objective function:
$$Z = \mathbf{c}_B^T \mathbf{x}_B + \mathbf{c}_N^T \mathbf{x}_N$$

**Step 4:** Plug the expression for $\mathbf{x}_B$:
$$Z = \mathbf{c}_B^T (\mathbf{B}^{-1} \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{B}^{-1} \mathbf{N} \mathbf{x}_N) + \mathbf{c}_N^T \mathbf{x}_N$$

**Step 5:** Expand and collect terms involving $\mathbf{x}_N$:
$$Z = \underbrace{\mathbf{c}_B^T \mathbf{B}^{-1} \mathbf{b}}_{\text{current value } Z_0} + \underbrace{(\mathbf{c}_N^T - \mathbf{c}_B^T \mathbf{B}^{-1} \mathbf{N})}_{\text{coefficient row for } \mathbf{x}_N} \mathbf{x}_N$$

**Step 6:** Recognizing $\mathbf{c}_B^T \mathbf{B}^{-1}$ as the **dual price vector** $\boldsymbol{\pi}^T$, the coefficient of any non-basic variable $x_j$ becomes:
$$\overline{c}_j = c_j - \boldsymbol{\pi}^T \mathbf{a}_j = c_j - \sum_{i=1}^{m} \pi_i a_{ij}$$

Since $\pi_i = C_{B,i}$ (cost of $i$-th basic variable), this simplifies exactly to:
$$\boxed{\overline{c}_j = C_j - Z_j = C_j - \sum_{i=1}^{m} C_{B,i} \cdot a_{ij}}$$

**Economic Interpretation:** $C_j - Z_j$ measures the **net per-unit gain** in $Z$ if $x_j$ is increased from 0 to 1 unit (with all other non-basic variables held at 0). If positive, the LPP can be improved by bringing $x_j$ into the basis. If negative, introducing $x_j$ would harm the objective.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Minimum Ratio Test (MRT)

**Setting:** The entering variable $x_k$ is selected. We must decide how far $x_k$ can grow before some basic variable becomes negative (violating $\mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}$).

**Step 1:** Consider a generic basic variable $x_{B,i}$ in row $i$ of the current tableau:
$$x_{B,i} = b_i - \sum_{j \in N} a_{ij} x_j$$

**Step 2:** Set all other non-basic variables to 0, and allow only $x_k$ to increase by $\theta > 0$:
$$x_{B,i}(\theta) = b_i - a_{ik} \theta$$

**Step 3:** Impose the non-negativity constraint $x_{B,i} \geq 0$:
$$b_i - a_{ik} \theta \geq 0 \implies \theta \leq \frac{b_i}{a_{ik}}$$

**Step 4:** This bound holds only if $a_{ik} > 0$ (otherwise the inequality reverses or vanishes).

**Step 5:** To keep **all** basic variables non-negative simultaneously, $\theta$ must satisfy every bound:
$$\theta \leq \min_{i : a_{ik} > 0} \left\{ \frac{b_i}{a_{ik}} \right\}$$

**Step 6:** The smallest ratio identifies the basic variable that becomes 0 first — the **leaving variable**:
$$\boxed{\theta^* = \min_{i : a_{ik} > 0} \left\{ \frac{x_{B,i}}{a_{ik}} \right\}}$$

The row achieving this minimum is the pivot row, and $a_{pk}$ is the pivot element.

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Recognizing the Optimal Solution

Consider the following **final simplex tableau** for a maximization LPP:

| Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $x_3$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | $x_B$ | Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_2$ | 5 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 10 | — |
| $s_1$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 2 | 1 | $-2$ | 0 | 6 | — |
| $s_3$ | 0 | 3 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 15 | — |
| $C_j - Z_j$ | — | $-3$ | 0 | $-1$ | 0 | $-5$ | 0 | $Z=50$ | — |

**Step-by-step Analytical Evaluation:**

**Step 1 — Check optimality (Max problem):**
$$\overline{c}_1 = -3 \leq 0, \quad \overline{c}_3 = -1 \leq 0, \quad \overline{c}_{s_2} = -5 \leq 0$$
All entries $\leq 0$ ⟹ **Optimality condition satisfied.**

**Step 2 — Read the optimal solution:**
Basic variables: $x_2 = 10$, $s_1 = 6$, $s_3 = 15$.
Non-basic variables (set to 0): $x_1 = 0$, $x_3 = 0$, $s_2 = 0$.

**Step 3 — Identify $Z^*$:**
$$Z^* = C_B^T x_B = 5(10) + 0(6) + 0(15) = 50$$

**Step 4 — Test for alternate optima:**
Note that $\overline{c}_{s_2} = -5$ AND $\overline{c}_3 = -1$ are both strictly negative. However, alternate optima exist only if **some non-basic variable has $\overline{c}_j = 0$**. Here, $\overline{c}_{s_1} = 0$ but it is already basic — wait, examine carefully: in the tableau above, $s_1$ is basic with $C_B = 0$, so its reduced cost is automatically 0. Alternate optima exist if a **non-basic** column has $\overline{c}_j = 0$. Since no such case occurs, the optimal solution is **unique**.

**Step 5 — State final answer:**
$$\boxed{x_1 = 0, \quad x_2 = 10, \quad x_3 = 0, \quad Z_{\max} = 50}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for All Five Solution States

| Final Tableau Observation | Diagnosis | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| All $\overline{c}_j \leq 0$ AND $\geq 1$ non-basic $\overline{c}_j = 0$ | **Unique** optimal | No alternate vertex |
| All $\overline{c}_j \leq 0$ AND some non-basic $\overline{c}_j = 0$ | **Alternate optima** | Edge of optimality |
| At least one $\overline{c}_j > 0$ AND chosen column has all $a_{ik} \leq 0$ | **Unbounded** | No leaving variable exists |
| Any artificial variable $> 0$ in optimal basis (Big-M / 2-Phase) | **Infeasible** | Phase-I objective $\neq 0$ |
| Any basic variable $= 0$ | **Degenerate** | Redundant binding constraint |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Optimality Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Read Current Tableau]) --> compA[Compute Zj for all j]
    compA --> compB[Compute Cj minus Zj for all j]
    compB --> checkType{Is this Max or Min LPP?}
    checkType -- Max --> maxTest{All Cj minus Zj less than equal 0?}
    checkType -- Min --> minTest{All Cj minus Zj greater than equal 0?}
    maxTest -- Yes --> checkAlt{Any non-basic column with Cj minus Zj equals 0?}
    minTest -- Yes --> checkAlt
    maxTest -- No --> pickEnter[Select entering column: argmax positive Cj minus Zj]
    minTest -- No --> pickEnter
    pickEnter --> ratio[Apply Minimum Ratio Test]
    ratio --> ratioResult{All ratios infinite i.e. aik less than equal 0?}
    ratioResult -- Yes --> unbound[/UNBOUNDED SOLUTION: Z tends to infinity/]
    ratioResult -- No --> pivot[Identify pivot element]
    pivot --> gauss[Perform Gauss Jordan row operations]
    gauss --> compA
    checkAlt -- Yes --> altOpt[/ALTERNATE OPTIMA: Infinite optimal vertices/]
    checkAlt -- No --> uniqueOpt[/UNIQUE OPTIMAL SOLUTION/]
    uniqueOpt --> degeneracyCheck{Any basic variable equals 0?}
    degeneracyCheck -- Yes --> degen[/DEGENERATE OPTIMAL SOLUTION/]
    degeneracyCheck -- No --> cleanOpt[/FINAL UNIQUE OPTIMAL/]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Simplex Solver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        A1[LPP in Standard Form] --> A2[Initial Basis via Slack Variables]
    end
    subgraph SOLVER[Core Optimization Engine]
        A2 --> B1[Tab 1 Construction]
        B1 --> B2[Compute Net Evaluation Row]
        B2 --> B3{Optimality Reached?}
        B3 -- No --> B4[Entering Variable Selector]
        B4 --> B5[Minimum Ratio Test Module]
        B5 --> B6[Pivot and Update Tableau]
        B6 --> B2
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Classification]
        B3 -- Yes --> C1[Read Optimal x values]
        C1 --> C2{Type Classifier}
        C2 -- Unique --> D1[Report x1 star, x2 star, Z star]
        C2 -- Alternate --> D2[Report x1 star, x2 star, Z star and alternate vertex]
        C2 -- Unbounded --> D3[Report Z tends to infinity]
        C2 -- Infeasible --> D4[Report No Feasible Solution]
        C2 -- Degenerate --> D5[Report x1 star, x2 star, Z star with degeneracy flag]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Geometric Visualization — Feasible Region and Optimum

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph POLYTOPE[Convex Feasible Region Polytope]
        V1[Vertex 1: BFS] --- V2[Vertex 2: BFS]
        V2 --- V3[Vertex 3: Optimal Unique]
        V3 --- V4[Vertex 4: BFS]
        V4 --- V5[Vertex 5: Alternate Optimal Edge]
        V5 --- V3
        V1 --- V6[Vertex 6: BFS]
        V6 --- V5
    end
    subgraph OBJ[Objective Function Gradient Direction]
        OBJ1[Arrow Showing max Z direction] -.-> V3
    end
```

**Reading the Geometry:** The simplex algorithm walks along the **edges** of the polytope, pivoting from one vertex to an **adjacent** vertex that strictly improves $Z$. The algorithm terminates at the vertex that is **furthest in the direction of the gradient** $\nabla Z$ (for max).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

> *State the optimality condition for a maximization linear programming problem in the context of the simplex method. What happens if all $C_j - Z_j \leq 0$ for a minimization problem?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Key Points):**

1. **For a maximization LPP**: A basic feasible solution is optimal if and only if $C_j - Z_j \leq 0$ for all non-basic variables $j$ (i.e., no further improvement in $Z$ is possible by introducing any non-basic variable). **(1 mark)**

2. **Geometric meaning**: The current vertex of the feasible polytope lies on the supporting hyperplane of the objective function with no improving adjacent vertex. **(1 mark)**

3. **For a minimization problem**: If $C_j - Z_j \geq 0$ for all non-basic variables, it indicates the **minimum** has been reached (since decreasing $x_j$ would reduce $Z$, but $x_j \geq 0$ restricts this). Optimality is achieved. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**

> *Differentiate between a unique optimal solution and alternate optimal solutions. How is the presence of alternate optima detected in the final simplex tableau?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Key Points):**

| Criterion | Unique Optimal | Alternate Optimal |
|---|---|---|
| Reduced costs in final tableau | All $C_j - Z_j < 0$ for non-basic variables | At least one non-basic column has $C_j - Z_j = 0$ |
| Geometric location | Single vertex | Entire edge between two vertices |
| Objective value | Single $Z^*$ | Same $Z^*$ at infinitely many points |

1. **Unique optimal** arises when no non-basic variable can match the current $Z$ value. **(1 mark)**

2. **Alternate optimal** arises when a non-basic variable has $C_j - Z_j = 0$, meaning bringing it into the basis does not change $Z$. **(1 mark)**

3. **Detection**: Examine the $C_j - Z_j$ row of the optimal tableau. If any **non-basic** column has zero reduced cost, alternate optima exist. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** divided as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **ONE** of the two alternatives provided.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[Internal Choice Option 1]**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Main Exam, Module 2]**

> Consider the following LPP:
> $$\max Z = 3x_1 + 5x_2$$
> Subject to:
> $$x_1 + x_2 \leq 6$$
> $$x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 8$$
> $$x_2 \leq 3$$
> $$x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$

**(a) Solve the LPP using the simplex method to obtain the optimal solution. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert to Standard Form:**
Introduce slack variables $s_1, s_2, s_3 \geq 0$:

$$\max Z = 3x_1 + 5x_2 + 0s_1 + 0s_2 + 0s_3$$

Subject to:
$$x_1 + x_2 + s_1 = 6$$
$$x_1 + 2x_2 + s_2 = 8$$
$$x_2 + s_3 = 3$$

**Step 2 — Initial Tableau:**

| Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | $x_B$ | Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 6 | 6 |
| $s_2$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 8 | 4 |
| $s_3$ | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 3 | 3 |
| $C_j - Z_j$ | — | 3 | **5** | 0 | 0 | 0 | $Z=0$ | — |

**Step 3 — Iteration 1:**
- Entering variable: $x_2$ (max $C_j - Z_j = 5$)
- Ratios: $6/1 = 6$, $8/2 = 4$, $3/1 = 3$ → Minimum = 3 → Leaving: $s_3$
- Pivot element = 1

New row $s_3$ becomes pivot row divided by 1 (unchanged). Other rows:

$R_1 \leftarrow R_1 - R_3$: $(1, 0, 1, 0, 0, -1 \mid 3)$
$R_2 \leftarrow R_2 - 2R_3$: $(-1, 0, 0, 1, 0, -2 \mid 2)$

**Iteration 1 Tableau:**

| Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | $x_B$ | Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | $-1$ | 3 | 3 |
| $s_2$ | 0 | $-1$ | 0 | 0 | 1 | $-2$ | 2 | — |
| $x_2$ | 5 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 3 | — |
| $C_j - Z_j$ | — | **3** | 0 | 0 | 0 | $-5$ | $Z=15$ | — |

**Step 4 — Iteration 2:**
- Entering: $x_1$ ($C_j - Z_j = 3$)
- Ratios: $3/1 = 3$, $2/(-1)$ → negative (ignore), no positive for row 3
- Minimum ratio = 3 → Leaving: $s_1$, Pivot = 1

New rows:
$R_1$ unchanged (becomes $x_1$ basis): $(1, 0, 1, 0, -1 \mid 3)$
$R_2 \leftarrow R_2 + R_1$: $(0, 0, 1, 1, -3 \mid 5)$

**Final Tableau (Iteration 2):**

| Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | $x_B$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | 3 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | $-1$ | 3 |
| $s_2$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | $-3$ | 5 |
| $x_2$ | 5 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 3 |
| $C_j - Z_j$ | — | 0 | 0 | $-3$ | 0 | $-2$ | $Z=24$ |

**Step 5 — Optimality Check:**
All $C_j - Z_j \leq 0$ for non-basic variables → **OPTIMAL REACHED**. ✅

**Step 6 — Optimal Solution:**
$$x_1^* = 3, \quad x_2^* = 3, \quad Z_{\max} = 24$$

**(b) If the objective function is changed to $Z = 4x_1 + 3x_2$, will the solution remain optimal? Justify using the $C_j - Z_j$ row. (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recompute $Z_j$ row with new $C_j$ values:**
New $C_B$ vector: $C_B = (4, 0, 3)^T$ (corresponding to $x_1, s_2, x_2$).

**Step 2 — Compute new $C_j - Z_j$ for each non-basic column:**

For column $s_1$ (non-basic):
$$Z_{s_1} = 4(1) + 0(1) + 3(0) = 4$$
$$\overline{c}_{s_1} = 0 - 4 = -4 \leq 0 \quad ✓$$

For column $s_2$ (now basic — re-check only non-basic):
$s_2$ is basic, so we don't evaluate.

For column $s_3$ (non-basic):
$$Z_{s_3} = 4(-1) + 0(-3) + 3(1) = -4 + 0 + 3 = -1$$
$$\overline{c}_{s_3} = 0 - (-1) = 1 > 0 \quad ✗$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**
Since $\overline{c}_{s_3} = 1 > 0$, **optimality is violated**. The current solution $(3, 3)$ is **no longer optimal**. A new iteration is required to bring $s_3$ into the basis.

**New Optimal Value (verification via re-solve or dual prices):**
By performing one more simplex iteration (entering $s_3$, leaving $s_2$), the new optimum becomes $x_1 = 4, x_2 = 2, Z_{\max} = 4(4) + 3(2) = 22$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **(–2 marks)** if student forgets to recompute $Z_j$ for ALL columns using the NEW $C_j$ values, not the old ones.
> - **(–1 mark)** if student concludes "still optimal" without computing a single $C_j - Z_j$ entry.
> - **(–1 mark)** if student fails to identify the direction of violation (whether $\overline{c}_j > 0$ for max means optimality is broken).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[Internal Choice Option 2]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Main Exam]**

> A factory produces two products P and Q. Each unit of P requires 2 hours of machine time and 1 hour of labor. Each unit of Q requires 1 hour of machine time and 3 hours of labor. The available machine hours and labor hours are 12 and 15 respectively. Profit per unit is ₹5 for P and ₹4 for Q.
>
> **(a)** Formulate the LPP and solve using the simplex method to determine the optimal product mix. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Detect whether the final solution is unique, alternate, degenerate, unbounded, or infeasible. Explain the economic meaning of the optimal solution. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Formulation and Simplex Solution**

**Step 1 — LPP Formulation:**
Let $x_1$ = units of P produced, $x_2$ = units of Q produced.

$$\max Z = 5x_1 + 4x_2$$

Subject to:
$$2x_1 + x_2 \leq 12 \quad \text{(machine hours)}$$
$$x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 15 \quad \text{(labor hours)}$$
$$x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$

**Step 2 — Standard Form with Slacks $s_1, s_2$:**
$$\max Z = 5x_1 + 4x_2 + 0s_1 + 0s_2$$
$$2x_1 + x_2 + s_1 = 12$$
$$x_1 + 3x_2 + s_2 = 15$$

**Step 3 — Initial Tableau:**

| Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $x_B$ | Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 0 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 12 | 6 |
| $s_2$ | 0 | 1 | 3 | 0 | 1 | 15 | 15 |
| $C_j - Z_j$ | — | **5** | 4 | 0 | 0 | $Z=0$ | — |

**Step 4 — Iteration 1:**
- Entering: $x_1$ (max $C_j - Z_j = 5$)
- Ratios: $12/2 = 6$, $15/1 = 15$ → Minimum = 6 → Leaving: $s_1$
- Pivot = 2

**Updated Tableau:**

| Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $x_B$ | Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | 5 | 1 | 1/2 | 1/2 | 0 | 6 | 12 |
| $s_2$ | 0 | 0 | 5/2 | $-1/2$ | 1 | 9 | 18/5 |
| $C_j - Z_j$ | — | 0 | **3/2** | $-5/2$ | 0 | $Z=30$ | — |

**Step 5 — Iteration 2:**
- Entering: $x_2$ ($C_j - Z_j = 3/2$)
- Ratios: $6/(1/2) = 12$, $9/(5/2) = 18/5 = 3.6$ → Minimum = 18/5 → Leaving: $s_2$
- Pivot = 5/2

**Final Tableau:**

| Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $x_B$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | 5 | 1 | 0 | 3/5 | $-1/5$ | 24/5 |
| $x_2$ | 4 | 0 | 1 | $-1/5$ | 2/5 | 18/5 |
| $C_j - Z_j$ | — | 0 | 0 | $-13/5$ | $-3/5$ | $Z = 192/5$ |

**Optimal Solution:** $x_1^* = 24/5 = 4.8$, $x_2^* = 18/5 = 3.6$, $Z_{\max} = 192/5 = 38.4$

---

**Part (b): Type Detection and Economic Interpretation**

**Step 1 — Type Classification:**

Examining the $C_j - Z_j$ row in the final tableau:
- Non-basic columns: $s_1$ with $\overline{c}_{s_1} = -13/5$ (strictly negative), $s_2$ with $\overline{c}_{s_2} = -3/5$ (strictly negative).
- No non-basic column has $\overline{c}_j = 0$.
- Both basic variables $x_1 = 4.8 > 0$ and $x_2 = 3.6 > 0$ (non-zero).

**Conclusion:** **Unique Optimal Solution** with no degeneracy and no alternate optima.

**Step 2 — Economic Interpretation:**

1. **Production Plan**: The factory should manufacture **4.8 units of P** and **3.6 units of Q** per production cycle to maximize profit. **(2 marks)**

2. **Maximum Profit**: Total profit earned = ₹**38.4** (assuming integer values are acceptable; in practice, fractional units imply either rounding or integer programming extension). **(2 marks)**

3. **Resource Utilization**:
   - Machine hours used: $2(4.8) + 1(3.6) = 9.6 + 3.6 = 13.2$... wait, verify: Total available = 12.
   
   **Verification:** $2x_1 + x_2 = 2(24/5) + 18/5 = 48/5 + 18/5 = 66/5 = 13.2$. But constraint says $\leq 12$. **Correction:** Re-check the tableau arithmetic. The slack $s_1 = 0$ in the final basis (since $C_B$ shows $s_1$ is non-basic with value 0). So machine constraint is **fully utilized** at exactly 12 hours.
   
   Correct computation: $2(24/5) + (18/5) = 48/5 + 18/5 = 66/5 = 13.2$. Since this exceeds 12, **re-check the formulation** — actually the tableau is internally consistent with the slack variable values; the simplex iteration guarantees feasibility.
   
   **Resolution:** The initial tableau had $s_1 = 12, s_2 = 15$. After two iterations, $s_1$ became non-basic (value 0) and $s_2$ also non-basic (value 0), implying both constraints are **tight at the optimum**.
   
   By direct substitution back into the original constraints: $2x_1 + x_2 = 12$ and $x_1 + 3x_2 = 15$. Solving: $x_1 = 21/5 = 4.2$, $x_2 = 18/5 = 3.6$. Therefore the optimal solution is $x_1 = 4.2$, $x_2 = 3.6$, $Z = 5(4.2) + 4(3.6) = 21 + 14.4 = 35.4$. **(2 marks)**

4. **Shadow Prices (Dual Values)**: $\pi_1 = 13/5 = 2.6$ and $\pi_2 = 3/5 = 0.6$. An additional machine hour yields ₹2.6 extra profit, while an additional labor hour yields ₹0.6 extra profit. Hence, the manager should prioritize acquiring more machine time over labor time. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> - **(–2 marks)** if student reports the solution type without citing the reduced cost values from the final tableau.
> - **(–2 marks)** if economic interpretation is generic ("max profit") without referencing shadow prices or resource utilization percentages.
> - **(–1 mark)** for not verifying feasibility by substituting the solution back into the original constraints.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Optimal Solution"**

- ✅ **Definition**: An optimal solution is a feasible solution that optimizes the objective function over the entire feasible region.
- ✅ **Fundamental Theorem**: At least one vertex (BFS) of the convex polytope is optimal.
- ✅ **Max Optimality**: $C_j - Z_j \leq 0$ for all non-basic variables $j$.
- ✅ **Min Optimality**: $C_j - Z_j \geq 0$ for all non-basic variables $j$.
- ✅ **Reduced Cost Formula**: $\overline{c}_j = C_j - \sum_i C_{B,i} \cdot a_{ij}$.
- ✅ **Entering Variable (Max)**: Column with largest positive $C_j - Z_j$.
- ✅ **Entering Variable (Min)**: Column with largest negative $C_j - Z_j$.
- ✅ **Leaving Variable**: Determined by Minimum Ratio Test $\theta_i = x_{B,i} / a_{ik}$ where $a_{ik} > 0$.
- ✅ **Alternate Optima Indicator**: Non-basic column has $\overline{c}_j = 0$ in final tableau.
- ✅ **Unboundedness Indicator**: No positive entry in entering column → ratio test fails.
- ✅ **Infeasibility Indicator**: Artificial variable $> 0$ in final basis (Big-M or 2-Phase).
- ✅ **Degeneracy Indicator**: Any basic variable equals 0 in optimal tableau.
- ✅ **Shadow Prices**: $\pi^T = C_B^T B^{-1}$ represent marginal value of resources.
- ✅ **Verification**: Always substitute the final $x$ values back into original constraints.
- ✅ **Tie-Breaking**: Multiple equal $C_j - Z_j$ values → alternate optima; multiple equal ratios → degeneracy.

> **Final Mantra for KTU Board Success:** *"Optimality is reached when no $C_j - Z_j$ entry tells you to improve $Z$ further. The vertex you stand on is the summit of the profit mountain."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hungarian Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT746 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hungarian Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Hungarian Algorithm** is a polynomial-time combinatorial optimization procedure developed by **Harold W. Kuhn in 1955**, named in honor of Hungarian mathematicians **Dénes König** and **Jenő Egerváry**, whose theorem on bipartite matching forms its mathematical foundation. It is the standard algorithmic technique taught under KTU's **Assignment Problem** module to determine the **minimum-cost (or maximum-profit) one-to-one assignment** between a set of *n* jobs (tasks) and *n* workers (machines/agents), ensuring that each worker is assigned to exactly one job and vice versa.

Mathematically, given a cost matrix $C = [c_{ij}]_{n \times n}$, the Hungarian Algorithm solves:

$$\min Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} x_{ij}$$

subject to:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall\, i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall\, j \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$$

$$x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** "The Hungarian Algorithm is an iterative matrix-reduction technique that transforms the original cost matrix through successive row and column operations until the optimal assignment can be identified by selecting *n* independent zeros — one in each row and each column."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **placement coordinator** in a Kerala engineering college who must assign **5 final-year students** to **5 different companies** for their campus placement. Each student has a different rapport/fit score with each company, and the coordinator wants to **maximize total compatibility**. Brute-forcing all $5! = 120$ permutations is feasible, but for $n=20$ students, $20!$ is astronomically large. 

The Hungarian Algorithm is like a **judicious referee** who:
1. First **normalizes the scoreboard** (row reduction) so the worst student-company match for each student reads **zero**.
2. Then **normalizes across companies** (column reduction) so the weakest link for each company also reads **zero**.
3. Next, **draws the fewest possible lines** through rows and columns to cover all the zeros — a process equivalent to finding the maximum matching in a bipartite graph (König's Theorem).
4. If fewer than $n$ lines suffice, the referee **tweaks the matrix again** (subtracts the smallest uncovered value) and repeats.

The beauty: it **never enumerates** $n!$ permutations; it solves the problem in $O(n^3)$ time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The algorithm exploits the fact that **adding or subtracting a constant from any row or column does not change the optimal assignment** — only the optimal cost value shifts by that constant. This invariant is the engine of the entire procedure.

### 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Note |
|---|---|---|
| Computational complexity | $T(n)$ | $O(n^3)$ polynomial time |
| Bipartite matching theorem | König-Egerváry | Max matching $=$ Min vertex cover |
| Optimality condition | $n$ lines to cover all zeros | Equal to matrix order $n$ |
| Year of invention | 1955 | Harold W. Kuhn |
| Reinvention year | 1957 | James Munkres ($O(n^3)$ version) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Logic Flow — Decomposed

The Hungarian Algorithm is executed through **four rigorously-defined phases**. Below is the structural decomposition that KTU examiners expect students to articulate.

**Phase 1 — Row Reduction**
- For each row $i$ of the cost matrix, identify the row minimum $r_i = \min_j \{c_{ij}\}$.
- Subtract $r_i$ from every element in row $i$.
- Mathematical invariant: $c'_{ij} = c_{ij} - r_i$

**Phase 2 — Column Reduction**
- For each column $j$ of the row-reduced matrix, identify the column minimum $c_j = \min_i \{c'_{ij}\}$.
- Subtract $c_j$ from every element in column $j$.
- Mathematical invariant: $c''_{ij} = c'_{ij} - c_j$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why row-then-column?** Because after Phase 1, each row already contains at least one zero. Column reduction preserves this property while introducing column-wise zeros. The order is **not commutative** in general — column-first-then-row is equally valid only if one re-verifies the covering step.

**Phase 3 — Minimum Line Cover Test (Optimality Test)**
- Draw the **minimum number of horizontal and vertical lines** that cover **every zero** in the reduced matrix.
- Apply **König's Theorem**: the minimum number of lines equals the maximum number of independent zeros (i.e., the maximum matching in the bipartite graph formed by zero-positions).
- **Decision rule**:
  - If number of lines $= n$ (the matrix order) $\Rightarrow$ **OPTIMAL ASSIGNMENT FOUND** — make assignment by selecting $n$ zeros such that no two are in the same row or column.
  - If number of lines $< n$ $\Rightarrow$ **PROCEED TO PHASE 4**.

**Phase 4 — Matrix Modification (Augmentation)**
- Let $\theta$ = the **smallest uncovered element** (i.e., the smallest entry not crossed by any of the covering lines).
- Subtract $\theta$ from **every uncovered element**.
- Add $\theta$ to **every doubly-covered element** (intersection of two lines).
- Leave **singly-covered elements** (covered by exactly one line) **unchanged**.
- Return to Phase 3.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Step | Operation | Formula | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Row min | $r_i$ | $r_i = \min_j c_{ij}$ | Computed for all $i = 1 \dots n$ |
| Row reduction | $c'_{ij}$ | $c'_{ij} = c_{ij} - r_i$ | Every row gets at least one zero |
| Column min | $c_j$ | $c_j = \min_i c'_{ij}$ | Computed for all $j = 1 \dots n$ |
| Column reduction | $c''_{ij}$ | $c''_{ij} = c'_{ij} - c_j$ | Every column gets at least one zero |
| Cover test | $L$ | $L = $ min lines to cover zeros | Optimal iff $L = n$ |
| Uncovered min | $\theta$ | $\theta = \min\{c''_{ij} : \text{uncovered}\}$ | $\theta > 0$ when $L < n$ |
| Augmentation | $c'''_{ij}$ | $c'''_{ij} = c''_{ij} - \theta$ (uncovered) | New zeros emerge |
| Augmentation | $c'''_{ij}$ | $c'''_{ij} = c''_{ij} + \theta$ (doubly-covered) | Existing matches preserved |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Beyond the textbook assignment problem, the Hungarian Algorithm underpins modern applications in **robot task allocation**, **VLSI circuit design** (pin assignment), **vehicle routing**, **job-shop scheduling**, **aircraft crew scheduling**, and **image feature matching** in computer vision.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Illustration (KTU Board Standard)

**Problem Statement (Module 2 — Assignment Type):**

A construction firm in Kerala has **4 projects** and **4 contractors**. The estimated profits (in ₹ Lakhs) for assigning each contractor to each project are given below. Use the **Hungarian Algorithm** to determine the assignment that **maximizes total profit**.

| Contractor \ Project | P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **C1** | 42 | 35 | 28 | 50 |
| **C2** | 30 | 25 | 20 | 45 |
| **C3** | 38 | 40 | 30 | 35 |
| **C4** | 25 | 32 | 22 | 28 |

---

**Step 1 — Convert Maximization to Minimization**

To apply the Hungarian Algorithm (which natively solves **minimization**), we transform the profit matrix $P$ into a cost matrix $C$ using the **opportunity-loss transformation**:

$$c_{ij} = M - p_{ij}$$

where $M$ is the **largest element** in $P$. Here $M = 50$.

Computing each entry:

$$
c_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix}
50-42 & 50-35 & 50-28 & 50-50 \\
50-30 & 50-25 & 50-20 & 50-45 \\
50-38 & 50-40 & 50-30 & 50-35 \\
50-25 & 50-32 & 50-22 & 50-28
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
8 & 15 & 22 & 0 \\
20 & 25 & 30 & 5 \\
12 & 10 & 20 & 15 \\
25 & 18 & 28 & 22
\end{bmatrix}
$$

---

**Step 2 — Row Reduction**

Compute row minima $r_i$ and subtract:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= \min\{8, 15, 22, 0\} = 0 \\
r_2 &= \min\{20, 25, 30, 5\} = 5 \\
r_3 &= \min\{12, 10, 20, 15\} = 10 \\
r_4 &= \min\{25, 18, 28, 22\} = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

Row-reduced matrix:

$$
C^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
8 & 15 & 22 & 0 \\
15 & 20 & 25 & 0 \\
2 & 0 & 10 & 5 \\
7 & 0 & 10 & 4
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Each row now contains at least one zero (rows 1, 2, 3, 4 have zeros in columns 4, 4, 2, 2 respectively).

---

**Step 3 — Column Reduction**

Identify column minima of $C^{(1)}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_1 &= \min\{8, 15, 2, 7\} = 2 \\
c_2 &= \min\{15, 20, 0, 0\} = 0 \\
c_3 &= \min\{22, 25, 10, 10\} = 10 \\
c_4 &= \min\{0, 0, 5, 4\} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Column-reduced matrix:

$$
C^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
6 & 15 & 12 & 0 \\
13 & 20 & 15 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 5 \\
5 & 0 & 0 & 4
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Every row and every column now has at least one zero.

---

**Step 4 — Minimum Line Cover Test**

Let us locate all zero positions:

$$
Z = \{(1,4),\ (2,4),\ (3,1),\ (3,2),\ (3,3),\ (4,2),\ (4,3)\}
$$

Draw the minimum covering lines. By inspection, we can cover all zeros using only **3 lines**:
- **Line 1**: Row 3 (covers zeros at $(3,1), (3,2), (3,3)$)
- **Line 2**: Column 4 (covers zeros at $(1,4), (2,4)$)
- **Line 3**: Row 4 (covers zero at $(4,2), (4,3)$)

Since $L = 3 < n = 4$, **optimality is NOT yet reached**. Proceed to Step 5.

---

**Step 5 — Augmentation (Matrix Modification)**

Find the smallest **uncovered element** $\theta$. The uncovered entries are those **not** in row 3, column 4, or row 4.

Examining the matrix $C^{(2)}$ entries at non-covered positions:
- $(1,1) = 6$
- $(1,2) = 15$
- $(1,3) = 12$
- $(2,1) = 13$
- $(2,2) = 20$
- $(2,3) = 15$

Therefore:

$$\theta = \min\{6, 15, 12, 13, 20, 15\} = 6$$

Apply the augmentation rules:

$$c'''_{ij} = \begin{cases}
c^{(2)}_{ij} - \theta & \text{if } (i,j) \text{ is uncovered} \\
c^{(2)}_{ij} + \theta & \text{if } (i,j) \text{ is doubly-covered (row 3 ∩ col 4)} \\
c^{(2)}_{ij} & \text{if } (i,j) \text{ is singly-covered}
\end{cases}
$$

Doubly-covered positions: $(3,4)$ only.

Updated matrix:

$$
C^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 9 & 6 & 0 \\
7 & 14 & 9 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 11 \\
5 & 0 & 0 & 4
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why add $\theta$ to doubly-covered elements?** This prevents any previously-zero element from becoming positive, thereby preserving all valid assignments while creating new zeros in uncovered cells.

---

**Step 6 — Re-test Minimum Line Cover**

Zero positions in $C^{(3)}$:

$$
Z' = \{(1,1),\ (1,4),\ (2,4),\ (3,1),\ (3,2),\ (3,3),\ (4,2),\ (4,3)\}
$$

We attempt to find an independent set of 4 zeros — one per row and one per column:
- **C1 → P1** (cell $(1,1)$) ✓
- **C2 → P4** (cell $(2,4)$) ✓
- **C3 → P3** (cell $(3,3)$) ✓
- **C4 → P2** (cell $(4,2)$) ✓

All four chosen zeros are in distinct rows AND distinct columns $\Rightarrow$ **Independent Assignment Exists** $\Rightarrow$ $L = n = 4$ $\Rightarrow$ **OPTIMAL!**

---

**Step 7 — Compute Maximum Profit**

Using original profit matrix $P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C1} &\to \text{P1}: p_{11} = 42 \\
\text{C2} &\to \text{P4}: p_{24} = 45 \\
\text{C3} &\to \text{P3}: p_{33} = 30 \\
\text{C4} &\to \text{P2}: p_{42} = 32
\end{aligned}
$$

Total Profit:

$$
Z_{\max} = 42 + 45 + 30 + 32 = 149 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

---

**Step 8 — Verification of Augmented Matrix Optimality (Optional Cross-Check)**

Using the transformed matrix $C^{(3)}$, the chosen zeros all sum to **zero** — confirming the minimum opportunity loss. The opportunity loss total is:

$$Z_{\min}^{C} = 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 0$$

This corresponds to: $Z_{\max}^{P} = nM - Z_{\min}^{C} = 4(50) - 0 = 200$ — wait, this is **incorrect**; the formula uses the *original* $M$ but subtracts the augmented row/column reductions. The proper inverse transform requires tracking all subtracted constants:

- Total row reduction: $0 + 5 + 10 + 18 = 33$
- Total column reduction (after rows): $2 + 0 + 10 + 0 = 12$
- $\theta$ contributions from one augmentation: covered 6 cells, doubled 1 cell ⇒ net $\theta \cdot (\text{uncovered} - \text{doubly-covered}) = 6 \cdot (6 - 1) = 30$

But the simpler practical approach used in KTU valuation is to **directly read off the original profit matrix** for the optimal assignment, as done above: **₹149 Lakhs**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Input Cost Matrix C of order n"] --> B["Phase 1: Row Reduction"]
    B --> B1["For each row i, subtract row-min r_i from every element"]
    B1 --> C["Phase 2: Column Reduction"]
    C --> C1["For each column j, subtract col-min c_j from every element"]
    C1 --> D["Phase 3: Cover All Zeros with Min Lines L"]
    D --> D1{"Is L equal to n?"}
    D1 -- Yes --> E["OPTIMAL: Select n independent zeros"]
    D1 -- No --> F["Phase 4: Find smallest uncovered element theta"]
    F --> F1["Subtract theta from uncovered elements"]
    F1 --> F2["Add theta to doubly-covered elements"]
    F2 --> F3["Leave singly-covered elements unchanged"]
    F3 --> D
    E --> G["Read optimal assignment from original matrix"]
    G --> H["Compute Min Cost or Max Profit"]
    H --> I["End"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hungarian Algorithm Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input["INPUT MODULE: Cost Matrix Cn×n"] --> PreProc["PREPROCESSOR: Max→Min Transform M - pij"]
    PreProc --> RowRed["ROW REDUCTION SUBSYSTEM: r_i = min_j cij"]
    RowRed --> ColRed["COLUMN REDUCTION SUBSYSTEM: c_j = min_i cij"]
    ColRed --> Cover["COVERING SUBSYSTEM: König's Theorem Matcher"]
    Cover --> Decision{"OPTIMALITY DECISION MODULE"}
    Decision -- "L = n: PASS" --> Assign["ASSIGNMENT RESOLVER: Greedy Zero-Picker"]
    Decision -- "L less than n: FAIL" --> Aug["AUGMENTATION SUBSYSTEM: theta Modifier"]
    Aug --> Cover
    Assign --> Profit["PROFIT COST EVALUATOR"]
    Profit --> Output["OUTPUT MODULE: Optimal Pairing + Total"]
```

### 4.3 Iteration Trace — Visual Summary of the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEP 1: Original Profit Matrix P 4x4"] --> S2["STEP 2: Opportunity Loss C = 50 - P"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3: Row Reduced C-1"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4: Column Reduced C-2: L = 3 less than 4"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5: theta = 6: Augment → C-3"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6: New L = 4: OPTIMAL"]
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7: Zmax = 149 Lakhs"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Aid for Students:** Each block above represents a *deterministic state transition*. The Hungarian Algorithm has at most $O(n^2)$ augmentations, each costing $O(n^2)$ — yielding the celebrated $O(n^3)$ overall bound.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

State the **König-Egerváry theorem** that underlies the optimality test in the Hungarian Algorithm. Why is it essential for the algorithm's correctness?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The König-Egerváry theorem states that in any bipartite graph $G = (X \cup Y, E)$, the **maximum cardinality of a matching** equals the **minimum cardinality of a vertex cover**.

In the Hungarian Algorithm context, the bipartite graph is constructed by placing zeros of the reduced matrix as edges between row-nodes (workers) and column-nodes (jobs). The minimum number of lines drawn through rows and columns that cover all zeros is **exactly** the minimum vertex cover — and by the theorem, this equals the maximum independent zero-set, which is precisely the size of an optimal assignment. The theorem guarantees that when fewer than $n$ lines suffice, no full assignment exists yet, and augmentation is required. *(Examiner's Note: Award full marks only if both statement and role are articulated.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Explain why **adding or subtracting a constant from any row or column** of the cost matrix does not change the optimal assignment.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Consider the cost matrix $C = [c_{ij}]$ and a constant $k$ added to row $i$. Then the modified cost is $\tilde{c}_{ij} = c_{ij} + k$ for all $j$, and unchanged for other rows. For any assignment permutation $\sigma$, the total modified cost is:

$$\tilde{Z}(\sigma) = \sum_{i} \tilde{c}_{i,\sigma(i)} = \sum_{i} c_{i,\sigma(i)} + k = Z(\sigma) + k$$

Since $k$ is **independent of the permutation** $\sigma$, the permutation that minimizes $\tilde{Z}$ is the **same** as the one that minimizes $Z$. The optimal value shifts by $+k$, but the optimal assignment $\sigma^*$ remains invariant. This linearity property is the cornerstone of row and column reduction. *(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for invariance conclusion, 1 mark for the explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert maximization to minimization** — many students apply the algorithm directly to a profit matrix, which yields wrong answers. Always apply $c_{ij} = M - p_{ij}$.
> 2. **Incorrectly drawing covering lines** — students often draw $n$ lines and declare optimality without verifying that they are the *minimum* cover. Loss of **2 marks** in 14-mark problems is common.
> 3. **Augmentation sign errors** — subtracting $\theta$ from uncovered cells but **forgetting to add $\theta$** to doubly-covered cells is a frequent mistake that corrupts future iterations.
> 4. **Multiple optima** — if the reduced matrix yields more than one independent zero-set, all must be reported. Examiners award full credit only when alternate optima are mentioned.
