- **Mechanical Engineering:** Assembly-line planning in automotive and aerospace manufacturing.
- **Software Engineering:** Sprint planning where the critical path determines the **minimum time-to-delivery**.
- **Industrial Engineering:** Plant shut-down and maintenance turnarounds.
- **Shipbuilding & Aerospace:** Coordinating thousands of interdependent sub-assemblies.

> [!NOTE]
> In modern Project Management offices (PMOs), CPM is the analytical backbone of tools like **Microsoft Project, Primavera P6, and Smartsheet**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Worked Example)

## 3.1 Problem Statement

> A construction firm has identified the following activities for a small office building project. Draw the AOA network, perform the forward and backward passes, identify the critical path, and compute the total float for every activity.

| Activity | Immediate Predecessors | Duration (days) |
|:--------:|:----------------------:|:---------------:|
| A | — | 5 |
| B | — | 6 |
| C | A | 4 |
| D | A | 7 |
| E | B | 5 |
| F | B | 3 |
| G | C, E | 6 |
| H | D, F | 8 |
| I | G, H | 4 |
| J | I | 5 |

## 3.2 Network Logic (AOA Form)

- A: 1 → 2 (5)
- B: 1 → 3 (6)
- C: 2 → 4 (4)
- D: 2 → 5 (7)
- E: 3 → 4 (5)
- F: 3 → 5 (3)
- G: 4 → 6 (6)
- H: 5 → 6 (8)
- I: 6 → 7 (4)
- J: 7 → 8 (5)

## 3.3 Forward Pass — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Initialise the start event.

$$E_1 = 0$$

**Step 2:** Compute earliest occurrence of events 2 and 3 (single incoming activity each).

$$E_2 = E_1 + d_A = 0 + 5 = 5$$

$$E_3 = E_1 + d_B = 0 + 6 = 6$$

**Step 3:** Compute $E_4$. Two predecessors: C (from event 2) and E (from event 3).

$$E_4 = \max(E_2 + d_C,\; E_3 + d_E) = \max(5 + 4,\; 6 + 5) = \max(9, 11) = 11$$

**Step 4:** Compute $E_5$. Two predecessors: D (from event 2) and F (from event 3).

$$E_5 = \max(E_2 + d_D,\; E_3 + d_F) = \max(5 + 7,\; 6 + 3) = \max(12, 9) = 12$$

**Step 5:** Compute $E_6$. Two predecessors: G (from event 4) and H (from event 5).

$$E_6 = \max(E_4 + d_G,\; E_5 + d_H) = \max(11 + 6,\; 12 + 8) = \max(17, 20) = 20$$

**Step 6:** Compute $E_7$.

$$E_7 = E_6 + d_I = 20 + 4 = 24$$

**Step 7:** Compute $E_8$ (terminal event).

$$E_8 = E_7 + d_J = 24 + 5 = 29$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Project Duration $T_p = E_8 = 29$ days.**

## 3.4 Backward Pass — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Initialise the terminal event.

$$L_8 = E_8 = 29$$

**Step 2:** Compute $L_7$ (single incoming: J).

$$L_7 = L_8 - d_J = 29 - 5 = 24$$

**Step 3:** Compute $L_6$ (single incoming: I).

$$L_6 = L_7 - d_I = 24 - 4 = 20$$

**Step 4:** Compute $L_5$. Two outgoing: H (to 6).

$$L_5 = L_6 - d_H = 20 - 8 = 12$$

**Step 5:** Compute $L_4$. One outgoing: G (to 6).

$$L_4 = L_6 - d_G = 20 - 6 = 14$$

**Step 6:** Compute $L_3$. Two outgoing activities: E (to 4) and F (to 5).

$$L_3 = \min(L_4 - d_E,\; L_5 - d_F) = \min(14 - 5,\; 12 - 3) = \min(9, 9) = 9$$

**Step 7:** Compute $L_2$. Two outgoing: C (to 4) and D (to 5).

$$L_2 = \min(L_4 - d_C,\; L_5 - d_D) = \min(14 - 4,\; 12 - 7) = \min(10, 5) = 5$$

**Step 8:** Compute $L_1$ (start event). Two outgoing: A and B.

$$L_1 = \min(L_2 - d_A,\; L_3 - d_B) = \min(5 - 5,\; 9 - 6) = \min(0, 3) = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $L_1 = 0 = E_1$, the network is **logically consistent**.

## 3.5 Critical Path Identification

Compare $E_i$ and $L_i$ for every event:

| Event | $E_i$ | $L_i$ | Critical? |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:---------:|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| 2 | 5 | 5 | ✓ |
| 3 | 6 | 9 | ✗ (slack = 3) |
| 4 | 11 | 14 | ✗ (slack = 3) |
| 5 | 12 | 12 | ✓ |
| 6 | 20 | 20 | ✓ |
| 7 | 24 | 24 | ✓ |
| 8 | 29 | 29 | ✓ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Path: 1 → 2 → 5 → 6 → 7 → 8**
> **Critical Activities: A → D → H → I → J**
> **Project Duration: 29 days**

## 3.6 Activity Schedule Table

For every activity $(i, j)$:

$$ES = E_i, \quad EF = E_i + d_{ij}, \quad LS = L_j - d_{ij}, \quad LF = L_j$$

$$TF = LS - ES, \quad FF = E_j - EF$$

| Act | $(i,j)$ | $d_{ij}$ | $ES$ | $EF$ | $LS$ | $LF$ | $TF$ | $FF$ | Critical? |
|:---:|:-------:|:--------:|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----:|:---------:|
| A | (1,2) | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| B | (1,3) | 6 | 0 | 6 | 3 | 9 | 3 | 0 | ✗ |
| C | (2,4) | 4 | 5 | 9 | 10 | 14 | 5 | 2 | ✗ |
| D | (2,5) | 7 | 5 | 12 | 5 | 12 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| E | (3,4) | 5 | 6 | 11 | 9 | 14 | 3 | 0 | ✗ |
| F | (3,5) | 3 | 6 | 9 | 9 | 12 | 3 | 3 | ✗ |
| G | (4,6) | 6 | 11 | 17 | 14 | 20 | 3 | 3 | ✗ |
| H | (5,6) | 8 | 12 | 20 | 12 | 20 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| I | (6,7) | 4 | 20 | 24 | 20 | 24 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| J | (7,8) | 5 | 24 | 29 | 24 | 29 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Sum of durations on the critical path: $5 + 7 + 8 + 4 + 5 = 29$ days ✓ — this matches the project duration, confirming our critical path is correct.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Activity-on-Arrow (AOA) Network Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    n1((1)) -- A:5 --> n2((2))
    n1((1)) -- B:6 --> n3((3))
    n2((2)) -- C:4 --> n4((4))
    n2((2)) -- D:7 --> n5((5))
    n3((3)) -- E:5 --> n4((4))
    n3((3)) -- F:3 --> n5((5))
    n4((4)) -- G:6 --> n6((6))
    n5((5)) -- H:8 --> n6((6))
    n6((6)) -- I:4 --> n7((7))
    n7((7)) -- J:5 --> n8((8))

    style n1 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style n2 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style n3 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style n4 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style n5 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style n6 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style n7 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style n8 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Blue nodes (1, 2, 5, 6, 7, 8)** lie on the **Critical Path**. **Orange nodes (3, 4)** are off the critical path and have float. Each arrow is labelled `Activity:Duration`.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the CPM Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Project Inputs: Activities + Durations] --> A1[Step 1: Identify Precedence Relations]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Construct AOA Network Diagram]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Forward Pass - Compute E_i]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Backward Pass - Compute L_i]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Compute ES, EF, LS, LF for each activity]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Calculate Total Float TF = LS minus ES]
    A6 --> A7{TF equals 0?}
    A7 -- Yes --> A8[Mark Activity as CRITICAL]
    A7 -- No --> A9[Activity is NON-CRITICAL with Float]
    A8 --> A10[Compile Critical Path: Sequence of Critical Activities]
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11[Output: Project Duration, Critical Path, Float Table]
```

## 4.3 Float Visualisation Matrix

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Critical_Chain[CRITICAL CHAIN - Zero Float]
        c1[A: 5d] --> c2[D: 7d] --> c3[H: 8d] --> c4[I: 4d] --> c5[J: 5d]
    end

    subgraph Parallel_1[Parallel Branch 1: B then F]
        p1[B: 6d] --> p2[F: 3d]
    end

    subgraph Parallel_2[Parallel Branch 2: C and E feeding G]
        p3[C: 4d] --> p4[G: 6d]
        p5[E: 5d] --> p4
    end

    style c1 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style c2 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style c3 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style c4 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style c5 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style p1 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style p2 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style p3 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style p4 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style p5 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Red chain** = Critical path (no slack). **Green chain** = Non-critical activities that have float and can be delayed without affecting project completion.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the Critical Path Method. What is meant by a "critical activity"?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Critical Path Method (CPM)** is a network-based project scheduling technique used to plan and control complex engineering projects. It models a project as a network of activities (arrows) and events (nodes), calculates the earliest and latest occurrence times of each event through forward and backward passes, and identifies the **longest-duration path** through the network.

A **critical activity** is one that lies on the critical path and therefore has **zero total float ($TF = 0$)**. Any delay in a critical activity will directly delay the project completion date by an equal amount. In our worked example, activities **A, D, H, I, and J** are critical.

> [!NOTE]
> Critical activities are the *bottleneck* of a project and demand the most managerial attention.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Total Float and Free Float with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Total Float (TF) | Free Float (FF) |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| Definition | Time an activity can be delayed without delaying **project completion**. | Time an activity can be delayed without delaying the **earliest start of any successor activity**. |
| Formula | $TF = LS - ES$ | $FF = E_j - EF$ |
| Effect | Affects project deadline. | Affects only immediate successors. |
| Magnitude | Always $\geq$ Free Float. | Can be smaller than Total Float. |

**Example (from our worked example, Activity G):**

$$TF_G = LS - ES = 14 - 11 = 3 \text{ days}$$

$$FF_G = E_6 - EF_G = 20 - 17 = 3 \text{ days}$$

Here, activity G can be delayed by up to **3 days** without delaying the project, AND without delaying the start of its successor I.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Network Construction & Critical Path

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing project consists of 9 activities with the following precedence and duration data. Construct the AOA network diagram:

| Activity | A | B | C | D | E | F | G | H | I |
|:--------:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Duration (days) | 4 | 6 | 5 | 3 | 7 | 4 | 8 | 5 | 6 |
| Predecessor | — | — | A | A | B | C | D, E | F | G, H |

**(b) [7 Marks]** Perform the forward and backward passes, identify the critical path, and determine the project duration. Also find the total float for activity F.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Network Construction (AOA):**

- A: 1 → 2 (4)
- B: 1 → 3 (6)
- C: 2 → 4 (5)
- D: 2 → 5 (3)
- E: 3 → 5 (7)
- F: 4 → 6 (4)
- G: 5 → 7 (8)  *(merge node since both D and E feed into G)*
- H: 6 → 7 (5)  *(F feeds into H; H and G both feed into I, so we need a merge at 7)*
- I: 7 → 8 (6)

**Step 2 — Forward Pass:**

$$E_1 = 0$$
$$E_2 = 0 + 4 = 4$$
$$E_3 = 0 + 6 = 6$$
$$E_4 = 4 + 5 = 9$$
$$E_5 = \max(4 + 3,\; 6 + 7) = \max(7, 13) = 13$$
$$E_6 = 9 + 4 = 13$$
$$E_7 = \max(13 + 8,\; 13 + 5) = \max(21, 18) = 21$$
$$E_8 = 21 + 6 = 27$$

**Project Duration = 27 days.**

**Step 3 — Backward Pass:**

$$L_8 = 27$$
$$L_7 = 27 - 6 = 21$$
$$L_6 = 21 - 5 = 16$$
$$L_5 = 21 - 8 = 13$$
$$L_4 = 16 - 4 = 12$$
$$L_3 = 13 - 7 = 6$$
$$L_2 = \min(12 - 5,\; 13 - 3) = \min(7, 10) = 7$$
$$L_1 = \min(7 - 4,\; 6 - 6) = \min(3, 0) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Critical Path:**

Events where $E_i = L_i$: 1, 3, 5, 7, 8.

**Critical Path: 1 → 3 → 5 → 7 → 8**
**Critical Activities: B → E → G → I**
**Project Duration: 27 days**

**Step 5 — Total Float of Activity F (4 → 6):**

$$ES_F = E_4 = 9, \quad EF_F = 9 + 4 = 13$$
$$LF_F = L_6 = 16, \quad LS_F = 16 - 4 = 12$$
$$TF_F = LS_F - ES_F = 12 - 9 = \mathbf{3 \text{ days}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative: Float Analysis

`[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three types of float (Total, Free, and Independent) with formulae. Why is Independent Float the most conservative measure?

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the network below, compute the Free Float and Independent Float for every non-critical activity:

| Activity | (i, j) | Duration |
|:--------:|:------:|:--------:|
| A | (1,2) | 6 |
| B | (1,3) | 4 |
| C | (2,3) | 2 |
| D | (2,4) | 5 |
| E | (3,4) | 7 |
| F | (4,5) | 4 |

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Float Definitions:**

1. **Total Float:** $TF = LS - ES$. Maximum delay without delaying the project.
2. **Free Float:** $FF = E_j - EF$. Maximum delay without delaying any immediate successor.
3. **Independent Float:** $IF = \max(0, E_j - LF)$. Float available when all predecessors are at their **latest** times AND all successors start at their **earliest**. It is the most conservative because it assumes the **worst-case scheduling scenario**.

**Part (b) — Computations:**

Forward pass:
- $E_1 = 0, E_2 = 6, E_3 = \max(6+2, 0+4) = 8, E_4 = \max(6+5, 8+7) = 15, E_5 = 15+4 = 19$.

Project duration = **19 days**.

Backward pass:
- $L_5 = 19, L_4 = 19-4 = 15, L_3 = 15-7 = 8, L_2 = \min(8-2, 15-5) = 6, L_1 = \min(6-6, 8-4) = 0$.

Float calculations:

| Activity | $ES$ | $EF$ | $LS$ | $LF$ | $TF$ | $FF = E_j - EF$ | $IF = \max(0, E_j - LF)$ |
|:--------:|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----:|:---------------:|:------------------------:|
| A | 0 | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 0 | 0 (critical) |
| B | 0 | 4 | 4 | 8 | 4 | 4 | 0 |
| C | 6 | 8 | 6 | 8 | 0 | 0 | 0 (critical) |
| D | 6 | 11 | 10 | 15 | 4 | 4 | 0 |
| E | 8 | 15 | 8 | 15 | 0 | 0 | 0 (critical) |
| F | 15 | 19 | 15 | 19 | 0 | 0 | 0 (critical) |

**Critical Path: 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 (A → C → E → F), Duration = 19 days.**

Non-critical activities B and D each have $TF = 4$ days, $FF = 4$ days, and $IF = 0$ days.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting Dummy Activities** — In AOA networks, two activities sharing the same predecessor and successor require a *dummy activity* to preserve logical precedence. Missing dummies is the **#1 reason** for losing 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Forward & Backward Passes** — Many students incorrectly compute the start event's $L_1$ by ignoring the $\min$ rule across multiple outgoing branches.
> 3. **Identifying the Wrong "Critical Path"** — Students often pick the path with the most activities instead of the path with the **longest total duration**. Always verify by summing durations.
> 4. **Missing the Final Consistency Check** — KTU examiners award a full mark for explicitly stating $E_1 = L_1 = 0$ as a network consistency verification.
> 5. **Float Calculation Errors** — Using $E_j$ where $L_j$ is required (or vice-versa) results in zero marks for the float sub-part.
> 6. **Network with Loops** — Drawing a cyclic network is a fatal error; CPM assumes **DAGs** (Directed Acyclic Graphs).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
> - CPM is a **deterministic** network technique; PERT is its **probabilistic** counterpart.
> - Always use the **AOA (Activity-on-Arrow)** convention for CPM in KTU unless stated otherwise.
> - Forward pass uses **MAX** at converging nodes; Backward pass uses **MIN** at diverging nodes.
> - $E_1 = 0$ and $L_n = E_n$ are the boundary conditions.
> - An activity is **critical** iff $TF = 0$, which is equivalent to the path through the network having the **maximum total duration**.
> - $TF \geq FF \geq IF \geq 0$ always.
> - **Dummy activities** have zero duration and exist only to represent logical dependencies in AOA networks.
> - **Free Float** preserves the successor's earliest start; **Total Float** preserves only the project finish.
> - Always verify the critical path by summing durations of the consecutive critical activities — it should equal $E_n$.
> - Network must be **acyclic**, **single-start**, and **single-end**.
> - KTU exam tip: Draw the AOA diagram neatly with arrow labels, number the events, and present the **schedule table** as the final answer block for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programme Evaluation Review Technique (PERT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT746 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Programme Evaluation Review Technique (PERT) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Programme Evaluation Review Technique (PERT)** is a probabilistic project management tool developed in **1958** by the United States Navy's Special Projects Office in collaboration with the management consulting firm Booz, Allen & Hamilton, primarily for the planning and coordination of the **Polaris Missile Submarine Program**. PERT is designed to schedule, organize, and coordinate tasks within a project where the duration of activities is **uncertain** and follows a **Beta probability distribution**. Mathematically, PERT models each activity's duration using **three time estimates** — optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic — and computes a single **Expected Time ($t_e$)** that allows the analyst to apply Critical Path Method (CPM)-style network analysis on top of a probabilistic framework. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** PERT is classified under *Probabilistic Network Models* in the Module 3 syllabus. It is fundamentally different from CPM because activity durations are **not deterministic** but are treated as random variables. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wedding Planner" Intuition Imagine you are planning a destination wedding. Each task (booking the venue, sending invitations, arranging catering) takes time — but you don't know *exactly* how long. Your optimistic uncle says the venue booking will take **3 days**, the realistic planner says **7 days**, and your pessimistic cousin warns it could take **15 days** if there are paperwork delays. PERT lets you: 1. **Capture uncertainty** using these three estimates (just like the three relatives). 2. **Compute a weighted average expected time** that accounts for the fact that extreme outcomes are less likely than moderate ones. 3. **Find the critical path** — the chain of events that, if delayed, will delay the entire wedding. 4. **Quantify risk** — "What is the probability the wedding happens before the auspicious date?" The wedding date = project deadline. The critical path = the bottleneck activities. The probability = your confidence in meeting the auspicious date. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** PERT answers two crucial questions: (1) *When is the project expected to finish?* and (2) *What is the probability of finishing before a given deadline?* ## 1.3 Key Terminology | Term | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---|---| | Optimistic Time | $t_o$ | Minimum possible time if everything goes perfectly | | Most Likely Time | $t_m$ | Time required under normal working conditions | | Pessimistic Time | $t_p$ | Maximum time if nearly everything goes wrong | | Expected Time | $t_e$ | Weighted average estimate used for scheduling | | Variance | $\sigma^2$ | Measure of uncertainty/spread of the activity | | Standard Deviation | $\sigma$ | Square root of variance | | Scheduled Time | $T_s$ | Promised/required completion time | | Expected Project Duration | $T_E$ | Sum of $t_e$ along the critical path | | Critical Path | CP | Longest-duration path through the network | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Time Estimates — Detailed Interpretation PERT assumes the actual duration of an activity follows a **Beta distribution**, characterized by four parameters: minimum ($t_o$), maximum ($t_p$), mode ($t_m$), and two shape parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$. The Beta distribution is chosen because: 1. It is bounded on both sides (no negative durations, no infinite durations). 2. It is unimodal (single peak). 3. Its mean and variance can be estimated from the three time points alone. ### Interpretation of Each Estimate: - **$t_o$ (Optimistic):** The activity duration when *every* favourable event occurs. Probability of achieving this is very small. Conventionally, PERT assumes only a **1 in 100** chance that the activity is completed in less than $t_o$. - **$t_m$ (Most Likely):** The duration under normal/representative conditions. Corresponds to the **mode** of the Beta distribution. - **$t_p$ (Pessimistic):** The duration when *every* unfavourable event occurs. Probability of exceeding this is very small (1 in 100). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — PERT Master Reference > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMULAS FOR KTU EXAMS** ### Core Computational Formulas | # | Formula Name | Equation | Description / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Expected Time | $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ | Weighted average (days/weeks) | | 2 | Variance | $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$ | Squared units of time | | 3 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}$ | Same units as time | | 4 | Expected Project Duration | $T_E = \sum t_e \text{ along CP}$ | Critical path sum | | 5 | Project Variance | $\sigma_{CP}^2 = \sum \sigma^2 \text{ along CP}$ | Sum of variances on CP | | 6 | Project Std. Deviation | $\sigma_{CP} = \sqrt{\sigma_{CP}^2}$ | Square root of project variance | | 7 | Z-Value (Probability) | $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_{CP}}$ | Dimensionless standard score | | 8 | Probability | $P(Z) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{Z} \phi(z)\,dz$ | Read from Standard Normal Table | ### Forward Pass Formulas (Event Times) | # | Formula | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 9 | $E_j = \max_i \{E_i + t_e^{(i,j)}\}$ | Earliest occurrence time of event $j$ | | 10 | $E_1 = 0$ | Initial event always starts at zero | ### Backward Pass Formulas (Event Times) | # | Formula | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 11 | $L_i = \min_j \{L_j - t_e^{(i,j)}\}$ | Latest allowable occurrence time of event $i$ | | 12 | $L_n = E_n = T_E$ | Terminal event's latest = earliest | ### Slack / Float Formulas | # | Formula | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 13 | $S_j = L_j - E_j$ | Slack of event $j$ | | 14 | $S_A = L_j - E_i - t_e$ | Slack of activity $A$ (ending at $j$) | | 15 | Critical Event | $S_j = 0 \implies$ Event $j$ is critical | ## 2.3 The Logic Behind the Expected Time Formula The expected time formula $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ is a direct consequence of assuming a Beta distribution with parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ chosen such that the mode occurs at $t_m$. The Beta distribution mean is: $$\mu = \dfrac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta} \cdot (t_p - t_o) + t_o$$ For the Beta distribution, the mode is: $$\text{Mode} = \dfrac{(\alpha - 1)(t_p - t_o)}{(\alpha + \beta - 2)} + t_o$$ Setting Mode = $t_m$ and solving for the weights (with the assumption $\alpha + \beta = 6$, which yields the elegant 1-4-1 weighting), gives us the standard PERT formula. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** In construction, defense, aerospace, and R&D projects — where activity durations are inherently uncertain — PERT allows quantitative risk assessment. Modern software like **Microsoft Project, Primavera P6** still uses these exact formulas internally. ## 2.4 Difference Between PERT and CPM (Frequently Asked in KTU) | Feature | PERT | CPM | |---|---|---| | Time Estimates | Three estimates (probabilistic) | Single estimate (deterministic) | | Distribution | Beta distribution | Not applicable | | Focus | Time-focused | Time-cost trade-off | | Best For | R&D, research, non-repetitive | Construction, repetitive tasks | | Crashing | Not typically applied | Cost-based crashing is core | | Variability | Explicit variance calculation | Variance = 0 implicitly | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Expected Time Formula ### Starting Point: Beta Distribution Mean For a Beta distribution defined on the interval $[a, b]$ with shape parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$: $$\mu = a + \dfrac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta}(b - a)$$ ### Apply PERT Boundaries Let $a = t_o$ (optimistic, lower bound), $b = t_p$ (pessimistic, upper bound): $$\mu = t_o + \dfrac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta}(t_p - t_o)$$ ### Beta Distribution Mode The mode of a Beta distribution is: $$\text{Mode} = \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha + \beta - 2}(t_p - t_o) + t_o$$ ### Setting Mode Equal to Most Likely Time In PERT, the most likely time $t_m$ equals the mode of the Beta distribution: $$t_m = \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha + \beta - 2}(t_p - t_o) + t_o$$ ### Simplifying Assumption PERT assumes $\alpha + \beta = 6$. Then $\alpha - 1$ and $\alpha + \beta - 2 = 4$: $$t_m - t_o = \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{4}(t_p - t_o)$$ $$\dfrac{4(t_m - t_o)}{t_p - t_o} = \alpha - 1$$ $$\alpha = 1 + \dfrac{4(t_m - t_o)}{t_p - t_o}$$ ### Computing the Mean $$\mu = t_o + \dfrac{\alpha}{6}(t_p - t_o)$$ $$\mu = t_o + \dfrac{1}{6} \cdot \left[1 + \dfrac{4(t_m - t_o)}{t_p - t_o}\right] \cdot (t_p - t_o)$$ $$\mu = t_o + \dfrac{1}{6}\left[(t_p - t_o) + 4(t_m - t_o)\right]$$ $$\mu = \dfrac{6t_o + t_p - t_o + 4t_m - 4t_o}{6}$$ $$\mu = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$$ $$\boxed{\mu = t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of the Variance Formula The variance of a Beta distribution on $[t_o, t_p]$ is: $$\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\alpha \beta}{(\alpha + \beta)^2 (\alpha + \beta + 1)} \cdot (t_p - t_o)^2$$ Under the assumption $\alpha + \beta = 6$, PERT approximates this (using $\alpha \beta \approx 1$ when $t_m$ is near the midpoint) as: $$\sigma^2 \approx \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$$ $$\boxed{\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is an *approximation*. The exact Beta variance requires $\alpha$ and $\beta$, but for engineering-grade estimates, the 1/6 rule is universally accepted and used in all KTU examinations. ## 3.3 Worked Example: Complete PERT Analysis ### Problem Statement A project consists of 7 activities: A, B, C, D, E, F, G. The network structure is given below: - A and B are starting activities (parallel). - C follows A; D follows B. - E follows both C and D (merge node). - F follows E. - G follows F (terminal). | Activity | Predecessor(s) | $t_o$ | $t_m$ | $t_p$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | A | — | 2 | 5 | 8 | | B | — | 3 | 6 | 9 | | C | A | 4 | 7 | 10 | | D | B | 1 | 4 | 7 | | E | C, D | 3 | 5 | 13 | | F | E | 2 | 5 | 8 | | G | F | 1 | 3 | 5 | ### Step 1: Compute Expected Time and Variance for Each Activity For Activity A: $$t_e(A) = \dfrac{2 + 4(5) + 8}{6} = \dfrac{2 + 20 + 8}{6} = \dfrac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ days}$$ $$\sigma^2(A) = \left(\dfrac{8 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{6}{6}\right)^2 = 1 \text{ day}^2$$ For Activity B: $$t_e(B) = \dfrac{3 + 4(6) + 9}{6} = \dfrac{3 + 24 + 9}{6} = \dfrac{36}{6} = 6 \text{ days}$$ $$\sigma^2(B) = \left(\dfrac{9 - 3}{6}\right)^2 = 1 \text{ day}^2$$ For Activity C: $$t_e(C) = \dfrac{4 + 4(7) + 10}{6} = \dfrac{4 + 28 + 10}{6} = \dfrac{42}{6} = 7 \text{ days}$$ $$\sigma^2(C) = \left(\dfrac{10 - 4}{6}\right)^2 = 1 \text{ day}^2$$ For Activity D: $$t_e(D) = \dfrac{1 + 4(4) + 7}{6} = \dfrac{1 + 16 + 7}{6} = \dfrac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days}$$ $$\sigma^2(D) = \left(\dfrac{7 - 1}{6}\right)^2 = 1 \text{ day}^2$$ For Activity E: $$t_e(E) = \dfrac{3 + 4(5) + 13}{6} = \dfrac{3 + 20 + 13}{6} = \dfrac{36}{6} = 6 \text{ days}$$ $$\sigma^2(E) = \left(\dfrac{13 - 3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{10}{6}\right)^2 = \dfrac{100}{36} = 2.78 \text{ day}^2$$ For Activity F: $$t_e(F) = \dfrac{2 + 4(5) + 8}{6} = \dfrac{2 + 20 + 8}{6} = 5 \text{ days}$$ $$\sigma^2(F) = \left(\dfrac{8 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = 1 \text{ day}^2$$ For Activity G: $$t_e(G) = \dfrac{1 + 4(3) + 5}{6} = \dfrac{1 + 12 + 5}{6} = 3 \text{ days}$$ $$\sigma^2(G) = \left(\dfrac{5 - 1}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{4}{6}\right)^2 = \dfrac{16}{36} = 0.44 \text{ day}^2$$ ### Step 2: Compute Earliest Event Times (Forward Pass) The network has the following event numbering: - Event 1: Start (before A and B) - Event 2: After A - Event 3: After B - Event 4: After C and D (merge node) - Event 5: After E - Event 6: After F - Event 7: After G (end) | Event | Calculation | $E_j$ | |---|---|---| | $E_1$ | Initial | 0 | | $E_2$ | $E_1 + t_e(A) = 0 + 5$ | 5 | | $E_3$ | $E_1 + t_e(B) = 0 + 6$ | 6 | | $E_4$ | $\max\{E_2 + t_e(C),\ E_3 + t_e(D)\} = \max\{5+7,\ 6+4\} = \max\{12, 10\}$ | 12 | | $E_5$ | $E_4 + t_e(E) = 12 + 6$ | 18 | | $E_6$ | $E_5 + t_e(F) = 18 + 5$ | 23 | | $E_7$ | $E_6 + t_e(G) = 23 + 3$ | 26 | Therefore, **$T_E = 26$ days**. ### Step 3: Identify All Paths and the Critical Path | Path | Activities | Sum of $t_e$ | Duration | |---|---|---|---| | 1 → 2 → 4 → 5 → 6 → 7 | A → C → E → F → G | 5+7+6+5+3 | **26** | | 1 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 6 → 7 | B → D → E → F → G | 6+4+6+5+3 | 24 | **Critical Path:** 1 → 2 → 4 → 5 → 6 → 7 (Path A-C-E-F-G) with duration **26 days**. ### Step 4: Compute Project Variance Only activities on the critical path contribute: $$\sigma_{CP}^2 = \sigma^2(A) + \sigma^2(C) + \sigma^2(E) + \sigma^2(F) + \sigma^2(G)$$ $$\sigma_{CP}^2 = 1 + 1 + 2.78 + 1 + 0.44 = 6.22 \text{ day}^2$$ $$\sigma_{CP} = \sqrt{6.22} \approx 2.494 \text{ days}$$ ### Step 5: Probability of Completion Before a Scheduled Date Suppose the project must be completed in **$T_s = 28$ days**. $$Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_{CP}} = \dfrac{28 - 26}{2.494} = \dfrac{2}{2.494} \approx 0.802$$ From the Standard Normal Table: $$P(Z \leq 0.80) \approx 0.7881$$ Therefore, **probability of completing within 28 days ≈ 78.81%**. ### Step 6: Find the Scheduled Time for a Target Probability Suppose management wants **90% probability** of completion. From the Z-table, $P(Z) = 0.90 \Rightarrow Z = 1.28$. $$T_s = T_E + Z \cdot \sigma_{CP} = 26 + 1.28 \times 2.494$$ $$T_s = 26 + 3.192 \approx 29.19 \text{ days}$$ So management should promise **approximately 29.2 days** to achieve 90% confidence. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 PERT Network Block Diagram (Activity-on-Node Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR startNode([Event 1: START]) endNode([Event 7: END]) A[Activity A: te=5] B[Activity B: te=6] C[Activity C: te=7] D[Activity D: te=4] E[Activity E: te=6] F[Activity F: te=5] G[Activity G: te=3] startNode --> A startNode --> B A --> C B --> D C --> E D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> endNode classDef critical fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef noncritical fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#888,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class A,C,E,F,G critical class B,D noncritical ``` > [!NOTE] > **Gold-highlighted path** = Critical Path (A → C → E → F → G). The grey path (B → D) has slack and can be delayed without delaying the project. ## 4.2 PERT Analytical Workflow — Process Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD input1[Step 1: Identify Project Activities] input2[Step 2: Establish Precedence Relationships] input3[Step 3: Draw Network Diagram] est[Step 4: Obtain Three Time Estimates
to, tm, tp from Experts] calc1[Step 5: Compute Expected Time
te = to + 4tm + tp divided by 6] calc2[Step 6: Compute Variance
sigma squared equals tp minus to divided by 6 squared] forward[Step 7: Forward Pass
Compute Earliest Event Times Ej] backward[Step 8: Backward Pass
Compute Latest Event Times Lj] crit[Step 9: Identify Critical Path
Activities with zero slack] prob[Step 10: Compute Project Variance
Sum variances on Critical Path] zval[Step 11: Compute Z value
Z equals Ts minus TE divided by sigma CP] final[Step 12: Read Probability from
Standard Normal Distribution Table] decision{Probability Acceptable?} yes[Accept Schedule] no[Adjust Schedule or Add Resources] input1 --> input2 --> input3 --> est est --> calc1 est --> calc2 calc1 --> forward calc2 --> prob forward --> backward backward --> crit crit --> prob prob --> zval zval --> final final --> decision decision -->|Yes| yes decision -->|No| no ``` ## 4.3 Slack Calculation Visualization ```mermaid graph TB subgraph CriticalChain["CRITICAL PATH PATH 1"] direction LR P1A[Activity A] P1C[Activity C] P1E[Activity E] P1F[Activity F] P1G[Activity G] end subgraph NonCritical["NON-CRITICAL PATH 2"] direction LR P2B[Activity B] P2D[Activity D] end P1A -.joins to.-> P1E P2B -.joins to.-> P2D -.joins to.-> P1E style P1A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style P1C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style P1E fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style P1F fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style P1G fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style P2B fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#666 style P2D fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#666 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define PERT. List the three time estimates used in PERT analysis.** **Model Answer:** PERT (Programme Evaluation Review Technique) is a probabilistic project scheduling technique used to plan, coordinate, and control complex projects involving uncertainty in activity durations. It was developed in **1958** for the US Navy's Polaris missile program. The three time estimates are: 1. **Optimistic Time ($t_o$):** The minimum time required to complete the activity under ideal conditions. 2. **Most Likely Time ($t_m$):** The time required under normal/representative working conditions (corresponds to the mode of the Beta distribution). 3. **Pessimistic Time ($t_p$):** The maximum time required under worst-case conditions. These three estimates are combined to compute the expected time $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ and variance $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between PERT and CPM.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | PERT | CPM | |---|---|---| | Activity Duration | Three estimates, probabilistic | Single estimate, deterministic | | Distribution Used | Beta distribution | None | | Focus | Time scheduling under uncertainty | Time-cost trade-off | | Variance | Calculated explicitly | Not considered | | Suitable For | R&D, defense, research projects | Construction, repetitive projects | | Time-Cost Optimization | Not typically done | Crashing is a key feature | | Origin | 1958, US Navy Polaris | 1957, DuPont and Remington Rand | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks) A project has the following activities, time estimates (in weeks), and dependencies: | Activity | Predecessors | $t_o$ | $t_m$ | $t_p$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | A | — | 3 | 6 | 9 | | B | — | 2 | 5 | 8 | | C | A | 4 | 7 | 16 | | D | B | 1 | 4 | 7 | | E | C | 3 | 5 | 7 | | F | D | 2 | 5 | 8 | | G | E, F | 5 | 8 | 17 | #### (a) Draw the network, compute the expected time and variance for each activity, and determine the critical path along with expected project duration. **[7 Marks]** **Solution:** **Step 1: Compute $t_e$ and $\sigma^2$ for each activity:** For Activity A: $$t_e(A) = \dfrac{3 + 4(6) + 9}{6} = \dfrac{36}{6} = 6 \text{ weeks}$$ $$\sigma^2(A) = \left(\dfrac{9 - 3}{6}\right)^2 = 1$$ For Activity B: $$t_e(B) = \dfrac{2 + 4(5) + 8}{6} = \dfrac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ weeks}$$ $$\sigma^2(B) = \left(\dfrac{8 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = 1$$ For Activity C: $$t_e(C) = \dfrac{4 + 4(7) + 16}{6} = \dfrac{48}{6} = 8 \text{ weeks}$$ $$\sigma^2(C) = \left(\dfrac{16 - 4}{6}\right)^2 = 4$$ For Activity D: $$t_e(D) = \dfrac{1 + 4(4) + 7}{6} = \dfrac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ weeks}$$ $$\sigma^2(D) = \left(\dfrac{7 - 1}{6}\right)^2 = 1$$ For Activity E: $$t_e(E) = \dfrac{3 + 4(5) + 7}{6} = \dfrac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ weeks}$$ $$\sigma^2(E) = \left(\dfrac{7 - 3}{6}\right)^2 = \dfrac{4}{9} = 0.444$$ For Activity F: $$t_e(F) = \dfrac{2 + 4(5) + 8}{6} = \dfrac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ weeks}$$ $$\sigma^2(F) = \left(\dfrac{8 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = 1$$ For Activity G: $$t_e(G) = \dfrac{5 + 4(8) + 17}{6} = \dfrac{54}{6} = 9 \text{ weeks}$$ $$\sigma^2(G) = \left(\dfrac{17 - 5}{6}\right)^2 = 4$$ **Step 2: Network and Forward Pass** Network structure: - Start → A → C → E → G → End - Start → B → D → F → G → End - E and F merge at G. Earliest event times: - $E_1 = 0$ - $E_2 = E_1 + t_e(A) = 0 + 6 = 6$ - $E_3 = E_1 + t_e(B) = 0 + 5 = 5$ - $E_4 = E_2 + t_e(C) = 6 + 8 = 14$ - $E_5 = E_3 + t_e(D) = 5 + 4 = 9$ - $E_6 = \max\{E_4 + t_e(E), E_5 + t_e(F)\} = \max\{14+5, 9+5\} = \max\{19, 14\} = 19$ - $E_7 = E_6 + t_e(G) = 19 + 9 = 28$ **Step 3: Identify Paths** | Path | Sum of $t_e$ | |---|---| | A → C → E → G | 6 + 8 + 5 + 9 = 28 | | B → D → F → G | 5 + 4 + 5 + 9 = 23 | **Critical Path:** A → C → E → G with **$T_E = 28$ weeks**. **Project Variance:** $$\sigma_{CP}^2 = 1 + 4 + 0.444 + 4 = 9.444$$ --- #### (b) Find the probability of completing the project in (i) 30 weeks, and (ii) within what time should the project be completed to have a 95% confidence? **[7 Marks]** **Solution:** We have $T_E = 28$ weeks, $\sigma_{CP}^2 = 9.444$, so $\sigma_{CP} = \sqrt{9.444} \approx 3.073$ weeks. **(i) Probability of completion within 30 weeks:** $$Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_{CP}} = \dfrac{30 - 28}{3.073} = \dfrac{2}{3.073} \approx 0.651$$ From standard normal table: $P(Z \leq 0.65) \approx 0.7422$. **Probability ≈ 74.22%**. **(ii) Time for 95% confidence:** For 95% probability, $Z = 1.645$. $$T_s = T_E + Z \cdot \sigma_{CP} = 28 + 1.645 \times 3.073$$ $$T_s = 28 + 5.055 = 33.055 \approx 33.06 \text{ weeks}$$ **The project should be scheduled for 33.06 weeks for 95% confidence.** --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) A construction project has the following network data: | Activity | $t_o$ | $t_m$ | $t_p$ | Follower | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1-2 | 3 | 6 | 9 | 3, 4 | | 2-3 | 2 | 5 | 8 | 5 | | 2-4 | 4 | 7 | 10 | 5 | | 3-5 | 1 | 4 | 7 | 6 | | 4-5 | 2 | 5 | 14 | 6 | | 5-6 | 3 | 5 | 7 | End | #### (a) Determine the expected time and variance for each activity. Identify the critical path and expected project duration. **[7 Marks]** **Solution:** **Computing $t_e$ and $\sigma^2$:** Activity 1-2: $$t_e(1-2) = \dfrac{3 + 4(6) + 9}{6} = 6 \text{ days}, \quad \sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{6}{6}\right)^2 = 1$$ Activity 2-3: $$t_e(2-3) = \dfrac{2 + 4(5) + 8}{6} = 5 \text{ days}, \quad \sigma^2 = 1$$ Activity 2-4: $$t_e(2-4) = \dfrac{4 + 4(7) + 10}{6} = 7 \text{ days}, \quad \sigma^2 = 1$$ Activity 3-5: $$t_e(3-5) = \dfrac{1 + 4(4) + 7}{6} = 4 \text{ days}, \quad \sigma^2 = 1$$ Activity 4-5: $$t_e(4-5) = \dfrac{2 + 4(5) + 14}{6} = 6 \text{ days}, \quad \sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{12}{6}\right)^2 = 4$$ Activity 5-6: $$t_e(5-6) = \dfrac{3 + 4(5) + 7}{6} = 5 \text{ days}, \quad \sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{4}{6}\right)^2 = 0.444$$ **Forward Pass:** $E_1 = 0$ $E_2 = E_1 + t_e(1-2) = 6$ $E_3 = E_2 + t_e(2-3) = 6 + 5 = 11$ $E_4 = E_2 + t_e(2-4) = 6 + 7 = 13$ $E_5 = \max\{E_3 + t_e(3-5), E_4 + t_e(4-5)\} = \max\{11+4, 13+6\} = \max\{15, 19\} = 19$ $E_6 = E_5 + t_e(5-6) = 19 + 5 = 24$ **Paths:** | Path | Duration | |---|---| | 1-2-3-5-6 | 6+5+4+5 = 20 | | 1-2-4-5-6 | 6+7+6+5 = **24** | **Critical Path: 1-2-4-5-6**, $T_E = 24$ days. **Project Variance:** $$\sigma_{CP}^2 = 1 + 1 + 4 + 0.444 = 6.444$$ $$\sigma_{CP} = 2.539$$ --- #### (b) What is the probability of completing the project (i) in 26 days, and (ii) within what duration will the project be completed with 80% probability? **[7 Marks]** **Solution:** **(i) For $T_s = 26$ days:** $$Z = \dfrac{26 - 24}{2.539} = \dfrac{2}{2.539} \approx 0.788$$ $P(Z \leq 0.79) \approx 0.7852$. **Probability ≈ 78.52%**. **(ii) For 80% probability:** $Z = 0.842$ (from tables). $$T_s = 24 + 0.842 \times 2.539 = 24 + 2.138 = 26.14 \text{ days}$$ **Approximately 26.14 days for 80% confidence.** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Variance on Wrong Path:** Students often sum variances across ALL activities. **Only activities on the critical path** contribute to project variance. (Deducts 2 marks) > 2. **Using Wrong $Z$ Table:** For "completion within $T_s$", use $P(Z \leq z)$. For "completion beyond $T_s$", use $1 - P(Z \leq z)$. Many students confuse the two. (Deducts 1 mark) > 3. **Forgetting the Forward Pass:** Some students directly guess the critical path by inspection. Always perform a forward pass and list ALL paths with their total durations. (Deducts 2 marks) > 4. **Rounding Errors in $Z$:** Round $Z$ to two decimal places before looking up the table. Premature rounding leads to wrong probability values. (Deducts 0.5–1 mark) > 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** If $t_o$ is in weeks and $t_p$ in days, normalize first. KTU strictly checks units. (Deducts 1 mark) > 6. **Skipping Network Drawing:** Even in computation problems, KTU expects a labeled network diagram. Always include one. (Deducts 1 mark) --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Rapid Revision Checklist - **Definition:** PERT = Project scheduling under **uncertainty**, using **Beta distribution** and **three time estimates**. - **Origin:** 1958, US Navy Polaris program, Booz Allen Hamilton. - **Three Estimates:** $t_o$ (optimistic), $t_m$ (most likely), $t_p$ (pessimistic). - **Expected Time:** $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ — weight ratio is **1 : 4 : 1**. - **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$. - **Critical Path:** The path with the **largest sum of $t_e$**. - **Project Variance:** Sum of variances **only on critical path** activities. - **Project SD:** $\sigma_{CP} = \sqrt{\sigma_{CP}^2}$. - **Z-Formula:** $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_{CP}}$. - **Probability:** Read from the **Standard Normal Distribution Table**. - **CPM vs PERT:** CPM = deterministic, single estimate. PERT = probabilistic, three estimates. - **Network:** Use **Forward Pass** ($E_j = \max$) and **Backward Pass** ($L_i = \min$). - **Slack:** $S = L_j - E_j$. Critical events have **zero slack**. - **Crashing:** NOT used in PERT (only in CPM). - **Key Assumption:** Activity durations are **statistically independent** random variables. ### Must-Remember Numerical Anchors | $Z$ Value | $P(Z)$ | |---|---| | 0.00 | 0.5000 | | 1.00 | 0.8413 | | 1.28 | 0.8997 | | 1.645 | 0.9500 | | 2.00 | 0.9772 | | 2.33 | 0.9901 | | 3.00 | 0.9987 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always present your solution in this order: (1) Table of $t_e$ and $\sigma^2$, (2) Network diagram, (3) Forward/Backward pass, (4) Critical path, (5) Probability calculation. Examiners award **methodology marks** even if the final numerical answer has a small rounding error.

topics.title: Decision Theory: Steps in Decision Theory Approach and Decision Making Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT746 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Decision Theory: Steps in Decision Theory Approach and Decision Making Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Decision Theory** is a mathematical and analytical framework within Operations Research that provides a systematic, logical, and quantitative methodology for selecting the most favorable course of action from a set of available alternatives, given specific objectives and varying states of nature (or environment conditions). It structures complex, multi-criteria choices into a model that can be evaluated using probabilistic and statistical reasoning.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Decision Theory is a *prescriptive* (normative) tool — it tells the decision-maker *what they should do* to maximize expected value or minimize expected loss, based on the information available about the future.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Standard)**
> - **Decision Maker (DM):** The individual or entity responsible for selecting the action.
> - **Act / Alternative ($a_i$):** A controllable choice available to the DM.
> - **State of Nature ($s_j$):** An uncontrollable external condition that can occur.
> - **Payoff ($v_{ij}$):** The outcome (profit, cost, utility) resulting from pairing an act with a state.
> - **Payoff Matrix:** A tabular representation of payoffs for every (act, state) combination.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are planning an **outdoor evening party in Kerala during monsoon season**. You have two alternatives:
- *Act 1:* Book an open-air terrace venue (cheaper, scenic).
- *Act 2:* Book an enclosed banquet hall (costlier, safer).

The state of nature is the **weather** — sunny, light rain, or heavy rain. Your payoff (guest satisfaction minus cost) changes dramatically based on what nature delivers. Decision Theory is like a *strategic dashboard* that helps you weigh these combinations, account for the probability of rain, and pick the venue that statistically serves you best over many such events.

## 1.3 The Four Conditions of Decision Making

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus specifically categorizes decision-making environments into **four** conditions, classified by the DM's *knowledge* of the states of nature.

| Condition | Description | Knowledge Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Certainty** | The outcome of each alternative is known with 100% confidence. | Perfect information about the future |
| **Risk** | Probabilities of each state of nature are known (objective or subjective). | Probabilistic knowledge |
| **Uncertainty** | Probabilities are unknown; choices must be made under ignorance. | No probabilistic knowledge |
| **Conflict** | Outcomes depend on the actions of competing rational opponents. | Adversarial / Game-theoretic |

The physical constants or boundary assumptions in Decision Theory are the **axioms of rational choice** — completeness, transitivity, and independence — which are assumed valid for all analyses.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Standard Steps of Decision Theory Approach

The decision theory methodology is a **closed-loop, structured procedure** that moves from problem definition to implementation, and crucially, back to monitoring.

### Step 1: Define the Problem and Objectives
- Clearly articulate the decision to be made.
- Identify the *goal* (maximize profit, minimize cost, maximize utility, minimize regret).
- Specify the time horizon and scope.

### Step 2: Identify the Decision Alternatives (Acts)
- List all feasible courses of action: $A = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_m\}$.
- Mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive.

### Step 3: Identify the States of Nature
- Enumerate all possible future events: $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$.
- These are *uncontrollable* by the DM.

### Step 4: Construct the Payoff Matrix
- For each $(a_i, s_j)$ pair, determine the payoff $v_{ij}$.
- May be a **Profit Matrix** (maximization) or a **Cost/Loss Matrix** (minimization).

### Step 5: Apply the Appropriate Decision Criterion
- Choose the criterion based on the **condition** (certainty, risk, uncertainty).
- Compute Expected Monetary Value (EMV), Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL), or apply a criterion under uncertainty (Maximax, Maximin, etc.).

### Step 6: Perform Sensitivity Analysis and Implement
- Test how robust the decision is to changes in probabilities or payoffs.
- Implement the chosen alternative.
- Monitor outcomes and feed information back into Step 1 (closed loop).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Decision Theory underwrites capital budgeting in finance, route selection in civil engineering, inventory policy in supply chains, and reliability-based maintenance in mechanical systems.

## 2.2 Decision Criteria Under Different Conditions

### A. Decision Making Under Certainty
The DM knows exactly which state of nature will occur. The rule is trivial:

$$\text{Choose } a^* = \begin{cases} \arg\max_i \, v_{ij} & \text{if profit matrix} \\ \arg\min_i \, v_{ij} & \text{if cost matrix} \end{cases}$$

### B. Decision Making Under Risk (with known probabilities $p_j$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Expected Monetary Value (EMV):**
> $$\text{EMV}(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} v_{ij} \cdot p_j$$
> Choose the act with the **maximum EMV** (profit case) or **minimum EMV** (cost case).

**Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL):** First build a *Regret Table* by computing opportunity loss:
$$r_{ij} = \begin{cases} \max_k v_{kj} - v_{ij} & \text{(profit)} \\ v_{ij} - \min_k v_{kj} & \text{(cost)} \end{cases}$$

Then:
$$\text{EOL}(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} r_{ij} \cdot p_j$$
Choose the act with **minimum EOL**.

> **Fundamental Equivalence Theorem (KTU Favorite):**
> $$\min_i \text{EOL}(a_i) = \max_i \text{EMV}(a_i) - \text{EV}_{\text{with PI}}$$

### C. Decision Making Under Uncertainty (no probabilities)

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Classical Criteria:**

1. **Maximax (Optimistic):** Pick the best of the best.
$$\text{Choose } a^* : \max_i \left[ \max_j v_{ij} \right]$$

2. **Maximin (Pessimistic):** Maximize the minimum possible payoff.
$$\text{Choose } a^* : \max_i \left[ \min_j v_{ij} \right]$$

3. **Minimax Regret (Savage):** Minimize the maximum opportunity loss.
$$\text{Choose } a^* : \min_i \left[ \max_j r_{ij} \right]$$

4. **Laplace (Principle of Insufficient Reason):** Assume equal probabilities $\frac{1}{n}$.
$$\text{Choose } a^* : \max_i \left[ \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} v_{ij} \right]$$

5. **Hurwicz (Coefficient of Optimism $\alpha \in [0,1]$):**
$$H(a_i) = \alpha \cdot \max_j v_{ij} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \min_j v_{ij}$$
$$\text{Choose } a^* : \max_i H(a_i)$$

6. **Minimax (for Cost Matrices):**
$$\text{Choose } a^* : \min_i \left[ \max_j v_{ij} \right]$$

### D. Decision Making Under Conflict (Game Theory)
At least **two rational opponents** with **conflicting objectives** make simultaneous or sequential decisions. Solution concepts include:
- **Saddle Point / Pure Strategy**
- **Mixed Strategy** (when no saddle point exists)
- **Dominance Principle**
- **2×2 Game Algebraic Solution**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | EMV | $\text{EMV}(a_i) = \sum v_{ij} p_j$ | Risk |
| 2 | EOL | $\text{EOL}(a_i) = \sum r_{ij} p_j$ | Risk |
| 3 | Opportunity Loss | $r_{ij} = \max_k v_{kj} - v_{ij}$ | Profit matrix |
| 4 | Maximax | $\max_i \max_j v_{ij}$ | Uncertainty |
| 5 | Maximin | $\max_i \min_j v_{ij}$ | Uncertainty |
| 6 | Minimax Regret | $\min_i \max_j r_{ij}$ | Uncertainty |
| 7 | Laplace | $\max_i \frac{1}{n}\sum v_{ij}$ | Uncertainty |
| 8 | Hurwicz | $H_i = \alpha \max v_{ij} + (1-\alpha)\min v_{ij}$ | Uncertainty |
| 9 | Expected Value of Perfect Information | $\text{EVPI} = \text{Best EMV with PI} - \text{Best EMV without PI}$ | Risk |
| 10 | Certainty Equivalent | $\min \text{EOL} = \text{EMV}^* - \text{EVPI}$ | Risk |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence Between Minimizing EOL and Maximizing EMV

**Starting Point:** Let $v_{ij}$ be profit payoff, and define opportunity loss as:
$$r_{ij} = \max_k v_{kj} - v_{ij}$$

Summing over states with probabilities $p_j$:
$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} r_{ij} p_j = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \left( \max_k v_{kj} \right) p_j - \sum_{j=1}^{n} v_{ij} p_j$$

Let $M = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \left( \max_k v_{kj} \right) p_j$ — this is the **Expected Value with Perfect Information (EVwPI)**, a constant independent of $i$.

Thus:
$$\text{EOL}(a_i) = M - \text{EMV}(a_i)$$

Minimizing EOL is therefore equivalent to maximizing EMV. Rearranging:

$$\min_i \text{EOL}(a_i) = M - \max_i \text{EMV}(a_i)$$

$$\therefore \max_i \text{EMV}(a_i) - \min_i \text{EOL}(a_i) = M = \text{EVwPI}$$

And the **Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI):**

$$\text{EVPI} = \text{EVwPI} - \max_i \text{EMV}(a_i) = \min_i \text{EOL}(a_i)$$

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A company must choose between three products $A_1, A_2, A_3$ to launch. Market demand may be High ($H$), Medium ($M$), or Low ($L$) with probabilities $p(H) = 0.3, p(M) = 0.5, p(L) = 0.2$. Profit matrix (₹ lakhs):

| | $H$ (0.3) | $M$ (0.5) | $L$ (0.2) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A_1$ | 200 | 150 | 100 |
| $A_2$ | 250 | 180 | 80 |
| $A_3$ | 175 | 160 | 120 |

**Step 1: Compute EMV**

$$\text{EMV}(A_1) = 200(0.3) + 150(0.5) + 100(0.2) = 60 + 75 + 20 = 155$$

$$\text{EMV}(A_2) = 250(0.3) + 180(0.5) + 80(0.2) = 75 + 90 + 16 = 181$$

$$\text{EMV}(A_3) = 175(0.3) + 160(0.5) + 120(0.2) = 52.5 + 80 + 24 = 156.5$$

**Decision (Risk / EMV Criterion):** $\max(155, 181, 156.5) = 181 \Rightarrow \text{Choose } A_2$.

**Step 2: Build the Regret Table**

Column maximums: $\max(H) = 250$, $\max(M) = 180$, $\max(L) = 120$.

$$r_{ij} = \max_k v_{kj} - v_{ij}$$

| | $H$ | $M$ | $L$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A_1$ | 50 | 30 | 20 |
| $A_2$ | 0 | 0 | 40 |
| $A_3$ | 75 | 20 | 0 |

**Step 3: Compute EOL**

$$\text{EOL}(A_1) = 50(0.3) + 30(0.5) + 20(0.2) = 15 + 15 + 4 = 34$$

$$\text{EOL}(A_2) = 0(0.3) + 0(0.5) + 40(0.2) = 8$$

$$\text{EOL}(A_3) = 75(0.3) + 20(0.5) + 0(0.2) = 22.5 + 10 + 0 = 32.5$$

**Decision (Risk / EOL Criterion):** $\min(34, 8, 32.5) = 8 \Rightarrow \text{Choose } A_2$. ✓ Consistent.

**Step 4: Compute EVPI**

$$\text{EVwPI} = 250(0.3) + 180(0.5) + 120(0.2) = 75 + 90 + 24 = 189$$

$$\text{EVPI} = 189 - 181 = 8 \equiv \min \text{EOL} = 8 \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Decision Criterion

| Decision Condition | Information Available | Recommended Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| Certainty | Exact state known | Pick best payoff for that state |
| Risk | $p_j$ known | EMV / EOL / EVPI analysis |
| Uncertainty — Optimistic DM | None | Maximax |
| Uncertainty — Pessimistic DM | None | Maximin |
| Uncertainty — Regret-averse DM | None | Minimax Regret |
| Uncertainty — Neutral DM | None | Laplace |
| Uncertainty — Mixed attitude ($\alpha$) | None | Hurwicz |
| Conflict | Opponent rationality | Saddle / Dominance / Mixed Strategy |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Steps of Decision Theory Approach

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Step 1: Define Problem and Objective] --> stepB[Step 2: Identify Alternatives A equals a1 a2 to am]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Identify States of Nature S equals s1 s2 to sn]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Construct Payoff Matrix vij]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Select Condition and Apply Criterion]
    stepE --> condA{Certainty}
    stepE --> condB{Risk}
    stepE --> condC{Uncertainty}
    stepE --> condD{Conflict}
    condA --> critA[Pick best payoff for known state]
    condB --> critB[Compute EMV and EOL Pick best]
    condC --> critC[Apply Maximax Maximin Laplace Hurwicz or Regret]
    condD --> critD[Apply Saddle Point Dominance or Mixed Strategy]
    critA --> stepF[Step 6: Sensitivity Analysis and Implement]
    critB --> stepF
    critC --> stepF
    critD --> stepF
    stepF --> loopA[Monitor outcomes and feed back to Step 1]
    loopA --> startA
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Decision-Making Conditions Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dtA[Decision Theory Framework] --> dtB[Knowledge About States of Nature]
    dtB --> dtC[Perfect Knowledge]
    dtB --> dtD[Probabilistic Knowledge]
    dtB --> dtE[No Knowledge Ignorance]
    dtB --> dtF[Adversarial Knowledge]
    dtC --> cerA[Certainty Pick the best for known state]
    dtD --> riskA[Risk Use EMV EOL and EVPI]
    dtE --> uncA[Uncertainty Apply Maximax Maximin Laplace Hurwicz or Minimax Regret]
    dtF --> conA[Conflict Use Saddle Point or Game Theory Methods]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Information Value Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    v0[No Information] --> v1[Sample Market Data]
    v1 --> v2[Estimate Probabilities pj]
    v2 --> v3[Compute EMV of each act]
    v3 --> v4[Best EMV without Perfect Info]
    v2 --> v5[Compute EV with Perfect Info]
    v5 --> v6[EVwPI = sum max vkj pj]
    v4 --> v7[EVPI = EVwPI minus Best EMV]
    v6 --> v7
    v7 --> v8[Maximum Price to Pay for Research]
```

## 4.4 Decision-Criterion Selection Matrix (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    qstart[DM Attitude and Information] --> q1{Is probability known?}
    q1 -->|Yes| qrisk[Use EMV or EOL Criterion]
    q1 -->|No| q2{Is DM Optimistic?}
    q2 -->|Yes| qmaximax[Use Maximax]
    q2 -->|No| q3{Is DM Pessimistic?}
    q3 -->|Yes| qmaximin[Use Maximin]
    q3 -->|No| q4{Does DM want to minimize regret?}
    q4 -->|Yes| qregret[Use Minimax Regret]
    q4 -->|No| q5{Does DM believe in equal likelihood?}
    q5 -->|Yes| qlaplace[Use Laplace]
    q5 -->|No| qhurwicz[Use Hurwicz with alpha]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define Decision Theory. List the four conditions under which decisions are made.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Decision Theory is a quantitative framework in Operations Research that provides a systematic procedure for selecting the best course of action from a set of alternatives under varying states of nature, in order to optimize a chosen objective (profit, cost, or utility).
- **Four Conditions (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
  1. **Decision Making Under Certainty** — the state of nature is known with certainty.
  2. **Decision Making Under Risk** — probabilities of states of nature are known.
  3. **Decision Making Under Uncertainty** — no probabilistic information is available.
  4. **Decision Making Under Conflict** — outcome depends on the rational action of an opponent (game situation).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**State and explain the Laplace criterion for decision making under uncertainty.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Statement (2 marks):** The **Laplace criterion** (also called the *Principle of Insufficient Reason*) assumes that since no information is available about the relative likelihood of the states of nature, all states are assumed to be **equally probable**, i.e., $p_j = \frac{1}{n}$ for all $j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$.
- **Decision Rule (1 mark):** The DM selects the alternative with the maximum average payoff:
$$\text{Choose } a^* : \max_i \left[ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} v_{ij} \right]$$

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **six steps of the Decision Theory approach**. Discuss how the criterion for decision-making is selected based on the operating condition. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A garment company must decide whether to launch a *premium*, *standard*, or *budget* product line. Market response may be *Excellent*, *Average*, or *Poor*, with probabilities 0.4, 0.4, and 0.2 respectively. The estimated profits (₹ lakhs) are tabulated below. Determine the optimal decision using **(i) EMV criterion** and **(ii) EOL criterion**. Also compute the **EVPI**. **(7 Marks)**

| Decision | Excellent (0.4) | Average (0.4) | Poor (0.2) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Premium | 80 | 40 | 10 |
| Standard | 60 | 50 | 30 |
| Budget | 30 | 30 | 25 |

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

1. **Step 1 – Define Problem & Objective:** Identify whether to maximize profit or minimize cost. (1 mark)
2. **Step 2 – Identify Alternatives:** Enumerate all feasible actions $A = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_m\}$. (1 mark)
3. **Step 3 – Identify States of Nature:** $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$. (1 mark)
4. **Step 4 – Construct Payoff Matrix:** $v_{ij}$ for every $(a_i, s_j)$ pair. (1 mark)
5. **Step 5 – Choose Decision Criterion Based on Condition:**
   - Certainty → Direct selection
   - Risk → EMV / EOL
   - Uncertainty → Maximax / Maximin / Laplace / Hurwicz / Minimax Regret
   - Conflict → Game theory
   (2 marks)
6. **Step 6 – Sensitivity Analysis & Implementation:** Check robustness, then implement. (1 mark)

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) EMV Computation:**

$$\text{EMV}(\text{Premium}) = 80(0.4) + 40(0.4) + 10(0.2) = 32 + 16 + 2 = 50$$

$$\text{EMV}(\text{Standard}) = 60(0.4) + 50(0.4) + 30(0.2) = 24 + 20 + 6 = 50$$

$$\text{EMV}(\text{Budget}) = 30(0.4) + 30(0.4) + 25(0.2) = 12 + 12 + 5 = 29$$

**EMV Decision:** Both Premium and Standard tie at ₹50 lakhs. (2 marks)

**(ii) Regret Table:** Column maximums: Excellent = 80, Average = 50, Poor = 30.

| Decision | Excellent | Average | Poor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Premium | 0 | 10 | 20 |
| Standard | 20 | 0 | 0 |
| Budget | 50 | 20 | 5 |

**EOL Computation:**

$$\text{EOL}(\text{Premium}) = 0(0.4) + 10(0.4) + 20(0.2) = 0 + 4 + 4 = 8$$

$$\text{EOL}(\text{Standard}) = 20(0.4) + 0(0.4) + 0(0.2) = 8$$

$$\text{EOL}(\text{Budget}) = 50(0.4) + 20(0.4) + 5(0.2) = 20 + 8 + 1 = 29$$

**EOL Decision:** Both Premium and Standard tie at 8 (minimum). (2 marks)

**EVPI Computation:**

$$\text{EVwPI} = 80(0.4) + 50(0.4) + 30(0.2) = 32 + 20 + 6 = 58$$

$$\text{EVPI} = 58 - 50 = 8 \equiv \min \text{EOL} = 8 \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** Both **Premium** and **Standard** are equally optimal; Standard is preferred for lower downside risk (poor state gives ₹30 vs. ₹10). **Maximum price for research = ₹8 lakhs.** (1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Define the following criteria for decision making under uncertainty with formulas: **Maximax, Maximin, Minimax Regret, Hurwicz**. (Use $\alpha = 0.6$ if needed.) **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A startup can choose between three investment strategies $S_1, S_2, S_3$ under three economic scenarios: Boom, Normal, Recession. The payoff (₹ lakhs) matrix is:

| Strategy | Boom | Normal | Recession |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ | 50 | 30 | 10 |
| $S_2$ | 40 | 35 | 20 |
| $S_3$ | 30 | 25 | 15 |

Apply all four criteria from part (a) and recommend the best strategy. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

1. **Maximax (Optimistic) (2 marks):**
$$\text{Choose } a^* = \max_i \max_j v_{ij}$$
$$v_{\max} = \{\max(50,30,10)=50,\; \max(40,35,20)=40,\; \max(30,25,15)=30\}$$
$$\text{Answer} = \max(50,40,30) = 50 \Rightarrow S_1$$

2. **Maximin (Pessimistic) (2 marks):**
$$\text{Choose } a^* = \max_i \min_j v_{ij}$$
$$v_{\min} = \{\min(50,30,10)=10,\; \min(40,35,20)=20,\; \min(30,25,15)=15\}$$
$$\text{Answer} = \max(10,20,15) = 20 \Rightarrow S_2$$

3. **Minimax Regret (Savage) (2 marks):**
Column max: Boom=50, Normal=35, Recession=20. Compute $r_{ij} = \max_k v_{kj} - v_{ij}$:

| | Boom | Normal | Recession | Max Regret |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ | 0 | 5 | 10 | 10 |
| $S_2$ | 10 | 0 | 0 | 10 |
| $S_3$ | 20 | 10 | 5 | 20 |

$$\text{Answer} = \min(10, 10, 20) = 10 \Rightarrow S_1 \text{ or } S_2$$

4. **Hurwicz ($\alpha = 0.6$) (1 mark):**
$$H(S_1) = 0.6(50) + 0.4(10) = 30 + 4 = 34$$
$$H(S_2) = 0.6(40) + 0.4(20) = 24 + 8 = 32$$
$$H(S_3) = 0.6(30) + 0.4(15) = 18 + 6 = 24$$
$$\text{Answer} = \max(34, 32, 24) = 34 \Rightarrow S_1$$

**Final Recommendation:** $S_1$ (Premium) wins under 3 of 4 criteria, hence the best strategy. (Conclusion 1 mark — accounted in distribution.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Mixing criteria:** Students often apply Maximax on a cost matrix or compute EMV with incorrect probability signs (use of $1-p$ instead of $p$).
> 2. **Regret table sign errors:** Forgetting to subtract *each* entry from the *column* maximum (not row maximum).
> 3. **No internal choice:** KTU 2024 scheme mandates an **internal choice** in Part B (typically 14-mark questions). Attempting only one option forfeits 7 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the formula statement:** Mere computation without writing the criterion's *definition and formula* attracts 1–2 mark deduction.
> 5. **In Sensitivity analysis:** When EMV is tied, the tie-breaker is the **maximin** (or minimum EOL) — students often miss this step.
> 6. **Hurwicz:** Confusing $\alpha$ between 0 and 1 — if not given, take $\alpha = 0.5$ (Laplace equivalent).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets**

- ✅ Decision Theory = selecting the *best act* under different *states of nature*.
- ✅ **4 conditions**: Certainty, Risk, Uncertainty, Conflict.
- ✅ **6 steps**: Define → Alternatives → States → Payoff Matrix → Criterion → Sensitivity.
- ✅ **EMV** = $\sum v_{ij} p_j$ → choose maximum (profit) or minimum (cost).
- ✅ **EOL** = $\sum r_{ij} p_j$ → choose minimum; $r_{ij} = \text{column max} - v_{ij}$.
- ✅ **EVPI** = $\text{EVwPI} - \text{Best EMV}$ = $\min \text{EOL}$ (verify both equal).
- ✅ **Maximax** = $\max_i \max_j v_{ij}$ → optimistic.
- ✅ **Maximin** = $\max_i \min_j v_{ij}$ → pessimistic.
- ✅ **Minimax Regret** = $\min_i \max_j r_{ij}$ → use *regret* table.
- ✅ **Laplace** = $\max_i \frac{1}{n}\sum v_{ij}$ → assume equal probabilities.
- ✅ **Hurwicz** = $\max_i [\alpha \max_j v_{ij} + (1-\alpha) \min_j v_{ij}]$, $\alpha \in [0,1]$.
- ✅ **Conflict** → Game theory: saddle point, dominance, or 2×2 algebraic solution.
- ✅ **Tie-Breaker Rule:** When EMVs tie, choose the act with the *larger* minimum payoff (or smaller EOL).
- ✅ **Sensitivity:** Change probabilities by ±10% and verify the chosen act remains optimal.
- ✅ **Final check:** KTU answers must show (a) the formula, (b) the substitution, (c) the numerical value, and (d) the conclusion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decisions Under Risk, Decision Under Uncertainty Conditions, Decision Tree Analysis. Game Theory: Games with Saddle Points
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT746 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Decisions Under Risk, Decision Under Uncertainty Conditions, Decision Tree Analysis. Game Theory: Games with Saddle Points

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Decision Making Under Risk

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A decision-making environment is classified as **Decision Under Risk** when the decision-maker can identify all possible states of nature (future events), assign quantitative **probability distributions** (subjective or objective) to each state, and then choose an alternative based on the expected value of each decision's outcome.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Characteristic of Risk:** Probabilities are **KNOWN or estimable**. The decision-maker cannot control the state of nature, but can quantify its likelihood. This distinguishes Risk from Uncertainty, where probabilities are entirely unknown.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a farmer in Kerala deciding whether to plant **Rice (Monsoon crop)** or **Tapioca (Drought-resistant crop)** for the upcoming season. The farmer has historical IMD (India Meteorological Department) data showing a **70% chance of normal monsoon** and a **30% chance of drought**. Since probabilities are known, this is a decision **under risk**. The farmer computes the expected profit for each crop and selects the one with the highest expected monetary value.

---

### 1.2 Decision Making Under Uncertainty

**Formal Definition:**
A decision-making environment is classified as **Decision Under Uncertainty** when the decision-maker can list all possible states of nature and the corresponding payoffs for each alternative, **but cannot assign probabilities** to those states. The decision-maker must then rely on heuristics or pessimistic/optimistic criteria to choose.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Characteristic of Uncertainty:** Probabilities are **UNKNOWN** and **NOT estimable**. The decision-maker uses qualitative criteria (maximin, maximax, Hurwicz, Laplace, minimax regret) to navigate ambiguity.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Consider a startup founder launching a **never-before-seen tech product** in an entirely new market. There is no historical data, no comparable product launch, and no reliable analogy. The founder cannot assign any probability to success or failure. They must rely on personal judgment and standardized decision rules (e.g., "plan for the worst" = maximin criterion).

---

### 1.3 Decision Tree Analysis

**Formal Definition:**
A **Decision Tree** is a graphical analytical tool that represents the sequence of decisions, chance events, and outcomes over time using a tree-like structure with **decision nodes (squares)**, **chance nodes (circles)**, and **terminal nodes (branches end)**. It enables the computation of Expected Monetary Value (EMV) by a **roll-back** procedure from right to left.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Node Convention:**
> - **Square (□)** = Decision node (decision-maker controls)
> - **Circle (○)** = Chance node (state of nature controls)
> - **Triangle (△)** = End/Result node (terminal payoff)

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a decision tree as a **choose-your-own-adventure flowchart** for a business problem. At each fork (square), the manager picks a path; at each coin-flip (circle), nature picks. By multiplying each payoff by its probability and "rolling back" from the end, the manager finds the smartest path.

---

### 1.4 Game Theory — Games with Saddle Points

**Formal Definition:**
**Game Theory** is the mathematical study of strategic interaction among rational decision-makers. A **Two-Person Zero-Sum Game** is one where one player's gain is exactly the other player's loss. A **Saddle Point** exists when the player can adopt a deterministic **pure strategy** that is optimal regardless of the opponent's choice, identified by the equality: **max(min) = min(max)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Saddle Point (Pure Strategy Equilibrium):**
> A cell in the payoff matrix that is simultaneously the **minimum of its row** (row player's security) and the **maximum of its column** (column player's security). If such a cell exists, the game is said to have a **Pure Strategy Solution**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a **penalty shootout** in football. The goalkeeper and striker each choose a corner (left or right). If the striker always kicks right and the goalkeeper always dives right, the striker's strategy is optimal only against that specific dive. But if a particular corner-choice combination is best for BOTH regardless of what the other does — that is the **saddle point** (a stable equilibrium).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decision Under Risk — Foundational Concepts

When probabilities are known, the analyst computes four critical expected-value metrics:

1. **Expected Monetary Value (EMV):**
   The probability-weighted average of payoffs for each decision alternative.

2. **Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL):**
   The probability-weighted average of regret values for each alternative.

3. **Expected Profit under Perfect Information (EPPI):**
   The maximum expected payoff when the state of nature is known in advance.

4. **Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI):**
   The maximum amount a rational decision-maker should pay for perfect (100% accurate) forecast information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:** Select the alternative with the **maximum EMV** (equivalently, the **minimum EOL**). Both methods must yield identical recommendations if computed correctly.

---

### 2.2 Decision Under Uncertainty — Five Standard Criteria

| # | Criterion | Formula / Logic | Optimism Stance |
|---|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Maximin (Wald)** | Choose alternative with **maximum of the row minimums** | Pessimistic |
| 2 | **Maximax (Optimistic)** | Choose alternative with **maximum of the row maximums** | Optimistic |
| 3 | **Hurwicz (Weighted)** | $\text{Hurwicz Value} = \alpha \cdot (\text{Max}) + (1-\alpha) \cdot (\text{Min})$ | Adjustable via $\alpha$ |
| 4 | **Laplace (Equal Likelihood)** | Choose alternative with **maximum row average** (each state equally likely) | Neutral |
| 5 | **Minimax Regret (Savage)** | First construct a **regret matrix**, then choose the minimum of row maximums | Risk-Averse |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hurwicz Coefficient $\alpha$:** When $\alpha \to 1$, the criterion is purely **optimistic (maximax)**. When $\alpha \to 0$, it degenerates to purely **pessimistic (maximin)**. The KTU 2024 syllabus typically specifies $\alpha = 0.5$ unless otherwise stated.

---

### 2.3 Decision Tree Analysis — Procedure

**Step-by-Step Roll-Back Procedure:**

1. **Structure** the problem left-to-right: identify decision points and chance events in chronological order.
2. **Label** each terminal branch with the resulting **payoff** (profit or cost).
3. **Compute EMV** at every chance node (circle) by multiplying each payoff by its probability and summing.
4. **Select** the branch with the highest EMV at every decision node (square) — discard other branches (indicated by double slash **||**).
5. **Roll back** from right to left, propagating the selected EMV leftward until the initial decision node is reached.

---

### 2.4 Game Theory — Saddle Point Identification

A **saddle point** is the unique cell where:

$$\max_i \left( \min_j a_{ij} \right) = \min_j \left( \max_i a_{ij} \right) = v$$

Where:
- $a_{ij}$ is the payoff to Player A (row player) at row $i$, column $j$
- $\min_j a_{ij}$ is the row minimum (security value for Player A)
- $\max_i a_{ij}$ is the column maximum (security value for Player B)
- $v$ is the **value of the game**

> [!WARNING]
> If **$\max_i (\min_j a_{ij}) \neq \min_j (\max_i a_{ij})$**, the game **has no saddle point in pure strategies** and requires **mixed strategies** (Linear Programming solution — covered in higher semesters).

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notation |
|---------|---------|----------|
| Expected Monetary Value | $\text{EMV}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} p_j \cdot a_{ij}$ | $p_j$ = prob. of state $j$, $a_{ij}$ = payoff |
| Expected Opportunity Loss | $\text{EOL}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} p_j \cdot r_{ij}$ | $r_{ij}$ = regret value |
| Regret Value | $r_{ij} = \max_k a_{kj} - a_{ij}$ | Max payoff in column $j$ minus actual payoff |
| Expected Profit with Perfect Info | $\text{EPPI} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} p_j \cdot \max_i a_{ij}$ | Best payoff in each state, weighted |
| Expected Value of Perfect Info | $\text{EVPI} = \text{EPPI} - \text{EMV}^*$ | $\text{EMV}^*$ = optimal EMV |
| Hurwicz Criterion | $H_i = \alpha \cdot \max_j a_{ij} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \min_j a_{ij}$ | $\alpha$ = optimism coefficient |
| Laplace Criterion | $L_i = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij}$ | Equal probability $\tfrac{1}{n}$ |
| Saddle Point Condition | $\max_i \min_j a_{ij} = \min_j \max_i a_{ij}$ | Pure strategy equilibrium |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Construction Engineering:** Choosing between foundation types under varying soil conditions with known geological probability distributions.
- **Manufacturing:** Selecting production capacity under forecasted demand uncertainty.
- **Investment Planning:** R&D project selection with probabilistic market response.
- **Strategic Bidding:** Competitive tender analysis using saddle-point game formulations.
- **Agricultural Planning:** Crop selection under monsoon variability (highly relevant for Kerala's agro-economy).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Decision Under Risk (Comprehensive)

**Problem Statement:**
A Kerala-based electronics company must choose one of three products (P1, P2, P3) to launch. Based on market research, the demand state probabilities are: **Favourable = 0.5**, **Moderate = 0.3**, **Unfavourable = 0.2**. The profit table (in ₹ Lakhs) is:

| Alternative | Favourable (0.5) | Moderate (0.3) | Unfavourable (0.2) |
|-------------|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **P1** | 200 | 100 | -50 |
| **P2** | 150 | 120 | 30 |
| **P3** | 100 | 80 | 60 |

**Required:** Find (i) EMV table, (ii) EOL table, (iii) EPPI, (iv) EVPI, and (v) optimal decision.

---

**Step (i): Compute EMV for each alternative.**

For **P1**:
$$\text{EMV}_{P1} = (0.5)(200) + (0.3)(100) + (0.2)(-50)$$
$$= 100 + 30 + (-10) = 120 \text{ Lakhs}$$

For **P2**:
$$\text{EMV}_{P2} = (0.5)(150) + (0.3)(120) + (0.2)(30)$$
$$= 75 + 36 + 6 = 117 \text{ Lakhs}$$

For **P3**:
$$\text{EMV}_{P3} = (0.5)(100) + (0.3)(80) + (0.2)(60)$$
$$= 50 + 24 + 12 = 86 \text{ Lakhs}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimal Decision:** Select **P1** with the highest EMV of **₹120 Lakhs**. The row of dominant EMV is shaded: $\text{EMV}^* = 120$.

---

**Step (ii): Construct the Regret Matrix and compute EOL.**

Regret $r_{ij}$ = (Column Maximum) $-$ (Cell Value)

**Column-wise maxima:**
- Favourable column: max(200, 150, 100) = **200**
- Moderate column: max(100, 120, 80) = **120**
- Unfavourable column: max(-50, 30, 60) = **60**

**Regret Matrix:**

| Alternative | Favourable | Moderate | Unfavourable |
|-------------|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **P1** | 200−200 = 0 | 120−100 = 20 | 60−(−50) = 110 |
| **P2** | 200−150 = 50 | 120−120 = 0 | 60−30 = 30 |
| **P3** | 200−100 = 100 | 120−80 = 40 | 60−60 = 0 |

**EOL Computation:**

For **P1**: $\text{EOL}_{P1} = (0.5)(0) + (0.3)(20) + (0.2)(110) = 0 + 6 + 22 = 28$

For **P2**: $\text{EOL}_{P2} = (0.5)(50) + (0.3)(0) + (0.2)(30) = 25 + 0 + 6 = 31$

For **P3**: $\text{EOL}_{P3} = (0.5)(100) + (0.3)(40) + (0.2)(0) = 50 + 12 + 0 = 62$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Verification:** The optimal decision under EOL rule selects the alternative with **minimum EOL** — i.e., **P1** (EOL = 28). This **matches** the EMV result, confirming consistency.

---

**Step (iii): Compute EPPI (Expected Profit under Perfect Information).**

The best payoff in each column is selected (as if we knew the state in advance):
- Favourable: best is **200** (from P1)
- Moderate: best is **120** (from P2)
- Unfavourable: best is **60** (from P3)

$$\text{EPPI} = (0.5)(200) + (0.3)(120) + (0.2)(60)$$
$$= 100 + 36 + 12 = 148 \text{ Lakhs}$$

---

**Step (iv): Compute EVPI.**

$$\text{EVPI} = \text{EPPI} - \text{EMV}^* = 148 - 120 = 28 \text{ Lakhs}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Managerial Interpretation:** The company should **not pay more than ₹28 Lakhs** for any market research/forecast report. Paying more means the cost of information exceeds its expected benefit.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Decision Under Uncertainty (All Five Criteria)

Using the same profit table as above but **treating probabilities as unknown**:

**Step 1: Row Minima and Row Maxima**

| Alternative | Favourable | Moderate | Unfavourable | Row Min | Row Max |
|-------------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **P1** | 200 | 100 | -50 | **-50** | **200** |
| **P2** | 150 | 120 | 30 | **30** | **150** |
| **P3** | 100 | 80 | 60 | **60** | **100** |

**Step 2: Apply Each Criterion**

**(a) Maximin (Wald — Pessimistic):**
- max(-50, 30, 60) = **60** → Choose **P3**

**(b) Maximax (Optimistic):**
- max(200, 150, 100) = **200** → Choose **P1**

**(c) Hurwicz (with $\alpha = 0.6$):**
$$H_{P1} = (0.6)(200) + (0.4)(-50) = 120 - 20 = 100$$
$$H_{P2} = (0.6)(150) + (0.4)(30) = 90 + 12 = 102$$
$$H_{P3} = (0.6)(100) + (0.4)(60) = 60 + 24 = 84$$
- max(100, 102, 84) = **102** → Choose **P2**

**(d) Laplace (Equal Probability):**
$$L_{P1} = \frac{200 + 100 - 50}{3} = \frac{250}{3} \approx 83.33$$
$$L_{P2} = \frac{150 + 120 + 30}{3} = \frac{300}{3} = 100$$
$$L_{P3} = \frac{100 + 80 + 60}{3} = \frac{240}{3} = 80$$
- max(83.33, 100, 80) = **100** → Choose **P2**

**(e) Minimax Regret (Savage):**
- Maximum regret in each row (from regret matrix in 3.1):
  - P1: max(0, 20, 110) = **110**
  - P2: max(50, 0, 30) = **50**
  - P3: max(100, 40, 0) = **100**
- min(110, 50, 100) = **50** → Choose **P2**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Summary of Recommendations:**

| Criterion | Recommended Alternative |
|-----------|:---:|
| Maximin | P3 |
| Maximax | P1 |
| Hurwicz ($\alpha = 0.6$) | P2 |
| Laplace | P2 |
| Minimax Regret | P2 |

> Most criteria converge on **P2** as the robust choice when probabilities are unknown.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Decision Tree Analysis

**Problem Statement:**
A startup must decide whether to **Test Market** a new product before full launch, or **Launch Directly**.
- Test Market cost: ₹1 Lakh
- Test Market result: **Favourable** (prob. = 0.7) → predicts success of launch
- Test Market result: **Unfavourable** (prob. = 0.3) → predicts failure
- If predicted Favourable, Launch: Revenue = ₹10 Lakhs, Cost = ₹3 Lakhs → **Profit = ₹7 Lakhs**
