- If predicted Unfavourable, Do NOT Launch: Profit = ₹0
- Direct Launch (without test): Success (prob = 0.6) → Profit = ₹7 Lakhs; Failure (prob = 0.4) → Loss = ₹3 Lakhs

**Step 1: Compute EMV at Chance Node 2 (Launch after Favourable test):**
$$\text{EMV}_{\text{Launch after Favourable}} = (1.0)(7) = 7 \text{ Lakhs}$$

(Note: Since test was favourable, the conditional probability of success is taken as 1.0 in the simplified textbook formulation.)

**Step 2: Compute EMV at Chance Node 3 (Decision after Unfavourable test):**
The firm chooses **NOT to launch**, so EMV = **0** (instead of negative expected loss).

**Step 3: Roll back to Chance Node 1 (Test Market outcome):**
$$\text{EMV}_{\text{Test Market}} = (0.7)(7) + (0.3)(0) - 1 \text{ (cost)}$$
$$= 4.9 + 0 - 1 = 3.9 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 4: EMV of Direct Launch:**
$$\text{EMV}_{\text{Direct}} = (0.6)(7) + (0.4)(-3) = 4.2 - 1.2 = 3.0 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 5: Compare at Initial Decision Node:**
$$\max(3.9, 3.0) = 3.9 \rightarrow \text{Choose Test Market}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimal Decision:** **Test Market first**, then launch if favourable. Expected profit = **₹3.9 Lakhs**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Game Theory with Saddle Point

**Problem Statement (Two-Person Zero-Sum Game):**

Player A (row player) and Player B (column player). Payoff matrix (A's payoffs):

| | B1 | B2 | B3 | Row Min |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A1** | **2** | 4 | 6 | 2 |
| **A2** | 6 | 7 | 5 | **5** |
| **A3** | 8 | 3 | 4 | 4 |
| **Col Max** | **8** | 7 | 6 | |

**Step 1: Identify Row Minimums (Player A's security strategy):**
- min(A1) = 2, min(A2) = **5**, min(A3) = 4

$$\max_i (\min_j a_{ij}) = \max(2, 5, 4) = 5$$

**Step 2: Identify Column Maximums (Player B's security strategy):**
- max(B1) = **8**, max(B2) = 7, max(B3) = 6

$$\min_j (\max_i a_{ij}) = \min(8, 7, 6) = 6$$

**Step 3: Compare:**
$$\max_i (\min_j) = 5 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \min_j (\max_i) = 6$$

$$5 \neq 6 \implies \text{No Saddle Point exists in pure strategies}$$

**Step 4: Modified Example — A Saddle Point Exists:**

Consider this refined payoff matrix:

| | B1 | B2 | B3 |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A1** | 5 | 4 | 6 |
| **A2** | **8** | 7 | 5 |
| **A3** | 9 | 6 | 4 |

- Row Minima: A1 → 4, A2 → 5, A3 → 4
- Column Maxima: B1 → 9, B2 → 7, B3 → 6

$$\max_i (\min_j) = 5 \quad \text{and} \quad \min_j (\max_i) = 6$$

Still no saddle point. Let me construct a **classical saddle-point example**:

| | B1 | B2 | B3 |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A1** | 3 | 7 | 4 |
| **A2** | 5 | **6** | 5 |
| **A3** | 4 | 8 | 6 |

- Row Minima: A1 → 3, A2 → 5, A3 → 4
- Column Maxima: B1 → 5, B2 → 8, B3 → 6

$$\max(\text{Row Min}) = 5 \quad \text{and} \quad \min(\text{Col Max}) = 5$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Saddle Point Located:** At cell **(A2, B2) = 6**. The value of the game is **$v = 5$**. Player A plays pure strategy **A2**; Player B plays pure strategy **B2**.

**Verification of Saddle Point:**
- 6 is the **minimum** in row A2 (the row player's guaranteed payoff)
- 6 is the **maximum** in column B2 (the column player's worst-case cost)

The cell is simultaneously the worst for B (column max) and the best guaranteed outcome for A (row min) — the geometric "saddle" of the matrix.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Summary

| Method | Information Needed | Decision Rule | When to Use |
|--------|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| EMV / EOL | Probabilities + Payoffs | Maximize EMV / Minimize EOL | Risk environment |
| Maximin | Payoffs only | Maximize row minimum | Highly pessimistic manager |
| Maximax | Payoffs only | Maximize row maximum | Highly optimistic manager |
| Hurwicz | Payoffs + $\alpha$ | $\alpha \cdot \text{max} + (1-\alpha)\cdot \text{min}$ | Adjustable optimism |
| Laplace | Payoffs only | Maximize row average | Rational indifference |
| Minimax Regret | Payoffs only | Minimize max regret | Risk-averse, loss-averse manager |
| Decision Tree | Probabilities + Sequential Decisions | Roll-back EMV | Multi-stage decisions |
| Pure Strategy Game | Payoff matrix | $\max(\min) = \min(\max)$ | Competitive conflict with equilibrium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Tree Schematic — Test Market Example

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Decision: Test or Direct Launch] -->|Test Market| B(Chance: Test Result)
    A -->|Direct Launch| H(Chance: Market Response)
    B -->|Favourable p=0.7| C[Decision: Launch or Not]
    B -->|Unfavourable p=0.3| F[Decision: Launch or Not]
    C -->|Launch| D(Chance: Market Success 1.0)
    C -->|Do Not Launch| E[Profit = 0]
    F -->|Launch| G(Chance: Loss Risk)
    F -->|Do Not Launch| I[Profit = 0]
    D -->|Success| J[Profit = 7 Lakhs]
    G -->|Failure| K[Loss = 3 Lakhs]
    H -->|Success p=0.6| L[Profit = 7 Lakhs]
    H -->|Failure p=0.4| M[Loss = 3 Lakhs]
```

---

### 4.2 Decision Tree with EMV Roll-Back Values

```mermaid
graph TD
    N1[Initial Decision Node]:::decision -->|Test Market cost 1L| N2(EMV = 3.9L):::chance
    N1 -->|Direct Launch| N3(EMV = 3.0L):::chance
    N2 -->|Favourable 0.7| N4[Launch: EMV = 7L]:::decision
    N2 -->|Unfavourable 0.3| N5[Skip: EMV = 0L]:::decision
    N4 -.->|Discard| N4x[Cut Branch]
    N5 -.->|Choose| N5x[Selected Path]
    N3 -->|Success 0.6| N6[Profit = 7L]
    N3 -->|Failure 0.4| N7[Loss = -3L]

    classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef chance fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.3 Decision-Making Framework — Choice of Method

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Decision Problem Identified] --> Q1{Known Probabilities?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, Known| RISK[Decision Under Risk]
    Q1 -->|No, Unknown| UNCER[Decision Under Uncertainty]
    Q1 -->|Opponent Strategic| GAME[Game Theory]

    RISK --> R1[Compute EMV Table]
    R1 --> R2[Compute Regret Matrix]
    R2 --> R3[Compute EOL Table]
    R3 --> R4[Compute EPPI and EVPI]
    R4 --> R5[Select Maximum EMV]

    UNCER --> U1{Multiple Criteria}
    U1 --> U2[Maximin Wald Pessimistic]
    U1 --> U3[Maximax Optimistic]
    U1 --> U4[Hurwicz Weighted]
    U1 --> U5[Laplace Equal]
    U1 --> U6[Minimax Regret Savage]

    GAME --> G1[Identify Row Minima]
    GAME --> G2[Identify Column Maxima]
    G1 --> G3{Max Min equals Min Max?}
    G2 --> G3
    G3 -->|Yes| G4[Saddle Point Found Pure Strategy]
    G3 -->|No| G5[No Pure Strategy Use Mixed Strategy LP]
```

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Saddle Point Detection

```mermaid
graph TD
    INPUT[Input Payoff Matrix A m x n] --> ROWSCAN[Compute Row Minimums r_min_i]
    INPUT --> COLSCAN[Compute Column Maximums c_max_j]
    ROWSCAN --> MAXROW[Find Max of Row Mins max min]
    COLSCAN --> MINCOL[Find Min of Col Maxs min max]
    MAXROW --> COMPARE{Equality Check}
    MINCOL --> COMPARE
    COMPARE -->|Equal value v| SADDLE[Output Saddle Point Coordinates and Value v]
    COMPARE -->|Not Equal| NOSADDLE[Output No Saddle Point Proceed to Mixed Strategy]
    SADDLE --> STRATEGY[Pure Strategy Player A plays row i Player B plays column j]
    NOSADDLE --> LPFORM[Formulate Linear Program for Optimal Mixed Strategy]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define "Decision Under Risk" and state two criteria that can be used when probabilities of states of nature are known.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A decision-making situation is termed **"Decision Under Risk"** when the decision-maker can list all possible states of nature and **assign known probabilities** (objective from past data or subjective from expert judgment) to each state, though the actual state that will occur is beyond the decision-maker's control.

Two criteria used under risk:
1. **Expected Monetary Value (EMV) Criterion** — Select the alternative with the maximum probability-weighted expected payoff.
2. **Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL) Criterion** — Equivalent to EMV; select the alternative with the minimum probability-weighted expected regret.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Insight:** A complete answer must mention (a) probabilities are known, (b) states are identifiable, and (c) two named criteria. Missing any one element costs 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Distinguish between the **Maximin** and **Minimax Regret** criteria of decision-making under uncertainty.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Maximin (Wald) | Minimax Regret (Savage) |
|--------|----------------|-------------------------|
| **Focus** | Original payoffs | Opportunity loss (regret) matrix |
| **Logic** | Maximize the worst-case payoff | Minimize the worst-case regret |
| **Procedure** | Find row minimums → choose maximum | Build regret matrix → find row max → choose minimum |
| **Philosophy** | Pessimistic — assumes worst will happen | Loss-averse — minimizes missed opportunity |

The Maximin criterion works directly on the payoff matrix, while Minimax Regret first transforms the matrix by subtracting each cell value from its column maximum to obtain the regret matrix.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Decision Under Risk & Uncertainty**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** A company is considering three investment alternatives I1, I2, I3. The profits (in ₹ Crores) under three market conditions are given below:

| Alternative | Boom (p=0.4) | Stable (p=0.4) | Recession (p=0.2) |
|-------------|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **I1** | 50 | 30 | 10 |
| **I2** | 40 | 35 | 25 |
| **I3** | 30 | 25 | 20 |

Determine the optimal decision using **(i) EMV criterion** and **(ii) EOL criterion**. Also compute **EVPI**.

**(b)** Re-examine the same problem assuming probabilities are unknown. Apply **Maximin, Laplace, and Minimax Regret** criteria and recommend the best alternative. Use $\alpha = 0.7$ for Hurwicz if applicable.

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Decision Under Risk (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: EMV Computation**

For I1: $\text{EMV}_{I1} = (0.4)(50) + (0.4)(30) + (0.2)(10) = 20 + 12 + 2 = 34$

For I2: $\text{EMV}_{I2} = (0.4)(40) + (0.4)(35) + (0.2)(25) = 16 + 14 + 5 = 35$

For I3: $\text{EMV}_{I3} = (0.4)(30) + (0.4)(25) + (0.2)(20) = 12 + 10 + 4 = 26$

$$\boxed{\text{EMV}^* = 35 \text{ at I2} \Rightarrow \text{Choose I2}}$$

**Step 2: Regret Matrix Construction**

Column Maxima: Boom = 50, Stable = 35, Recession = 25

| Alt | Boom Regret | Stable Regret | Recession Regret |
|-----|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| I1 | 50-50 = 0 | 35-30 = 5 | 25-10 = 15 |
| I2 | 50-40 = 10 | 35-35 = 0 | 25-25 = 0 |
| I3 | 50-30 = 20 | 35-25 = 10 | 25-20 = 5 |

**Step 3: EOL Computation**

$\text{EOL}_{I1} = (0.4)(0) + (0.4)(5) + (0.2)(15) = 0 + 2 + 3 = 5$

$\text{EOL}_{I2} = (0.4)(10) + (0.4)(0) + (0.2)(0) = 4 + 0 + 0 = 4$

$\text{EOL}_{I3} = (0.4)(20) + (0.4)(10) + (0.2)(5) = 8 + 4 + 1 = 13$

$$\boxed{\min \text{EOL} = 4 \text{ at I2} \Rightarrow \text{Choose I2 (consistent with EMV)}}$$

**Step 4: EPPI and EVPI**

$\text{EPPI} = (0.4)(50) + (0.4)(35) + (0.2)(25) = 20 + 14 + 5 = 39$

$\text{EVPI} = \text{EPPI} - \text{EMV}^* = 39 - 35 = 4 \text{ Crores}$

---

**Part (b) — Decision Under Uncertainty (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Maximin**
- Row Minima: I1 → 10, I2 → 25, I3 → 20
- max(10, 25, 20) = **25** → Choose **I2**

**Step 2: Laplace**
- $L_{I1} = (50+30+10)/3 = 30$
- $L_{I2} = (40+35+25)/3 = 33.33$
- $L_{I3} = (30+25+20)/3 = 25$
- max(30, 33.33, 25) = **33.33** → Choose **I2**

**Step 3: Minimax Regret** (using regret matrix)
- Row Max Regret: I1 → max(0, 5, 15) = 15, I2 → max(10, 0, 0) = 10, I3 → max(20, 10, 5) = 20
- min(15, 10, 20) = **10** → Choose **I2**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** All three criteria unanimously recommend **Alternative I2** as the optimal choice under uncertainty.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Decision Tree & Game Theory**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** A company can either **conduct a market survey** (cost ₹2 Lakhs) before launching a product or **launch directly**. The survey gives a favourable report with probability 0.8 and unfavourable with probability 0.2. The product's net profit is:
- ₹20 Lakhs if launched and market is good
- ₹5 Lakhs if launched and market is poor
- ₹0 if not launched
- Market is good with prior probability 0.6 (without survey), 0.9 given favourable survey, 0.3 given unfavourable survey.

Construct a decision tree and determine the optimal strategy.

**(b)** Solve the following two-person zero-sum game using the saddle-point method:

| | B1 | B2 | B3 | B4 |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A1** | 4 | 6 | 8 | 2 |
| **A2** | 2 | 4 | 4 | 6 |
| **A3** | 6 | 8 | 4 | 4 |
| **A4** | 4 | 4 | 0 | 8 |

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Decision Tree Analysis (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Compute EMV at Terminal Chance Nodes**

- **Launch after favourable survey**: $\text{EMV} = (0.9)(20) + (0.3)(5) = 18 + 1.5 = 19.5$ Lakhs
- **No launch after unfavourable survey**: $\text{EMV} = 0$
- **Launch after unfavourable survey**: $\text{EMV} = (0.3)(20) + (0.7)(5) = 6 + 3.5 = 9.5$ Lakhs (but risk-averse managers choose "Not Launch")
- **Direct Launch**: $\text{EMV} = (0.6)(20) + (0.4)(5) = 12 + 2 = 14$ Lakhs

**Step 2: Roll back to Decision Node after Favourable Survey**
- Choose "Launch" → EMV = 19.5

**Step 3: Roll back to Decision Node after Unfavourable Survey**
- Choose "Do Not Launch" → EMV = 0 (avoid risk)

**Step 4: EMV at Survey Chance Node**
$$\text{EMV}_{\text{Survey}} = (0.8)(19.5) + (0.2)(0) - 2 \text{ (cost)}$$
$$= 15.6 + 0 - 2 = 13.6 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 5: Comparison**
$$\text{EMV}_{\text{Survey}} = 13.6 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{EMV}_{\text{Direct}} = 14$$

$$\boxed{\text{Choose Direct Launch (EMV} = 14 \text{ Lakhs)}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Game Theory with Saddle Point (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Compute Row Minima**

| | B1 | B2 | B3 | B4 | Row Min |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A1 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 2 | **2** |
| A2 | 2 | 4 | 4 | 6 | **2** |
| A3 | 6 | 8 | 4 | 4 | **4** |
| A4 | 4 | 4 | 0 | 8 | **0** |
| Col Max | **6** | **8** | **8** | **8** | |

**Step 2: Identify Security Strategies**
$$\max_i (\min_j a_{ij}) = \max(2, 2, 4, 0) = 4$$

$$\min_j (\max_i a_{ij}) = \min(6, 8, 8, 8) = 6$$

**Step 3: Comparison**
$$4 \neq 6 \implies \text{No saddle point in pure strategies exists}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since the game has no pure-strategy saddle point, the solution must be obtained via **mixed strategies** (Linear Programming formulation — covered in advanced OR courses).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Maximin and Minimax terminology** (1–2 marks deducted): Maximin applies to the **row player** (maximizer); Minimax applies to the **column player** (minimizer). Writing "minimax" when you mean "maximin" is a frequent error.
>
> 2. **Incorrect regret matrix construction** (2–3 marks deducted): The regret value must be (Column Max − Cell Value). Students often incorrectly use (Cell Value − Column Max) yielding negative regrets, which then propagate through the EOL calculation.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract the survey cost in decision tree roll-back** (2 marks deducted): The cost of conducting a survey must be subtracted at the appropriate chance node, not at the end.
>
> 4. **Wrong sign convention in game theory** (1 mark deducted): In a saddle-point check, ensure you compute $\max(\min)$ for the maximizer's strategy and $\min(\max)$ for the minimizer's strategy — never the reverse.
>
> 5. **Using $\alpha$ inversely in Hurwicz criterion** (1 mark deducted): $\alpha$ weights the **maximum** (optimistic) outcome, not the minimum. Formula is $\alpha \cdot \max + (1-\alpha) \cdot \min$.
>
> 6. **Not labeling decision vs chance nodes** in tree diagrams (1 mark deducted per occurrence): Decision nodes must be **squares**, chance nodes must be **circles**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4**

- [ ] **Risk vs Uncertainty:** Risk = known probabilities; Uncertainty = unknown probabilities
- [ ] **EMV formula:** $\sum p_j \cdot a_{ij}$ for each alternative; choose the **maximum**
- [ ] **Regret formula:** Column max − Cell value; never reverse the sign
- [ ] **EOL rule:** Select alternative with **minimum** EOL
- [ ] **EPPI:** Always multiply the **column maximums** (not the chosen alternative's payoffs) by probabilities
- [ ] **EVPI = EPPI − EMV\***: This is the maximum justifiable expenditure on perfect information
- [ ] **Maximin (Wald):** Pick row with **maximum of the row minimums** — pessimistic
- [ ] **Maximax:** Pick row with **maximum of the row maximums** — optimistic
- [ ] **Hurwicz:** $\alpha \cdot \text{max} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \text{min}$; $\alpha = 1$ → maximax; $\alpha = 0$ → maximin
- [ ] **Laplace:** Average of all payoffs in each row; pick **maximum average**
- [ ] **Minimax Regret (Savage):** Build regret table → pick row with **minimum of the row maximums**
- [ ] **Decision Tree nodes:** Squares = Decision, Circles = Chance, Triangles = End
- [ ] **Roll-back direction:** Always right to left; discard branches with double slash (||)
- [ ] **Don't forget to subtract** survey/test market costs in tree roll-back
- [ ] **Game theory setup:** Row player = maximizer (A), Column player = minimizer (B)
- [ ] **Saddle point check:** $\max(\min) = \min(\max) \Rightarrow$ Pure strategy exists
- [ ] **Saddle point cell:** Must simultaneously be row minimum AND column maximum
- [ ] **Value of game ($v$):** The common value when $\max(\min) = \min(\max)$
- [ ] **No saddle point:** Game requires mixed strategies (LP-based solution)

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a problem provides probabilities, always compute **both EMV and EOL** to cross-verify. When probabilities are absent, expect a sub-question on each criterion (Maximin, Laplace, Hurwicz, Minimax Regret). For games, the fastest method is to **compute row min and column max simultaneously** — saddle point identification typically takes less than 60 seconds once the matrix is given.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dominance Property
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT746 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dominance Property

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DOMINANCE PROPERTY (KTU Definition):**
> In the theory of two-person zero-sum games with a rectangular payoff matrix, the **Dominance Property** is a strategic simplification rule that allows the elimination of certain "inferior" (dominated) pure strategies of a player — either the row player (Player A / Maximizer) or the column player (Player B / Minimizer) — *without affecting the value of the game or the optimal mixed strategy*.

**Formal Statement:**
Given an $m \times n$ payoff matrix $\mathbf{A} = [a_{ij}]$, a pure strategy $R_i$ of the row player is said to be **dominated** by another pure strategy $R_k$ (or by a convex combination of strategies) if:

- **Strict Domination:** $a_{kj} > a_{ij} \quad \forall\, j = 1, 2, \dots, n$
- **Weak Domination:** $a_{kj} \geq a_{ij} \quad \forall\, j = 1, 2, \dots, n$  with at least one strict inequality.

The column player's strategies are dominated analogously (inequalities reversed, since B is the minimizer).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why would a rational player ever play a move that is ALWAYS worse?"**

Imagine you are playing a game of **Rock-Paper-Scissors**, but the referee has secretly told you the opponent's move pattern. If you discover that one of your strategies (say, *always playing Scissors*) **always loses or ties against every possible response of the opponent**, you would NEVER choose it — no matter how clever your randomization is.

That "loser strategy" is said to be **dominated**. So you simply cross it off your list and solve a smaller game.

**Real-World Analogy — Choosing a Job Offer:**
Suppose Company A offers a salary strictly less than Company B **for every single role/condition** (location, bonus, growth, work hours). Then offer A is *strictly dominated* by B. A rational candidate ignores A entirely. This is *exactly* the Dominance Property — eliminating the "always-inferior" choice.

---

## 1.3 Physical / Standard Metrics Highlight

> [!TIP]
> **Key Metrics in Dominance Analysis:**
> - **Payoff Matrix Order Reduced:** From $m \times n$ down to $m' \times n'$ where $m' < m$ or $n' < n$.
> - **Equilibrium Invariant:** The saddle point / value of the game $v$ is **preserved** exactly.
> - **Typical Reduction Ratios:** $\geq 25\%$ in mid-sized games, enabling hand-solvability of up to $5 \times 5$ matrices.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structured Logic Breakdown

### A. Types of Dominance

1. **Row Player's Dominance** (Max-player A eliminates a row):
   - Row $R_i$ is dominated by Row $R_k$ if $a_{kj} \geq a_{ij}\, \forall j$ (with at least one strict $>$). The dominant row $R_k$ is **retained**.

2. **Column Player's Dominance** (Min-player B eliminates a column):
   - Column $C_j$ is dominated by Column $C_\ell$ if $a_{i\ell} \leq a_{ij}\, \forall i$ (with at least one strict $<$). The dominant column $C_\ell$ is **retained**.

3. **Strict vs. Weak Dominance:**
   - **Strict:** All inequalities are strict ($>$ or $<$). Strategy is **always** strictly worse.
   - **Weak:** All inequalities are $\geq$ or $\leq$ with at least one strict. Strategy is *never better* and sometimes worse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Rule:** In weak dominance, the dominated strategy can sometimes still be part of an *optimal mixed strategy* — so **strict dominance is the safer elimination rule** for board answers. State which type you are using.

4. **Simultaneous & Iterated Dominance:**
   - After eliminating one strategy, NEW dominances may appear. Always **re-check the matrix** after each round.

### B. Conditions When Dominance CANNOT Be Applied

- No row/column strictly dominates another.
- Strategies differ in opposing directions (one better in some columns, worse in others).
- Game is in a **pure strategy equilibrium already** (then no further simplification needed).

### C. The Underlying Game-Theoretic Justification

The Dominance Principle is valid because:
- A rational player will never mix in a strictly dominated strategy (it strictly lowers expected payoff).
- The minimax–maximin value $v$ of the game is invariant under strategy elimination (a theorem due to **Brown-von Neumann**, 1950s).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Statement | Condition | Effect on Matrix |
|---|---------|------------------------|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | Strict Row Dominance | $a_{kj} > a_{ij}\quad \forall j$ | All strict $>$ | **Delete row** $R_i$ |
| 2 | Weak Row Dominance | $a_{kj} \geq a_{ij}\quad \forall j$ | At least one strict | Delete row $R_i$ (caution) |
| 3 | Strict Column Dominance | $a_{i\ell} < a_{ij}\quad \forall i$ | All strict $<$ | **Delete column** $C_j$ |
| 4 | Weak Column Dominance | $a_{i\ell} \leq a_{ij}\quad \forall i$ | At least one strict | Delete column $C_j$ (caution) |
| 5 | Mixed Strategy Dominance | $R_i \prec \sum_k \lambda_k R_k$ with $\sum \lambda_k = 1,\ \lambda_k > 0$ | Linear convex combo | Delete row $R_i$ |
| 6 | Invariant Game Value | $v(\mathbf{A}) = v(\mathbf{A}')$ | Where $\mathbf{A}'$ is reduced | Value unchanged |
| 7 | Optimal Mix Invariance | $X^* = X'^*$ (after normalization) | Reduced game solved | Mix preserved |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Dominance reduction is extensively used in:
> - **Auction design** (eliminating losing bids).
> - **Network routing games** (prune dominated routes).
> - **Military strategy games** (Lanchester-type attrition models).
> - **Economic mechanism design** (rationalizability in Bayesian games).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — End-to-End KTU Board Style

### Problem Statement
Two firms (A and B) compete in a market. The payoff matrix (in ₹ Crores) for Firm A is:

$$
\mathbf{A} =
\begin{bmatrix}
2 & 4 & 6 \\
3 & 5 & 4 \\
7 & 1 & 3
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Here, A chooses the **row** (maximizer) and B chooses the **column** (minimizer). Solve using the **Dominance Property** and find the value of the game.

---

### Step 1 — Compare Rows for Row Dominance (Firm A's perspective)

We compare $R_1 = (2, 4, 6)$ with $R_2 = (3, 5, 4)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{21} - a_{11} &= 3 - 2 = 1 > 0 \\
a_{22} - a_{12} &= 5 - 4 = 1 > 0 \\
a_{23} - a_{13} &= 4 - 6 = -2 < 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $R_2$ is better in some columns and worse in others, **no dominance between $R_1$ and $R_2$**.

Now compare $R_1$ with $R_3 = (7, 1, 3)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{31} - a_{11} &= 7 - 2 = 5 > 0 \\
a_{32} - a_{12} &= 1 - 4 = -3 < 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**No dominance.** Compare $R_2$ with $R_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{31} - a_{21} &= 7 - 3 = 4 > 0 \\
a_{32} - a_{22} &= 1 - 5 = -4 < 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**No row dominance found** in the original matrix.

---

### Step 2 — Compare Columns for Column Dominance (Firm B's perspective)

Recall B is the **minimizer**, so B prefers *smaller* payoffs.

Compare $C_1 = (2, 3, 7)^T$ with $C_2 = (4, 5, 1)^T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{12} - a_{11} &= 4 - 2 = 2 > 0 \quad (\text{C}_2 \text{ worse}) \\
a_{22} - a_{21} &= 5 - 3 = 2 > 0 \quad (\text{C}_2 \text{ worse}) \\
a_{32} - a_{31} &= 1 - 7 = -6 < 0 \quad (\text{C}_2 \text{ better})
\end{aligned}
$$

**No dominance.** Compare $C_1$ with $C_3 = (6, 4, 3)^T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{13} - a_{11} &= 6 - 2 = 4 > 0 \quad (\text{C}_3 \text{ worse}) \\
a_{23} - a_{21} &= 4 - 3 = 1 > 0 \quad (\text{C}_3 \text{ worse}) \\
a_{33} - a_{31} &= 3 - 7 = -4 < 0 \quad (\text{C}_3 \text{ better})
\end{aligned}
$$

**No dominance.** Compare $C_2$ with $C_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{13} - a_{12} &= 6 - 4 = 2 > 0 \quad (\text{C}_3 \text{ worse}) \\
a_{23} - a_{22} &= 4 - 5 = -1 < 0 \quad (\text{C}_3 \text{ better})
\end{aligned}
$$

**No dominance at this stage.**

---

### Step 3 — Iterated Dominance Trick (Solve the Game First, Then Verify)

When direct dominance fails, apply algebraic solution first, then verify dominance in the *reduced* matrix from the optimal support.

**Solve $3 \times 3$ matrix** by assuming all strategies are used (no saddle point — verified since $\min$ of row maxes = 4 ≠ max of column mins = 5, so saddle point absent).

Let $A$ mix with $(p_1, p_2, p_3)$ and $B$ mix with $(q_1, q_2, q_3)$. For B's indifference:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 p_1 + 3 p_2 + 7 p_3 &= v \\
4 p_1 + 5 p_2 + 1 p_3 &= v \\
6 p_1 + 4 p_2 + 3 p_3 &= v
\end{aligned}
$$

with $p_1 + p_2 + p_3 = 1$.

Equating (1) and (2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 p_1 + 3 p_2 + 7 p_3 &= 4 p_1 + 5 p_2 + 1 p_3 \\
\Rightarrow 6 p_3 &= 2 p_1 + 2 p_2 \\
\Rightarrow p_1 + p_2 &= 3 p_3 \quad \text{...(I)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating (2) and (3):

$$
\begin{aligned}
4 p_1 + 5 p_2 + 1 p_3 &= 6 p_1 + 4 p_2 + 3 p_3 \\
\Rightarrow p_2 &= 2 p_1 + 2 p_3 \quad \text{...(II)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute $p_3 = 1 - p_1 - p_2$ into (II):

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_2 &= 2 p_1 + 2(1 - p_1 - p_2) \\
p_2 &= 2 p_1 + 2 - 2 p_1 - 2 p_2 \\
3 p_2 &= 2 \\
p_2 &= \frac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then $p_1 = 2 p_3 - p_2 \Rightarrow$ from (I): $p_1 = 3 p_3 - p_2 = 3 p_3 - \frac{2}{3}$. Also $p_1 + p_2 + p_3 = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 + \frac{2}{3} + p_3 &= 1 \Rightarrow p_1 + p_3 = \frac{1}{3} \\
\text{From (I):}\ p_1 + p_2 &= 3 p_3 \Rightarrow p_1 + \frac{2}{3} = 3 p_3 \Rightarrow p_1 = 3 p_3 - \frac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $3 p_3 - \frac{2}{3} + p_3 = \frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow 4 p_3 = 1 \Rightarrow p_3 = \frac{1}{4}$.

Then $p_1 = \frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{12}$ and $p_2 = \frac{2}{3}$.

**Verification:** Since $p_1 = \frac{1}{12} > 0$, $p_2 = \frac{2}{3} > 0$, $p_3 = \frac{1}{4} > 0$ — **all three rows ARE in the optimal support**. So in this example, dominance does NOT reduce the matrix.

---

### Step 4 — A Cleaner Example Where Dominance Works

Consider:

$$
\mathbf{B} =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 3 & 2 \\
4 & 6 & 5 \\
7 & 5 & 8
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Check rows:** Compare $R_1 = (1, 3, 2)$ with $R_2 = (4, 6, 5)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4 > 1, \quad 6 > 3, \quad 5 > 2
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **$R_2$ strictly dominates $R_1$** — **Delete $R_1$**.

**Check rows in reduced $2 \times 3$:** $R_2 = (4, 6, 5)$, $R_3 = (7, 5, 8)$ — mixed, no further row dominance.

**Check columns:** $C_1 = (4, 7)^T$, $C_2 = (6, 5)^T$, $C_3 = (5, 8)^T$.

For B (minimizer), smaller is better:
- $C_1$ vs $C_2$: $4 < 6$ ✅ but $7 > 5$ ❌ — no dominance.
- $C_2$ vs $C_3$: $6 > 5$ ❌ — no dominance.

So we reduce to a clean $2 \times 3$ matrix:

$$
\mathbf{B}' =
\begin{bmatrix}
4 & 6 & 5 \\
7 & 5 & 8
\end{bmatrix}
$$

This is now **hand-solvable** by Linear Programming / graphical method.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Dominance Reduction Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start: Given m x n Payoff Matrix]) --> A1[Step 1: List all rows of Player A]
    A1 --> A2{Does any row R_i have all entries <= some other row R_k with at least one strict ?}
    A2 -- Yes --> A3[Delete the dominated row R_i]
    A2 -- No --> A4[Step 2: List all columns of Player B]
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5{Does any column C_j have all entries >= some other column C_l with at least one strict ?}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Delete the dominated column C_j]
    A5 -- No --> A7[Step 3: Was matrix reduced ?]
    A6 --> A7
    A7 -- Yes --> A1
    A7 -- No --> A8[Step 4: Apply LP or graphical method to reduced matrix]
    A8 --> A9[Step 5: Find value v and optimal strategies]
    A9 --> A10([End: Solution Invariant to Original Game])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Conceptual Architecture of Dominance

```mermaid
graph LR
    N1[Original Game m x n] --> N2[Scan Rows for Dominance]
    N1 --> N3[Scan Columns for Dominance]
    N2 --> N4{Dominance Found ?}
    N3 --> N4
    N4 -- Row Dominance --> N5[Eliminate Row]
    N4 -- Column Dominance --> N6[Eliminate Column]
    N4 -- None --> N7[Use LP Method Directly]
    N5 --> N8[Reduced Game m-prime x n]
    N6 --> N9[Reduced Game m x n-prime]
    N8 --> N10[Recheck for Further Dominance]
    N9 --> N10
    N10 --> N4
    N7 --> N11[Optimal Strategies X* and Y*]
    N10 --> N11
```

## 4.3 Schematic Table — Decision Logic

| Player | Direction | Dominance Test | Action |
|--------|-----------|----------------|--------|
| Row (A) | Maximizer | $a_{kj} \geq a_{ij}\,\forall j$ (with strict) | Drop $R_i$ |
| Row (A) | Maximizer | No such $R_k$ exists | Keep all rows |
| Column (B) | Minimizer | $a_{i\ell} \leq a_{ij}\,\forall i$ (with strict) | Drop $C_j$ |
| Column (B) | Minimizer | No such $C_\ell$ exists | Keep all columns |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

### Q1. Define Dominance Property in the context of a two-person zero-sum game. When is a strategy said to be strictly dominated?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Dominance Property** is a principle of simplification in rectangular games, which states that if a pure strategy of a player is *never better* (and is *sometimes worse*) than another pure strategy of the same player, regardless of the opponent's choice, then the dominated strategy can be **safely eliminated** from the game without affecting the game's value.

A pure strategy $R_i$ of the row player is **strictly dominated** by strategy $R_k$ if:

$$a_{kj} > a_{ij} \quad \text{for all } j = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

That is, every entry in row $R_k$ is strictly greater than the corresponding entry in row $R_i$. The strictly dominated row $R_i$ is removed from the matrix during solution.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

### Q2. Distinguish between Strict Dominance and Weak Dominance. Which one is the safer rule for elimination in board examinations?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Strict Dominance | Weak Dominance |
|--------|------------------|----------------|
| Inequality | $a_{kj} > a_{ij}\,\forall j$ | $a_{kj} \geq a_{ij}\,\forall j$ with at least one strict |
| Strictness | All inequalities strict | At least one non-strict |
| Safety | Always safe to eliminate | Can change optimal mixed strategy |

**Conclusion:** **Strict dominance** is the safer rule for board examinations, as it **guarantees** that the eliminated strategy has a zero probability in any optimal mix. Weak dominance elimination is allowed but **must be explicitly stated as "weak"** in the answer to gain full credit.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the concept of Dominance Property in detail. Discuss the types of dominance with suitable examples. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** For the payoff matrix below, use the Dominance Property to reduce the matrix and hence find the value of the game and the optimal strategies for both players using the graphical method.

$$
\mathbf{A} =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 5 & 8 \\
3 & 4 & 7 \\
6 & 2 & 9
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**[(7 Marks)]**

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a):

**Concept:** The Dominance Property in game theory allows elimination of inferior strategies. There are four types:

1. **Strict Row Dominance:** $a_{kj} > a_{ij}\,\forall j$. The inferior row $R_i$ is deleted.
2. **Strict Column Dominance:** $a_{i\ell} < a_{ij}\,\forall i$. The inferior column $C_j$ is deleted.
3. **Weak Row Dominance:** $a_{kj} \geq a_{ij}\,\forall j$ with at least one strict inequality.
4. **Weak Column Dominance:** $a_{i\ell} \leq a_{ij}\,\forall i$ with at least one strict inequality.

**Example:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
2 & 4 \\
6 & 8
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Here $R_1$ is strictly dominated by $R_2$ since $6>2$ and $8>4$. The reduced $1 \times 2$ game has value $6$.

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Check row dominance in** $\mathbf{A}$:

- $R_1 = (1, 5, 8)$, $R_2 = (3, 4, 7)$, $R_3 = (6, 2, 9)$.
- $R_3 - R_1 = (5, -3, 1)$ — mixed, no dominance.
- $R_3 - R_2 = (3, -2, 2)$ — mixed, no dominance.
- $R_2 - R_1 = (2, -1, -1)$ — mixed, no dominance.

No row dominance.

**Step 2 — Check column dominance:**

- $C_1 = (1, 3, 6)^T$, $C_2 = (5, 4, 2)^T$, $C_3 = (8, 7, 9)^T$.
- For minimizer B, smaller is better.
- $C_2$ vs $C_1$: $5>1$ ❌, no dominance.
- $C_2$ vs $C_3$: $5<8$ ✅, $4<7$ ✅, $2<9$ ✅ — **$C_2$ strictly dominates $C_3$!** **Delete $C_3$.**

**Reduced matrix:**

$$
\mathbf{A}' =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 5 \\
3 & 4 \\
6 & 2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Recheck rows in** $\mathbf{A}'$:

- $R_1 = (1, 5)$, $R_2 = (3, 4)$, $R_3 = (6, 2)$.
- $R_3$ vs $R_1$: $(6>1, 2<5)$ — no.
- $R_3$ vs $R_2$: $(6>3, 2<4)$ — no.
- $R_2$ vs $R_1$: $(3>1, 4<5)$ — no.

**Step 4 — Recheck columns in** $\mathbf{A}'$:

- $C_1 = (1, 3, 6)^T$, $C_2 = (5, 4, 2)^T$.
- $C_1$ vs $C_2$: $1<5$ ✅, $3<4$ ✅, $6>2$ ❌ — no dominance.

**Step 5 — Graphical solution for** $3 \times 2$ **game:**

Firm A mixes between $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$. Expected payoff lines against $q$ (prob of $C_1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1(q) &= 1 \cdot q + 5(1-q) = 5 - 4q \\
E_2(q) &= 3 \cdot q + 4(1-q) = 4 - q \\
E_3(q) &= 6 \cdot q + 2(1-q) = 2 + 4q
\end{aligned}
$$

The maximin occurs at intersection of $E_2$ and $E_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4 - q &= 2 + 4q \\
2 &= 5q \\
q &= \frac{2}{5}
\end{aligned}
$$

Value $v = 4 - \frac{2}{5} = \frac{18}{5} = 3.6$.

**Optimal Strategy for A:** Since $E_1$ at $q = \frac{2}{5}$ is $5 - 4 \cdot \frac{2}{5} = \frac{17}{5} = 3.4 < 3.6$, drop $R_1$. Thus A plays $(R_2, R_3)$ only:

$$
p_2 = ?, \quad p_3 = 1 - p_2.
$$

Indifference: $4 - q = 2 + 4q \Rightarrow q = 2/5$. To find $p_2, p_3$, use B's indifference:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3 p_2 + 6 p_3 &= v = 3.6 \\
4 p_2 + 2 p_3 &= v = 3.6
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting: $-p_2 + 4 p_3 = 0 \Rightarrow p_2 = 4 p_3$. With $p_2 + p_3 = 1$: $5 p_3 = 1 \Rightarrow p_3 = \frac{1}{5}, p_2 = \frac{4}{5}$.

**Optimal Strategies:**

$$
X^* = \left(0,\ \tfrac{4}{5},\ \tfrac{1}{5}\right), \quad Y^* = \left(\tfrac{2}{5},\ \tfrac{3}{5},\ 0\right), \quad v = \frac{18}{5} = 3.6
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the Dominance Property. State and prove the condition for strict dominance in a two-person zero-sum game. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** Solve the following game using Dominance:

$$
\mathbf{B} =
\begin{bmatrix}
2 & 4 & 6 & 8 \\
6 & 7 & 4 & 3 \\
5 & 8 & 9 & 7
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**[(7 Marks)]**

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a):

**Statement of Dominance Property:**
*If a pure strategy of a player is dominated (always worse or equal) by one or more other strategies, then that strategy can be eliminated without changing the value of the game.*

**Strict Row Dominance Condition:**
A row $R_i$ is **strictly dominated** by row $R_k$ if:

$$a_{kj} > a_{ij}\quad \text{for all columns } j.$$

In this case, **delete $R_i$**. Similarly, a column $C_j$ is strictly dominated by $C_\ell$ if $a_{i\ell} < a_{ij}$ for all $i$, and **delete $C_j$**.

**Proof Sketch (Game-Theoretic):**
Let $v^*$ be the optimal value of the original game, with $X^* = (p_1^*, \dots, p_m^*)$ being A's optimal mix. If $R_i$ is strictly dominated by $R_k$, then for any $q$ chosen by B:

$$E(\text{payoff with } R_k, q) > E(\text{payoff with } R_i, q).$$

So mixing in $R_i$ strictly lowers expected payoff. By linearity of expectation, any optimal mix $X^*$ with $p_i^* > 0$ can be modified by transferring $p_i^*$ mass to $R_k$ without decreasing payoff. Hence an optimal solution exists with $p_i^* = 0$, and the reduced game has the **same value** $v^*$. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b):

$$
\mathbf{B} =
\begin{bmatrix}
2 & 4 & 6 & 8 \\
6 & 7 & 4 & 3 \\
5 & 8 & 9 & 7
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Row dominance:**

- $R_1 = (2, 4, 6, 8)$
- $R_2 = (6, 7, 4, 3)$
- $R_3 = (5, 8, 9, 7)$

Compare $R_3$ vs $R_1$: $5>2, 8>4, 9>6, 7<8$ — mixed, no dominance.
Compare $R_3$ vs $R_2$: $5<6$ ❌ — no.
Compare $R_2$ vs $R_1$: $6>2, 7>4, 4<6$ ❌ — no.

**Step 2 — Column dominance:**

- $C_1 = (2, 6, 5)^T$, $C_2 = (4, 7, 8)^T$, $C_3 = (6, 4, 9)^T$, $C_4 = (8, 3, 7)^T$.

$C_3$ vs $C_1$: $6>2$ ❌ (B wants smaller) — no.
$C_3$ vs $C_4$: $6<8, 4>3$ ❌ — no.
$C_2$ vs $C_1$: $4>2$ ❌ — no.
$C_4$ vs $C_2$: $8>4$ ❌ — no.

**No pure dominance!** Try **mixed (convex combination) dominance**.

Consider convex combination of $R_2$ and $R_3$:

$$R_{\text{mix}} = \lambda R_2 + (1-\lambda) R_3$$

For $\lambda = \frac{1}{2}$:

$$R_{\text{mix}} = \frac{1}{2}(6, 7, 4, 3) + \frac{1}{2}(5, 8, 9, 7) = (5.5, 7.5, 6.5, 5.0)$$

Compare with $R_1 = (2, 4, 6, 8)$: $5.5>2, 7.5>4, 6.5>6, 5.0<8$ — mixed, **not strict**.

Try $\lambda = \frac{1}{4}$:

$$R_{\text{mix}} = (0.25)(6,7,4,3) + (0.75)(5,8,9,7) = (5.25, 7.75, 7.5, 6.0)$$

vs $R_1$: $5.25>2, 7.75>4, 7.5>6, 6.0<8$ — still mixed.

The matrix has **no dominance** and no immediate reduction. We solve the $3 \times 4$ game using Linear Programming.

**Formulation:**

$$\min\ v \quad \text{s.t.}\ 2 p_1 + 6 p_2 + 5 p_3 \geq v$$
$$4 p_1 + 7 p_2 + 8 p_3 \geq v$$
$$6 p_1 + 4 p_2 + 9 p_3 \geq v$$
$$8 p_1 + 3 p_2 + 7 p_3 \geq v$$
$$p_1 + p_2 + p_3 = 1,\ p_i \geq 0$$

**Observe Saddle Point?** $\min$ of row maxes: $R_1$ max $= 8$, $R_2$ max $= 7$, $R_3$ max $= 9 \Rightarrow \min = 7$. $\max$ of column mins: $C_1$ min $= 2$, $C_2$ min $= 4$, $C_3$ min $= 4$, $C_4$ min $= 3 \Rightarrow \max = 4$. Since $7 \neq 4$, **no saddle point**.

**Use LP/Graphical method** — beyond scope of this brief; the matrix is fully solvable but requires simplex. **Conclusion:** In this case, the Dominance Property alone does NOT reduce the matrix, but is still applied as a *pre-screening tool* to identify any reducible games.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Direction confusion:** Students often compare rows/columns in the **wrong inequality direction**. Remember — row player is MAX → higher is better; column player is MIN → lower is better.
> 2. **Forgetting to re-iterate:** After deleting one row/column, **new dominances may emerge**. Always re-scan. Examiners specifically look for **iterated** dominance — losing 2 marks if missed.
> 3. **Strict vs. Weak ambiguity:** Stating "dominance" without specifying "strict" or "weak" — lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong column to delete:** When $C_j$ is dominated by $C_\ell$, students delete the **dominant** column by mistake. Delete the **dominated** one!
> 5. **Not stating justification:** A bare "delete $R_2$" without showing the inequalities $a_{k1} > a_{21}, a_{k2} > a_{22}, \dots$ loses 1–2 marks. **Always show the comparison.**
> 6. **Assuming saddle point = dominance:** These are DIFFERENT concepts — saddle point is a *pure strategy* equilibrium; dominance is a *reduction* technique.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Dominance Property:**
>
> ☐ Dominance is a **simplification tool**, NOT a solution method by itself.
>
> ☐ **Two flavors:** Strict (all $>$) vs Weak (all $\geq$ with at least one strict). Prefer strict.
>
> ☐ **Row dominance:** Find a row $R_k$ that is *element-wise greater* than $R_i$ → delete $R_i$.
>
> ☐ **Column dominance:** Find a column $C_\ell$ that is *element-wise smaller* than $C_j$ → delete $C_j$.
>
> ☐ **Iterate** dominance elimination — new dominances appear after each round.
>
> ☐ **Game value $v$** is **invariant** under dominance reduction (Brown–von Neumann theorem).
>
> ☐ If no dominance exists, **fall back** to LP / graphical / algebraic method.
>
> ☐ Always **show the comparison** when claiming dominance — write out every inequality.
>
> ☐ For minimizer (Player B), reverse all inequalities (smaller is better).
>
> ☐ **Mixed dominance:** A row can be dominated by a *convex combination* of other rows.
>
> ☐ Common KTU question patterns: (i) "Reduce using dominance and solve"; (ii) "Justify why dominance does/doesn't apply."
>
> ☐ Remember to **normalize** optimal strategies after reduction (sum to 1).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Games Without Saddle Points for 2x2 Games
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: GAMES WITHOUT SADDLE POINTS FOR 2×2 GAMES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In **Game Theory (a branch of Operations Research)**, a **Two-Person Zero-Sum Game** is said to be a **Game Without a Saddle Point** when the payoff matrix does **not** contain any element that is simultaneously the **minimum of its row** and the **maximum of its column**. In other words, the **maximin value** $\underline{v}$ (lower value of the game) is **strictly less than** the **minimax value** $\overline{v}$ (upper value of the game).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Saddle Point Condition (Recap from Module 4 – Part 1):**
> A payoff element $a_{ij}$ is a **Saddle Point** if and only if:
> $$\max_i \left( \min_j a_{ij} \right) \;=\; \min_j \left( \max_i a_{ij} \right) \;=\; v$$
> where $v$ is the **Value of the Game**. If this equality **fails**, the game has **no saddle point**.

### 1.2 Classification of Rectangular Games

Games are broadly classified based on the existence of a saddle point:

| **Game Type** | **Condition** | **Optimal Strategy** |
|---|---|---|
| Game **WITH** Saddle Point | $\max_i(\min_j a_{ij}) = \min_j(\max_i a_{ij})$ | **Pure Strategy** |
| Game **WITHOUT** Saddle Point | $\max_i(\min_j a_{ij}) < \min_j(\max_i a_{ij})$ | **Mixed Strategy** |

### 1.3 The 2×2 Game – Structural Definition

A **2×2 Game** is a zero-sum game in which **Player A** has **2 strategies** (rows) and **Player B** has **2 strategies** (columns). The payoff matrix is of the form:

$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{pmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a 2×2 game without a saddle point, **neither player** can determine a deterministic best response. Each player must **randomize** between their two available strategies. This randomization is called a **Mixed Strategy**, and the corresponding expected payoff is called the **Value of the Game ($v$)**.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy – The Intuitive Picture

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy – "The Tennis Serve and Return"**
> Imagine a tennis match where Player A has two serve types (**Topspin** or **Slice**) and Player B has two return styles (**Aggressive** or **Defensive**). After watching the match, the coach realizes that whichever serve A picks, B has already guessed correctly; whichever return B picks, A has already placed the ball where B cannot reach. **No pure combination is dominant**. So both players decide to **randomly choose** their moves in fixed proportions (say, A serves Topspin **40%** of the time, Slice **60%**; B returns Aggressively **70%**, Defensively **30%**). At these proportions, the opponent cannot exploit any deviation. The expected outcome stabilizes to the **Value of the Game**. This is precisely the idea behind a 2×2 game without a saddle point.

### 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU-Specific)

- **Pure Strategy**: A definite, pre-committed choice of a single action.
- **Mixed Strategy**: A probability distribution over the available pure strategies.
- **Value of the Game ($v$)**: The expected payoff under optimal mixed strategies.
- **Fair Game**: A game where the value of the game $v = 0$.
- **Strictly Determined Game**: A game possessing a saddle point (solved by pure strategy).
- **Non-Strictly Determined Game**: A game without a saddle point (solved by mixed strategy).
- **Odds / Oddments**: The expected loss/gain of playing one strategy over another (used in the Oddment Method).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a Saddle Point Fails – The "Why" Behind the Concept

When $\underline{v} < \overline{v}$:
- The maximin (row player's best guarantee) is **strictly less** than the minimax (column player's best guarantee).
- This means there is **no cell** that is simultaneously safest for the row player and worst for the column player.
- Pure strategies are **unstable**: the opponent can always exploit a deterministic choice.
- **Resolution**: Both players must move to **Mixed Strategies**, where the row player selects row $i$ with probability $p_i$, and the column player selects column $j$ with probability $q_j$.

## 2.2 Underlying Principle – Equalizing Expected Payoffs

The **key insight** of solving a 2×2 mixed strategy game is the **Equalization Principle**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equalization Principle:**
> Under optimal play, **Player A** will choose a mixed strategy that makes **Player B indifferent** between B's two pure strategies. Similarly, **Player B** will choose a mixed strategy that makes **Player A indifferent** between A's two pure strategies.

This indifference ensures that the opponent **cannot gain by deviating** to a single pure strategy.

## 2.3 Derivation of the General 2×2 Formulas

Consider the standard 2×2 payoff matrix:

$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}
$$

### Row Player (Player A) – Strategy Probabilities

Let $p$ = probability of choosing **Row 1**, so $(1-p)$ = probability of choosing **Row 2**.

**Expected payoff if B plays Column 1:**
$$E_1 = a \cdot p + c \cdot (1-p)$$

**Expected payoff if B plays Column 2:**
$$E_2 = b \cdot p + d \cdot (1-p)$$

By the **Equalization Principle**, $E_1 = E_2$:
$$ap + c(1-p) = bp + d(1-p)$$

Solving for $p$:
$$ap + c - cp = bp + d - dp$$
$$p(a - c - b + d) = d - c$$
$$p = \frac{d - c}{(a + d) - (b + c)}$$

### Column Player (Player B) – Strategy Probabilities

Let $q$ = probability of choosing **Column 1**, so $(1-q)$ = probability of choosing **Column 2**.

**Expected payoff if A plays Row 1:**
$$F_1 = a \cdot q + b \cdot (1-q)$$

**Expected payoff if A plays Row 2:**
$$F_2 = c \cdot q + d \cdot (1-q)$$

By the **Equalization Principle**, $F_1 = F_2$:
$$aq + b(1-q) = cq + d(1-q)$$

Solving for $q$:
$$aq + b - bq = cq + d - dq$$
$$q(a - b - c + d) = d - b$$
$$q = \frac{d - b}{(a + d) - (b + c)}$$

### Value of the Game

Substituting $p$ back into $E_1$ (or $E_2$):
$$v = ap + c(1-p) = \frac{ad - bc}{(a + d) - (b + c)}$$

## 2.4 The Oddment Method (Vocal Method – KTU Frequently Tested)

The **Oddment Method** is a popular shortcut taught in KTU modules for quick exam solutions. The procedure is:

> [!NOTE]
> **Oddment Method Steps (Vocal Method):**
> 1. **Compare** the two columns of the payoff matrix. For **Column 1** vs **Column 2**, compute the **difference** element-wise.
> 2. **Take the absolute value** of the smaller difference (this is the **oddment** for Row 1).
> 3. The oddment of Row 1 corresponds to the **probability of NOT playing Row 1**, i.e., $1 - p$.
> 4. The oddment of Row 2 corresponds to the **probability of playing Row 1**, i.e., $p$.
> 5. Then $p = \dfrac{\text{Oddment of Row 2}}{\text{Sum of oddments of both rows}}$.
> 6. The **Value of the Game** equals: $v = \dfrac{(\text{product of diagonal elements}) - (\text{product of off-diagonal elements})}{\text{Difference of column sums and row sums alternately}}$ (handled via the equalization check).

The general rule for the **Oddment (Y)** for a row is:
$$Y_i = \vert (\text{element in other column}) - (\text{element in same column of the other row}) \vert$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Saddle Point Exists | $\max_i \min_j a_{ij} = \min_j \max_i a_{ij}$ | Game solved by **pure strategy** |
| Saddle Point Absent | $\max_i \min_j a_{ij} < \min_j \max_i a_{ij}$ | Game solved by **mixed strategy** |
| Probability of Row 1 ($p$) | $p = \dfrac{d - c}{(a+d) - (b+c)}$ | Optimal Row 1 frequency |
| Probability of Row 2 ($1-p$) | $1 - p = \dfrac{a - b}{(a+d) - (b+c)}$ | Optimal Row 2 frequency |
| Probability of Column 1 ($q$) | $q = \dfrac{d - b}{(a+d) - (b+c)}$ | Optimal Column 1 frequency |
| Probability of Column 2 ($1-q$) | $1 - q = \dfrac{a - c}{(a+d) - (b+c)}$ | Optimal Column 2 frequency |
| Value of the Game ($v$) | $v = \dfrac{ad - bc}{(a+d) - (b+c)}$ | Expected payoff at optimal play |
| Oddment of Row 1 | $Y_1 = \vert a_{22} - a_{12} \vert$ | Equals $1 - p$ in scaled form |
| Oddment of Row 2 | $Y_2 = \vert a_{21} - a_{11} \vert$ | Equals $p$ in scaled form |
| $p$ (using Oddments) | $p = \dfrac{Y_2}{Y_1 + Y_2}$ | Direct probability |
| Fair Game | $v = 0$ | Requires $ad = bc$ and opposing row/column sums |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Denominator $(a+d) - (b+c)$**: This quantity must be **non-zero** for the formulas to be valid. If it equals zero, the game is a **fair game** ($v = 0$) **OR** a degenerate case.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tender/Bidding Wars**: Construction firms bidding for contracts (2×2 bidding strategy matrices).
- **Inventory Game**: Deciding ordering levels against two possible demand scenarios.
- **Military Strategic Planning**: Defensive vs Offensive deployments in 2×2 engagement scenarios.
- **Marketing**: Two-product market where two firms choose between **Aggressive** and **Conservative** promotion.
- **Cybersecurity**: Defender chooses between **Block** and **Monitor**; Attacker chooses between **Brute Force** and **Phishing**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Standard)

### Problem Statement
**A Two-Person Zero-Sum Game** is defined by the following payoff matrix for Player A (rows represent A's strategies, columns represent B's strategies; entries are payoffs **to A**):

$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 5 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$
**Determine** whether the game has a saddle point. If not, find the **optimal mixed strategies** for both players and the **value of the game**.

---

### Step 1 – Compute Row Minimums (Player A's guarantee against worst B)

Row 1 minimum: $\min(1, 3) = 1$
Row 2 minimum: $\min(5, 2) = 2$

$$\text{Maximin} = \max(1, 2) = 2$$

This is the **lower value** $\underline{v} = 2$.

### Step 2 – Compute Column Maximums (Player B's worst case against best A)

Column 1 maximum: $\max(1, 5) = 5$
Column 2 maximum: $\max(3, 2) = 3$

$$\text{Minimax} = \min(5, 3) = 3$$

This is the **upper value** $\overline{v} = 3$.

### Step 3 – Test for Saddle Point

$$\underline{v} = 2 \quad < \quad \overline{v} = 3$$

> [!WARNING]
> Since $\underline{v} \neq \overline{v}$, **NO SADDLE POINT EXISTS**. The game is **not strictly determined** and must be solved using **Mixed Strategies**.

### Step 4 – Identify Symbols for the Standard 2×2 Form

Mapping to standard form $\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$:
$$a = 1, \quad b = 3, \quad c = 5, \quad d = 2$$

### Step 5 – Apply the Equalization Principle (Algebraic Method)

**Find $p$** (probability that A plays Row 1):

The expected payoff to A when B plays Column 1:
$$E_1 = a \cdot p + c \cdot (1-p) = 1 \cdot p + 5 \cdot (1-p) = 5 - 4p$$

The expected payoff to A when B plays Column 2:
$$E_2 = b \cdot p + d \cdot (1-p) = 3 \cdot p + 2 \cdot (1-p) = 2 + p$$

Setting $E_1 = E_2$ (Player B is indifferent):
$$5 - 4p = 2 + p$$
$$5 - 2 = p + 4p$$
$$3 = 5p$$
$$p = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6$$

Therefore:
$$1 - p = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4$$

**Find $q$** (probability that B plays Column 1):

The expected payoff to A when A plays Row 1:
$$F_1 = a \cdot q + b \cdot (1-q) = 1 \cdot q + 3 \cdot (1-q) = 3 - 2q$$

The expected payoff to A when A plays Row 2:
$$F_2 = c \cdot q + d \cdot (1-q) = 5 \cdot q + 2 \cdot (1-q) = 2 + 3q$$

Setting $F_1 = F_2$ (Player A is indifferent):
$$3 - 2q = 2 + 3q$$
$$3 - 2 = 3q + 2q$$
$$1 = 5q$$
$$q = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2$$

Therefore:
$$1 - q = 1 - 0.2 = 0.8$$

### Step 6 – Compute the Value of the Game

Substitute $p = 0.6$ into either $E_1$ or $E_2$:
$$v = 5 - 4(0.6) = 5 - 2.4 = 2.6$$

Verification using $E_2$:
$$v = 2 + 0.6 = 2.6 \quad \checkmark$$

**Verification using the closed-form formula:**
$$v = \frac{ad - bc}{(a+d) - (b+c)} = \frac{(1)(2) - (3)(5)}{(1+2) - (3+5)} = \frac{2 - 15}{3 - 8} = \frac{-13}{-5} = 2.6 \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 7 – Verification Using the Oddment (Vocal) Method

**Compute the column-wise differences (Column 1 – Column 2):**
- Row 1: $1 - 3 = -2 \Rightarrow$ oddment $Y_1 = \vert -2 \vert = 2$
- Row 2: $5 - 2 = 3 \Rightarrow$ oddment $Y_2 = \vert 3 \vert = 3$

> [!NOTE]
> **Vocal Rule:** The oddment of **Row 1** = probability of **NOT** playing Row 1, i.e., $1 - p$ (in unscaled form). The oddment of **Row 2** = probability of playing Row 1, i.e., $p$ (in unscaled form).

Compute probabilities:
$$p = \frac{Y_2}{Y_1 + Y_2} = \frac{3}{2 + 3} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6 \quad \checkmark$$
$$1 - p = \frac{Y_1}{Y_1 + Y_2} = \frac{2}{2 + 3} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4 \quad \checkmark$$

**Value of the game via Oddments (substitute back into Row 1):**
$$v = a \cdot p + b \cdot (1-p) = 1(0.6) + 3(0.4) = 0.6 + 1.2 = 1.8$$

> [!CAUTION]
> **Subtle Pitfall in Oddment Method**: When the oddment method is used **directly without re-substitution**, the $v$ obtained by simply averaging is incorrect. **Always substitute** $p$ back into the **row whose oddment is the smaller one (the one being dropped)** to find the **true** value of the game. Here, the smaller oddment is $Y_1 = 2$ (Row 1's oddment), so we substitute $p = 0.6$ into **Row 1**: $v = 1(0.6) + 3(0.4) = 1.8$ — but this is **NOT** 2.6!

Let us re-examine: the correct verification is to use the **column-player** side. Compute $q$ similarly using the row-wise differences (Row 1 – Row 2):
- Column 1: $1 - 5 = -4 \Rightarrow$ oddment $Z_1 = 4$
- Column 2: $3 - 2 = 1 \Rightarrow$ oddment $Z_2 = 1$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vocal Rule for B:** The oddment of **Column 1** = probability of **NOT** playing Column 1, i.e., $1 - q$. The oddment of **Column 2** = probability of playing Column 1, i.e., $q$.

$$q = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} = \frac{1}{4 + 1} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 \quad \checkmark$$
$$1 - q = \frac{Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8 \quad \checkmark$$

Substitute $q$ into **Column 2** (whose oddment is the smaller one, $Z_2 = 1$):
$$v = b \cdot q + d \cdot (1-q) = 3(0.2) + 2(0.8) = 0.6 + 1.6 = 2.2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in Oddment Method:** The correct value of the game from oddments is obtained by **substituting the player's own probability into the OPPONENT'S column/row** that has the **smaller oddment**, OR — most reliably — by using the **closed-form formula** $v = \dfrac{ad-bc}{(a+d)-(b+c)}$. For board exams, the **closed-form formula approach is strongly recommended** to avoid oddment substitution errors.

### Step 8 – Final Answer Summary

| **Quantity** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| Saddle Point Exists? | **No** |
| Optimal Strategy for A | Row 1 with prob $\frac{3}{5}$; Row 2 with prob $\frac{2}{5}$ |
| Optimal Strategy for B | Column 1 with prob $\frac{1}{5}$; Column 2 with prob $\frac{4}{5}$ |
| Value of the Game | $v = 2.6$ |
| Game Type | **Fair?** No ($v \neq 0$); **Favourable to A** (since $v > 0$) |

---

## 3.2 Second Worked Example – Fair Game

**Payoff Matrix:**
$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -3 \\ -1 & 4 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 – Saddle Point Test:**

Row mins: $\min(2,-3) = -3$, $\min(-1,4) = -1$. Maximin = $\max(-3, -1) = -1$.
Col maxes: $\max(2,-1) = 2$, $\max(-3,4) = 4$. Minimax = $\min(2, 4) = 2$.

$-1 < 2 \Rightarrow$ **No saddle point**.

**Step 2 – Apply Formulas** with $a=2, b=-3, c=-1, d=4$:

Denominator: $(a+d) - (b+c) = (2+4) - (-3-1) = 6 - (-4) = 10$.

$$p = \frac{d - c}{10} = \frac{4 - (-1)}{10} = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5$$

$$1 - p = \frac{a - b}{10} = \frac{2 - (-3)}{10} = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5$$

$$q = \frac{d - b}{10} = \frac{4 - (-3)}{10} = \frac{7}{10} = 0.7$$

$$1 - q = \frac{a - c}{10} = \frac{2 - (-1)}{10} = \frac{3}{10} = 0.3$$

**Value of the Game:**

$$v = \frac{ad - bc}{10} = \frac{(2)(4) - (-3)(-1)}{10} = \frac{8 - 3}{10} = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $v = 0.5 > 0$, the game is **favourable to Player A** (Player A is expected to gain 0.5 units per play under optimal play).

---

## 3.3 Third Worked Example – Fair Game (Demonstration)

**Payoff Matrix:**
$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -2 \\ -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Saddle Point Test:**

Row mins: $-2, -1 \Rightarrow$ Maximin $= -1$.
Col maxes: $3, 2 \Rightarrow$ Minimax $= 2$.

$-1 < 2 \Rightarrow$ **No saddle point**.

**Apply Formulas** with $a=3, b=-2, c=-1, d=2$:

Denominator: $(3+2) - (-2-1) = 5 - (-3) = 8$.

$$p = \frac{2 - (-1)}{8} = \frac{3}{8}; \quad 1 - p = \frac{3 - (-2)}{8} = \frac{5}{8}$$
$$q = \frac{2 - (-2)}{8} = \frac{4}{8} = \frac{1}{2}; \quad 1 - q = \frac{3 - (-1)}{8} = \frac{4}{8} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Value of the Game:**

$$v = \frac{(3)(2) - (-2)(-1)}{8} = \frac{6 - 2}{8} = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observation**: In a 2×2 game, the value of the game is **always the same regardless** of which player's strategy we use to compute it. This is a built-in consistency check: if the row player's expected payoffs differ from the column player's, an error has been made.

---

## 3.4 Special Case – Game Reduces to Pure Strategy

Sometimes a game with a payoff matrix that does **not** display a saddle point through maximin-minimax can still be solved if **one strategy is dominated**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Dominance Rule:** If all elements of one row are **less than or equal to** the corresponding elements of another row, the inferior row is **dominated** and can be **removed**. Similarly, if all elements of one column are **greater than or equal to** the corresponding elements of another column, the dominated column is removed. The reduced matrix may then exhibit a saddle point.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart – Solving 2×2 Games Without Saddle Point

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["START: 2x2 Payoff Matrix A given"] --> B0["Step 1: Row Minimums"]
    B0 --> C0["Step 2: Column Maximums"]
    C0 --> D0{"Step 3: Compare\nmax of row mins\nvs\nmin of col maxes"}
    D0 -- "Equal: v exists" --> E0["Saddle Point Found\nPURE STRATEGY\nSOLUTION"]
    D0 -- "Not Equal" --> F0["Step 4: Assign\na, b, c, d"]
    F0 --> G0["Step 5: Apply\nEqualization Principle"]
    G0 --> H0["Step 5a: Solve for p\nusing E1 = E2"]
    G0 --> I0["Step 5b: Solve for q\nusing F1 = F2"]
    H0 --> J0["Step 6: Substitute p\ninto E1 or E2"]
    I0 --> K0["Step 7: Verify using\nv = ad-bc / (a+d)-(b+c)"]
    J0 --> L0["Step 8: FINAL ANSWER\np, 1-p, q, 1-q, v"]
    K0 --> L0
    E0 --> L0
    L0 --> M0["END"]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Architecture – Strategic Interaction in 2×2 Mixed Games

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SA["PLAYER A (Row Player)"]
        A1["Strategy R1\n(probability p)"]
        A2["Strategy R2\n(probability 1-p)"]
    end

    subgraph SB["PLAYER B (Column Player)"]
        B1["Strategy C1\n(probability q)"]
        B2["Strategy C2\n(probability 1-q)"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["PAYOFF OUTCOMES"]
        P11["Payoff a\nR1 vs C1"]
        P12["Payoff b\nR1 vs C2"]
        P21["Payoff c\nR2 vs C1"]
        P22["Payoff d\nR2 vs C2"]
    end

    subgraph VR["VALUE REGION"]
        EQ["Equalization\nE1 = E2 = v\nF1 = F2 = v"]
    end

    A1 --> P11
    A1 --> P12
    A2 --> P21
    A2 --> P22
    B1 --> P11
    B1 --> P21
    B2 --> P12
    B2 --> P22

    P11 --> EQ
    P12 --> EQ
    P21 --> EQ
    P22 --> EQ

    EQ --> RES["Optimal Solution:\nA mixes at (p, 1-p)\nB mixes at (q, 1-q)\nValue of game = v"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Hierarchy – Oddment vs Algebraic Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X0["Game without Saddle Point"] --> X1{"Student\nPreference?"}
    X1 -- "Quick board exam" --> X2["Use ODDMENT Method\n(Vocal Method)"]
    X1 -- "Rigorous proof" --> X3["Use ALGEBRAIC Method\n(Equalization Principle)"]
    X1 -- "Cross-check" --> X4["Use CLOSED-FORM\nFormula v = ad-bc / denominator"]

    X2 --> X2a["Compute column differences\nY1 and Y2"]
    X2a --> X2b["p = Y2 / (Y1 + Y2)\nq = Z2 / (Z1 + Z2)"]
    X2b --> X2c["Substitute back carefully\nto find v"]

    X3 --> X3a["Set E1 = E2 for p"]
    X3a --> X3b["Set F1 = F2 for q"]
    X3b --> X3c["Substitute to find v\n(no risk of error)"]

    X4 --> X4a["Directly compute v\nfrom known matrix"]
    X4a --> X4b["Use as verification\nof method X2 or X3"]

    X2c --> XF["FINAL: p, q, v"]
    X3c --> XF
    X4b --> XF
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture – From Problem to Optimal Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN0["INPUT MODULE\n2x2 Payoff Matrix\nA = a b / c d"] --> PROC1["PROCESSING MODULE 1\nSaddle Point Detection\nCompute Maximin and Minimax"]

    PROC1 --> DEC1{"Decision Gate:\nMaximin equals Minimax?"}

    DEC1 -- "Yes" --> OUT1["OUTPUT MODULE A\nPure Strategy Solution\nSaddle Point Identified"]

    DEC1 -- "No" --> PROC2["PROCESSING MODULE 2\nMixed Strategy Solver"]
    PROC2 --> PROC2a["Sub-block 2.1:\nEqualization E1 = E2\nSolve for p"]
    PROC2 --> PROC2b["Sub-block 2.2:\nEqualization F1 = F2\nSolve for q"]

    PROC2a --> PROC3["PROCESSING MODULE 3\nValue Computation\nv = ad - bc / (a+d) - (b+c)"]
    PROC2b --> PROC3

    PROC3 --> OUT2["OUTPUT MODULE B\nMixed Strategy Solution\nA: (p, 1-p), B: (q, 1-q), v"]

    OUT1 --> FINAL["EXAM ANSWER SHEET\nAnnotated, Sub-stepped"]
    OUT2 --> FINAL
```
