# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q1.** Define the following terms in the context of a two-person zero-sum game:
**(a)** Saddle point
**(b)** Value of the game
**(c)** Mixed strategy

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Saddle Point:** A payoff element $a_{ij}$ in the payoff matrix is called a **Saddle Point** if it is simultaneously the **minimum of its row** and the **maximum of its column**. Mathematically:

$$a_{ij} = \min_k a_{ik} = \max_k a_{kj}$$

A saddle point, when it exists, gives the value of the game and the optimal pure strategies for both players.

**(b) Value of the Game ($v$):** The **Value of the Game** is the **expected payoff** to the row player when both players follow their **optimal strategies** (pure or mixed). It is the **guaranteed outcome** under rational play:

$$v = \max_i \min_j a_{ij} = \min_j \max_i a_{ij} \quad \text{(when saddle point exists)}$$

If the game has no saddle point, $v$ lies between the maximin and minimax values.

**(c) Mixed Strategy:** A **Mixed Strategy** is a probability distribution over the set of pure strategies available to a player. For a 2×2 game, Player A plays Row 1 with probability $p$ and Row 2 with probability $(1-p)$; Player B plays Column 1 with probability $q$ and Column 2 with probability $(1-q)$. Mixed strategies are adopted when the game has **no saddle point**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q2.** What is the difference between a **strictly determined** and a **non-strictly determined** game? When is a game said to be **fair**?

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Strictly Determined Game** | **Non-Strictly Determined Game** |
|---|---|---|
| Saddle Point | **Exists** | **Does not exist** |
| Maximin = Minimax | Yes | No (maximin < minimax) |
| Optimal Strategy | **Pure Strategy** | **Mixed Strategy** |
| Solution Method | Direct row-column analysis | Equalization / Oddment / Linear programming |

A game is said to be **Fair** if the **Value of the Game** $v = 0$. In a fair game, neither player has an inherent advantage; the expected outcome under optimal play is **zero payoff**. A 2×2 game is fair when:

$$ad - bc = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad ad = bc$$

For example, the matrix $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 6 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ is fair because $(2)(3) = 6 = (4)(6)$.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) – Choice Option 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q.A.** Consider the two-person zero-sum game with the following payoff matrix for Player A:

$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} 8 & 3 \\ 4 & -1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Determine whether the game has a saddle point. Justify your answer using the maximin-minimax principle.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Assuming no saddle point, find the optimal mixed strategies for both players and the value of the game.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 – Row Minimums:**

Row 1: $\min(8, 3) = 3$
Row 2: $\min(4, -1) = -1$

$$\text{Maximin } \underline{v} = \max(3, -1) = 3$$

**Step 2 – Column Maximums:**

Column 1: $\max(8, 4) = 8$
Column 2: $\max(3, -1) = 3$

$$\text{Minimax } \overline{v} = \min(8, 3) = 3$$

**Step 3 – Comparison:**

$$\underline{v} = 3 = \overline{v} = 3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Inference:** Since $\underline{v} = \overline{v} = 3$, the game **HAS a saddle point**. The element $a_{12} = 3$ is the saddle point (it is the minimum of Row 1 and the maximum of Column 2).

> **Conclusion:** The game is **strictly determined**; Player A should play **Row 1** and Player B should play **Column 2**. The value of the game is $v = 3$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

Since the game **has a saddle point** at $a_{12} = 3$, the optimal strategies are **pure**:

- **Player A**: Play **Row 1** with probability 1, Row 2 with probability 0.
- **Player B**: Play **Column 2** with probability 1, Column 1 with probability 0.
- **Value of the game**: $v = 3$.

If, however, we **proceed to apply mixed-strategy formulas** (as some textbooks suggest for consistency), with $a = 8, b = 3, c = 4, d = -1$:

Denominator: $(a+d) - (b+c) = (8-1) - (3+4) = 7 - 7 = 0$.

> [!WARNING]
> The denominator equals **zero**, which mathematically **confirms** that the game has a saddle point (the mixed strategy formula breaks down). The pure-strategy answer is the only valid one.

> **Final Answer for Q.A:** Saddle point **exists at cell (1,2)** with value $v = 3$. Optimal: A plays Row 1, B plays Column 2.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Choice Option 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q.B.** Consider the following two-person zero-sum game with payoff matrix (for Player A):

$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ -2 & 6 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Show that the game has no saddle point. Hence, find the optimal mixed strategy for Player A.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Find the optimal mixed strategy for Player B and the value of the game. Verify your answer using the closed-form formula.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 – Saddle Point Test:**

Row 1 minimum: $\min(4, 2) = 2$
Row 2 minimum: $\min(-2, 6) = -2$

$$\text{Maximin } \underline{v} = \max(2, -2) = 2$$

Column 1 maximum: $\max(4, -2) = 4$
Column 2 maximum: $\max(2, 6) = 6$

$$\text{Minimax } \overline{v} = \min(4, 6) = 4$$

**Step 2 – Comparison:**

$$\underline{v} = 2 < \overline{v} = 4$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inference:** Since $\underline{v} \neq \overline{v}$, the game has **NO saddle point**. Mixed strategies are required.

**Step 3 – Assign Variables** ($a = 4, b = 2, c = -2, d = 6$):

**Step 4 – Apply Equalization Principle to find $p$ (Player A's mix):**

Expected payoff if B plays Column 1:
$$E_1 = ap + c(1-p) = 4p + (-2)(1-p) = 6p - 2$$

Expected payoff if B plays Column 2:
$$E_2 = bp + d(1-p) = 2p + 6(1-p) = 6 - 4p$$

Set $E_1 = E_2$:
$$6p - 2 = 6 - 4p$$
$$10p = 8$$
$$p = \frac{8}{10} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$$

$$1 - p = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2 = \frac{1}{5}$$

> **Optimal Strategy for Player A:** Row 1 with probability $\frac{4}{5}$, Row 2 with probability $\frac{1}{5}$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 – Apply Equalization Principle to find $q$ (Player B's mix):**

Expected payoff if A plays Row 1:
$$F_1 = aq + b(1-q) = 4q + 2(1-q) = 2q + 2$$

Expected payoff if A plays Row 2:
$$F_2 = cq + d(1-q) = -2q + 6(1-q) = 6 - 8q$$

Set $F_1 = F_2$:
$$2q + 2 = 6 - 8q$$
$$10q = 4$$
$$q = \frac{4}{10} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4$$

$$1 - q = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6 = \frac{3}{5}$$

> **Optimal Strategy for Player B:** Column 1 with probability $\frac{2}{5}$, Column 2 with probability $\frac{3}{5}$.

**Step 2 – Compute the Value of the Game:**

Substitute $p = 0.8$ into $E_1$:
$$v = 6(0.8) - 2 = 4.8 - 2 = 2.8$$

**Step 3 – Verification using the closed-form formula:**

$$v = \frac{ad - bc}{(a+d) - (b+c)} = \frac{(4)(6) - (2)(-2)}{(4+6) - (2-2)} = \frac{24 - (-4)}{10 - 0} = \frac{28}{10} = 2.8 \quad \checkmark$$

> **Final Answer for Q.B:**
> - Player A's strategy: $\left(p, 1-p\right) = \left(\frac{4}{5}, \frac{1}{5}\right)$
> - Player B's strategy: $\left(q, 1-q\right) = \left(\frac{2}{5}, \frac{3}{5}\right)$
> - **Value of the game:** $v = 2.8$ — **favourable to Player A**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (KTU Board Exam Pitfalls)**
>
> 1. **Misidentifying Saddle Point**: Students often confuse "minimum of row" with "maximum of column" or apply the conditions in the wrong order. **Always** compute row minimums first → then column maximums.
>
> 2. **Sign Errors in Mixed Strategy Formulas**: The denominator $(a+d) - (b+c)$ is **not** $(a+c)-(b+d)$. Mix-up of row/column indices causes silent sign errors.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Verify the Solution**: A 14-mark question **must** be verified by either the closed-form formula OR by substituting the probabilities back into all four expected payoffs and confirming they are equal. Without verification, the examiner typically deducts **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Confusing $p$ and $1-p$**: Some students write $p = \frac{a - b}{\text{denominator}}$ instead of $p = \frac{d - c}{\text{denominator}}$. Re-derive the formula from $E_1 = E_2$ every time to avoid this.
>
> 5. **Oddment Method Substitution Pitfall**: In the oddment (vocal) method, the value of the game must be obtained by **substituting into the row/column whose oddment is the SMALLER one** (i.e., the one being eliminated). Substituting into the wrong row/column gives the **opponent's** expected loss, not the value of the game. The closed-form formula is the **safest** path to full marks.
>
> 6. **Reporting Game Type Incorrectly**: Always state explicitly whether the game is **fair** ($v=0$), **favourable to A** ($v>0$), or **favourable to B** ($v<0$). This is a **1-mark** item often missed.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Games Without Saddle Points (2×2 Games)**

- [x] A **2×2 game** has 2 strategies per player; payoff matrix is $\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$.
- [x] **Saddle point exists** $\iff \max_i \min_j a_{ij} = \min_j \max_i a_{ij}$.
- [x] **No saddle point** $\iff \max_i \min_j a_{ij} < \min_j \max_i a_{ij}$ → use **mixed strategy**.
- [x] **Equalization Principle**: At optimal play, the opponent is **indifferent** between their pure strategies.
- [x] **Key formulas** for 2×2 mixed strategy:
  - $p = \dfrac{d - c}{(a+d) - (b+c)}$
  - $q = \dfrac{d - b}{(a+d) - (b+c)}$
  - $v = \dfrac{ad - bc}{(a+d) - (b+c)}$
- [x] **Denominator = 0** ⇒ Saddle point exists OR degenerate case; mixed formula is invalid.
- [x] **Oddment (Vocal) Method**:
  - Compute column differences (for $p$) and row differences (for $q$).
  - $p = \dfrac{\text{Oddment of Row 2}}{\text{Sum of oddments}}$
  - Substitute back into the row with the **smaller** oddment to get $v$.
- [x] **Fair game** ⇒ $v = 0$, equivalently $ad = bc$.
- [x] **Dominance property** may reduce a 2×2 game to a 1×1 or 1×2 trivial game.
- [x] **Always verify** your final value $v$ by substituting probabilities into the alternative expression (e.g., compute $v$ from both $E_1$ and $F_1$).
- [x] **Game classification** (mandatory 1-mark addition in KTU answer sheets):
  - $v > 0$ → **Favourable to A**
  - $v < 0$ → **Favourable to B**
  - $v = 0$ → **Fair Game**

---

**END OF NOTES — MODULE 4: GAMES WITHOUT SADDLE POINTS FOR 2×2 GAMES**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | PERUT746 Operations Research*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graphical Method for m x 2 and 2 x n Games
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT746 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Graphical Method for m x 2 and 2 x n Games # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In the theory of **Rectangular Games**, when the payoff matrix of a two-person zero-sum game is not square (i.e., $m \neq n$), a closed-form saddle-point may not exist. Two important subclasses are: > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition** > - **m × 2 Game**: A two-person zero-sum game in which the maximizer (Player A) has **$m$ pure strategies** and the minimizer (Player B) has **$2$ pure strategies**, with a payoff matrix of order $m \times 2$. > - **2 × n Game**: A two-person zero-sum game in which the maximizer (Player A) has **$2$ pure strategies** and the minimizer (Player B) has **$n$ pure strategies**, with a payoff matrix of order $2 \times n$. For these rectangular games, the **Graphical Method** is used to find the optimal mixed strategies and the value of the game without solving the full $(m+n) \times (m+n)$ Linear Programming (LP) tableau. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine you are a **sales manager (Player A)** deciding how to allocate your limited advertising budget across **$m$ different media channels**, while your competitor (Player B) can choose between only **2 pricing strategies** (Discount vs. No-Discount). - Your channels are too many to brute-force test, so you cannot exhaustively simulate every combination. - The **Graphical Method** is like sketching a quick **"risk-versus-reward" map** on graph paper: for each channel, you draw a straight line showing how much profit you earn as your competitor tilts between his two strategies. - The **upper boundary** of these lines is the worst your competitor can do to you for any given move; the *lowest point* of this boundary is the profit you can **guarantee** — your **optimal strategy**. - This is exactly the *minimax* principle made visual. ## 1.3 Why Graphical? — Motivation A general $m \times n$ game requires solving an LP with $(m+n)$ variables and $(m+n)$ constraints. For $m \times 2$ or $2 \times n$ games, the LP reduces to **2 variables**, which can be solved by **plotting lines on a 2-D plane** — a fast, exam-friendly technique favored in KTU board examinations. > [!NOTE] > The graphical method yields the **value of the game ($v$)**, the optimal strategy of the player with **2 strategies**, and reduces the problem to a 2-variable LP for the other player's strategy. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ### 2.1.1 Setup for m × 2 Game (Player B has 2 strategies) Let Player A's mixed strategy be $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m)$ with $\sum x_i = 1, \; x_i \ge 0$. Let Player B's mixed strategy be $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2)$ with $y_1 + y_2 = 1, \; y_j \ge 0$. **Expected Payoff when A plays pure strategy $i$ and B plays $(y_1, y_2)$:** $$ E_i(y_1) = a_{i1} y_1 + a_{i2} y_2 = a_{i1} y_1 + a_{i2}(1 - y_1) $$ > This is a **linear function of $y_1$** (since $y_2 = 1 - y_1$). - Player B (the minimizer) chooses $y_1$ to **minimize** the expected payoff. - Player A (the maximizer) wants to **maximize the guaranteed (worst-case) payoff**. **Value of the game:** $$ v = \min_{0 \le y_1 \le 1} \left[ \max_{i = 1, \ldots, m} E_i(y_1) \right] $$ ### 2.1.2 Setup for 2 × n Game (Player A has 2 strategies) Let A's strategy be $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)$ and B's strategy be $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, \ldots, y_n)$. **Expected Payoff when B plays pure strategy $j$ and A plays $(x_1, x_2)$:** $$ C_j(x_1) = a_{1j} x_1 + a_{2j}(1 - x_1) $$ **Value of the game:** $$ v = \max_{0 \le x_1 \le 1} \left[ \min_{j = 1, \ldots, n} C_j(x_1) \right] $$ ## 2.2 Algorithmic Steps > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Step-by-Step Algorithm** **Algorithm A — For m × 2 Game:** 1. Identify the $m \times 2$ payoff matrix with entries $a_{ij}$, $i = 1, \ldots, m$; $j = 1, 2$. 2. For each row $i$, write $E_i(y_1) = a_{i1} y_1 + a_{i2}(1 - y_1)$. 3. **Plot** $m$ straight lines $E_i(y_1)$ on a graph with $y_1$ on the horizontal axis (range $[0, 1]$) and $v$ (payoff) on the vertical axis. 4. Draw the **Upper Envelope** — the locus of the pointwise maximum of all $m$ lines. 5. Identify the **Lowest Point** of the upper envelope. Read off $v^*$ (the $v$-coordinate) and $y_1^*$ (the $y_1$-coordinate). - $v^*$ = Value of the game. - $y_1^*$ = Optimal probability with which B plays Strategy 1; $y_2^* = 1 - y_1^*$. 6. Identify the rows (A's strategies) whose lines meet at $(y_1^*, v^*)$. These form A's **support**. 7. Solve the linear system: $$\sum_{j=1}^{2} a_{ij} y_j^* = v^* \quad \text{for every } i \text{ in A's support}$$ together with $\sum x_i = 1, \; x_i \ge 0$ to obtain $\mathbf{x}^*$. **Algorithm B — For 2 × n Game:** 1. Identify the $2 \times n$ payoff matrix with entries $a_{ij}$, $i = 1, 2$; $j = 1, \ldots, n$. 2. For each column $j$, write $C_j(x_1) = a_{1j} x_1 + a_{2j}(1 - x_1)$. 3. **Plot** $n$ straight lines $C_j(x_1)$ with $x_1 \in [0, 1]$ on the horizontal axis and $v$ on the vertical axis. 4. Draw the **Lower Envelope** — the locus of the pointwise minimum of all $n$ lines. 5. Identify the **Highest Point** of the lower envelope. Read off $v^*$ and $x_1^*$. - $v^*$ = Value of the game. - $x_1^*$ = Optimal probability with which A plays Strategy 1; $x_2^* = 1 - x_1^*$. 6. Identify B's strategies (columns) whose lines meet at $(x_1^*, v^*)$ — these form B's **support**. 7. Solve $\sum_{i=1}^{2} a_{ij} x_i^* = v^*$ for every $j$ in B's support, together with $\sum y_j = 1$. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes all key formulas. Note: vertical bars in absolute value expressions are replaced with $\vert$ for LaTeX compatibility. | # | Formula / Condition | Mathematical Form | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Expected payoff (m×2) | $E_i(y_1) = a_{i1} y_1 + a_{i2}(1 - y_1)$ | Line equation for each row | | 2 | Expected payoff (2×n) | $C_j(x_1) = a_{1j} x_1 + a_{2j}(1 - x_1)$ | Line equation for each column | | 3 | m×2 value | $v = \min_{y_1} \max_{i} E_i(y_1)$ | Minimax on upper envelope | | 4 | 2×n value | $v = \max_{x_1} \min_{j} C_j(x_1)$ | Maximin on lower envelope | | 5 | Strategy sum (A) | $\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i = 1, \; x_i \ge 0$ | Probability axiom | | 6 | Strategy sum (B) | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} y_j = 1, \; y_j \ge 0$ | Probability axiom | | 7 | Equi-payoff condition | $E_i(y_1^*) = v^*$ for all $i$ in support | Used to find $\mathbf{x}^*$ | | 8 | Domain constraint | $0 \le y_1 \le 1, \; 0 \le x_1 \le 1$ | Valid probability range | | 9 | Reduction to LP (m×2) | Maximize $v$ s.t. $\sum_{j} a_{ij} y_j \ge v \;\; \forall i$ | LP formulation | | 10 | Reduction to LP (2×n) | Minimize $v$ s.t. $\sum_{j} a_{ij} y_j \le v \;\; \forall i$ | LP formulation | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The graphical method for $m \times 2$ and $2 \times n$ games is widely applied in: - **Military Strategy & Defense Planning**: Limited tactical options vs. many deployment scenarios. - **Marketing**: A company deciding between 2 product lines (A) against competitor's $n$ pricing policies (B). - **Bidding / Auction Design**: Contractor (A) has 2 bid strategies; client (B) has many reserve-price levels. - **Cybersecurity**: Defender chooses 2 response policies; attacker has many intrusion vectors. - **Inventory & Logistics**: 2 supplier contracts vs. many demand scenarios. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked-Out Solved Examples (WSE)** — These mirror typical KTU ESE numerical problems. --- ## 3.1 WSE-1: A 3 × 2 Game (Complete Worked Solution) **Problem Statement:** Player A (maximizer) has 3 strategies; Player B (minimizer) has 2 strategies. Payoff matrix: $$ A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 5 & 2 \\ 4 & 6 \end{pmatrix} $$ Find the optimal strategies and the value of the game using the graphical method. ### Step 1 — Write $E_i(y_1)$ for each row Let $y_1$ be the probability that B plays column 1. Then $y_2 = 1 - y_1$. **Row 1:** $$ E_1(y_1) = 1 \cdot y_1 + 3 \cdot (1 - y_1) = 3 - 2y_1 $$ **Row 2:** $$ E_2(y_1) = 5 \cdot y_1 + 2 \cdot (1 - y_1) = 2 + 3y_1 $$ **Row 3:** $$ E_3(y_1) = 4 \cdot y_1 + 6 \cdot (1 - y_1) = 6 - 2y_1 $$ ### Step 2 — Compute line endpoints at $y_1 = 0$ and $y_1 = 1$ | Line | $E_i(0)$ | $E_i(1)$ | |------|----------|----------| | $E_1$ | $3$ | $1$ | | $E_2$ | $2$ | $5$ | | $E_3$ | $6$ | $4$ | ### Step 3 — Find intersections of lines **Intersection of $E_1$ and $E_2$:** $$ 3 - 2y_1 = 2 + 3y_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 1 = 5y_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; y_1 = 0.2, \;\; E = 2.6 $$ **Intersection of $E_2$ and $E_3$:** $$ 2 + 3y_1 = 6 - 2y_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 5y_1 = 4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; y_1 = 0.8, \;\; E = 4.4 $$ **Intersection of $E_1$ and $E_3$:** $$ 3 - 2y_1 = 6 - 2y_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 3 = 6 \;\; \text{(No solution — parallel lines)} $$ ### Step 4 — Identify the Upper Envelope For each $y_1 \in [0, 1]$, compute the maximum of $\{E_1, E_2, E_3\}$. | $y_1$ | $E_1$ | $E_2$ | $E_3$ | Upper Envelope (max) | Dominant Line | |-------|-------|-------|-------|----------------------|---------------| | 0.0 | 3 | 2 | 6 | 6 | $E_3$ | | 0.2 | 2.6 | 2.6 | 5.6 | 5.6 | $E_3$ | | 0.5 | 2 | 3.5 | 5 | 5 | $E_3$ | | 0.8 | 1.4 | 4.4 | 4.4 | 4.4 | $E_2, E_3$ | | 1.0 | 1 | 5 | 4 | 5 | $E_2$ | The upper envelope: $E_3$ for $y_1 \in [0, 0.8]$, then $E_2$ for $y_1 \in [0.8, 1]$. ### Step 5 — Find the Lowest Point of the Upper Envelope The upper envelope decreases from $6$ (at $y_1 = 0$) to $4.4$ (at $y_1 = 0.8$), then increases to $5$ (at $y_1 = 1$). The **minimum** occurs at: $$ y_1^* = 0.8, \quad v^* = 4.4 $$ Hence: $$ y_1^* = 0.8, \quad y_2^* = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2 $$ ### Step 6 — Determine A's Optimal Strategy At $y_1^* = 0.8$, the lines $E_2$ and $E_3$ both equal $v^* = 4.4$. So **A's support = {Row 2, Row 3}**, with $x_1 = 0$, $x_2 + x_3 = 1$. Let $x_2 = p$, $x_3 = 1 - p$. A's expected payoff against B's strategies must equal $v^*$: **Against B's strategy 1:** $$ 5 p + 4(1 - p) = 4.4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 4 + p = 4.4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p = 0.4 $$ **Against B's strategy 2 (check):** $$ 2 p + 6(1 - p) = 2(0.4) + 6(0.6) = 0.8 + 3.6 = 4.4 \;\checkmark $$ **Final Answer (m × 2 game):** $$ \boxed{x^* = (0,\; 0.4,\; 0.6), \quad y^* = (0.8,\; 0.2), \quad v^* = 4.4} $$ --- ## 3.2 WSE-2: A 2 × 3 Game (Complete Worked Solution) **Problem Statement:** Player A has 2 strategies; Player B has 3 strategies. Payoff matrix: $$ A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 4 & 1 \\ 3 & 1 & 5 \end{pmatrix} $$ ### Step 1 — Write $C_j(x_1)$ for each column Let $x_1$ be the probability A plays row 1, so $x_2 = 1 - x_1$. **Column 1:** $$ C_1(x_1) = 2 x_1 + 3(1 - x_1) = 3 - x_1 $$ **Column 2:** $$ C_2(x_1) = 4 x_1 + 1(1 - x_1) = 1 + 3x_1 $$ **Column 3:** $$ C_3(x_1) = 1 \cdot x_1 + 5(1 - x_1) = 5 - 4x_1 $$ ### Step 2 — Endpoints | Line | $C_j(0)$ | $C_j(1)$ | |------|----------|----------| | $C_1$ | $3$ | $2$ | | $C_2$ | $1$ | $4$ | | $C_3$ | $5$ | $1$ | ### Step 3 — Pairwise Intersections **$C_1 = C_2$:** $$ 3 - x_1 = 1 + 3x_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 2 = 4x_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x_1 = 0.5, \;\; C = 2.5 $$ **$C_1 = C_3$:** $$ 3 - x_1 = 5 - 4x_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 3x_1 = 2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x_1 = \tfrac{2}{3}, \;\; C = \tfrac{7}{3} \approx 2.33 $$ **$C_2 = C_3$:** $$ 1 + 3x_1 = 5 - 4x_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 7x_1 = 4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x_1 = \tfrac{4}{7} \approx 0.571, \;\; C = \tfrac{19}{7} \approx 2.71 $$ ### Step 4 — Identify the Lower Envelope | $x_1$ | $C_1$ | $C_2$ | $C_3$ | Lower Envelope (min) | Dominant Line | |-------|-------|-------|-------|----------------------|---------------| | 0.0 | 3 | 1 | 5 | 1 | $C_2$ | | 0.3 | 2.7 | 1.9 | 3.8 | 1.9 | $C_2$ | | 0.5 | 2.5 | 2.5 | 3.0 | 2.5 | $C_1, C_2$ | | 0.6 | 2.4 | 2.8 | 2.6 | 2.4 | $C_1$ | | 0.667 | 2.33 | 3.0 | 2.33 | 2.33 | $C_1, C_3$ | | 0.8 | 2.2 | 3.4 | 1.8 | 1.8 | $C_3$ | | 1.0 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 1 | $C_3$ | The lower envelope: $C_2$ for $x_1 \in [0, 0.5]$, then $C_1$ for $x_1 \in [0.5, 2/3]$, then $C_3$ for $x_1 \in [2/3, 1]$. ### Step 5 — Find the Highest Point of the Lower Envelope The envelope rises from $1$ (at $x_1 = 0$) to $2.5$ (at $x_1 = 0.5$), then falls to $2.33$ and then to $1$. $$ x_1^* = 0.5, \quad v^* = 2.5 $$ So: $$ x_1^* = 0.5, \quad x_2^* = 0.5 $$ ### Step 6 — Determine B's Optimal Strategy At $x_1^* = 0.5$, lines $C_1$ and $C_2$ both equal $v^* = 2.5$. So **B's support = {Column 1, Column 2}**, with $y_3 = 0$, $y_1 + y_2 = 1$. Let $y_1 = q$, $y_2 = 1 - q$. B's expected payoff against A's strategies must equal $v^*$: **Against A's row 1:** $$ 2 q + 4(1 - q) = 2.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 4 - 2q = 2.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; q = 0.75 $$ **Against A's row 2 (check):** $$ 3 q + 1(1 - q) = 3(0.75) + 0.25 = 2.5 \;\checkmark $$ **Final Answer (2 × n game):** $$ \boxed{x^* = (0.5,\; 0.5), \quad y^* = (0.75,\; 0.25,\; 0), \quad v^* = 2.5} $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix | Aspect | m × 2 Game | 2 × n Game | |--------|------------|------------| | Lines drawn | $m$ lines (one per row of A) | $n$ lines (one per column of B) | | Variable on x-axis | $y_1$ (B's probability on column 1) | $x_1$ (A's probability on row 1) | | Envelope type | **Upper envelope** | **Lower envelope** | | Optimum of envelope | **Lowest point** (min of max) | **Highest point** (max of min) | | First-found player's strategy | B's optimal $\mathbf{y}^*$ | A's optimal $\mathbf{x}^*$ | | Second player's strategy | Solve from support rows | Solve from support columns | | LP equivalent | Maximize $v$ | Minimize $v$ | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Game-Theoretic Decision Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start: Given m x 2 Payoff Matrix]) --> idA{Identify
Row player A} idA --> writeA[Write E sub i of y sub 1
= a sub i1 y sub 1 + a sub i2 1 minus y sub 1
for i = 1 to m] writeA --> plotA[Plot m straight lines
on y sub 1 in 0 to 1] plotA --> envA[Construct UPPER ENVELOPE
of the m lines] envA --> minA{Find LOWEST POINT
of upper envelope} minA --> readA[Read v star and y sub 1 star] readA --> supA[Identify A's strategies
forming the envelope at y sub 1 star] supA --> solA[Solve linear system
for x sub i values] solA --> endA([Output x star, y star, v star]) startB([Start: Given 2 x n Payoff Matrix]) --> idB{Identify
Column player B} idB --> writeB[Write C sub j of x sub 1
= a sub 1j x sub 1 + a sub 2j 1 minus x sub 1
for j = 1 to n] writeB --> plotB[Plot n straight lines
on x sub 1 in 0 to 1] plotB --> envB[Construct LOWER ENVELOPE
of the n lines] envB --> maxB{Find HIGHEST POINT
of lower envelope} maxB --> readB[Read v star and x sub 1 star] readB --> supB[Identify B's strategies
forming the envelope at x sub 1 star] supB --> solB[Solve linear system
for y sub j values] solB --> endB([Output x star, y star, v star]) ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Graphical Method Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input["INPUT STAGE"] m1[Payoff Matrix
m rows x 2 cols]:::box end subgraph PreProcess["PRE-PROCESSING"] m2[Apply DOMINANCE
to reduce matrix]:::box m3[Remove dominated
rows or columns]:::box end subgraph Graph["GRAPHICAL STAGE"] m4[Write line equations
E sub i of y sub 1]:::box m5[Plot all lines
on 2D plane]:::box m6[Mark UPPER envelope]:::box m7[Locate MINIMUM of envelope]:::box end subgraph Solve["SOLVE STAGE"] m8[Read v star and y sub 1 star]:::box m9[Identify support
of Player A]:::box m10[Form 2x2 system
for x sub i]:::box m11[Solve for x sub i]:::box end subgraph Output["OUTPUT"] m12[Optimal x star
Optimal y star
Value v star]:::box end m1 --> m2 --> m3 --> m4 --> m5 --> m6 --> m7 --> m8 --> m9 --> m10 --> m11 --> m12 classDef box fill:E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:1px,color:#0D47A1 ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Mapping: Game Theory ↔ Linear Programming ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Game["Game Theory View"] g1[Two-person Zero-sum Game]:::g g2[Payoff Matrix A]:::g g3[Minimax Principle]:::g g4[Optimal Mixed Strategies]:::g end subgraph LP["Linear Programming View"] l1[LP Maximize v]:::lp l2[Constraints: Σ a sub ij y sub j >= v]:::lp l3[Probability Simplex]:::lp l4[Dual LP Solution]:::lp end g1 --> l1 g2 --> l2 g3 --> l3 g4 --> l4 classDef g fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:1px,color:#E65100 classDef lp fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:1px,color:#1B5E20 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **"State the conditions under which the graphical method is applicable for solving rectangular games. Differentiate between $m \times 2$ and $2 \times n$ games."** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **graphical method** is applicable when one player has exactly **2 pure strategies** while the opponent has any number of pure strategies — i.e., for $m \times 2$ or $2 \times n$ games. **Difference:** | Feature | $m \times 2$ Game | $2 \times n$ Game | |---------|-------------------|--------------------| | Strategies of A | $m$ (many) | 2 | | Strategies of B | 2 | $n$ (many) | | Lines plotted | $m$ lines (rows of A) | $n$ lines (columns of B) | | Envelope used | Upper envelope | Lower envelope | | Optimum | Lowest point of upper envelope | Highest point of lower envelope | | LP formulation | Maximize $v$ | Minimize $v$ | *(1 mark for applicability, 2 marks for comparison)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **"Explain the concept of the upper envelope in the graphical solution of an $m \times 2$ game. How is the value of the game obtained from it?"** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** In an $m \times 2$ game, each row $i$ of the payoff matrix gives a straight line $E_i(y_1)$ representing A's expected payoff when B plays mixed strategy $(y_1, 1 - y_1)$. The **upper envelope** is the locus of the pointwise **maximum** of all these $m$ lines at every value of $y_1 \in [0, 1]$. Geometrically, it is the curve formed by joining the highest segments of the lines. Since B is the minimizer and A is the maximizer: - For any chosen $y_1$, A's guaranteed payoff is $\max_i E_i(y_1)$ — the upper envelope value at $y_1$. - B will choose $y_1$ to **minimize** this upper envelope value. - Therefore, the **value of the game** $v^*$ is the **lowest point (minimum) on the upper envelope**, and the corresponding $y_1$ coordinate is $y_1^*$. *(2 marks for definition + concept, 1 mark for procedure)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse *upper* and *lower* envelopes — remember **m × 2 uses UPPER**, **2 × n uses LOWER**. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Plotting $v$ on the wrong axis — the payoff $v$ MUST be on the vertical ($y$) axis; the probability is on the horizontal ($x$) axis. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to **check** the solution by verifying $E_i(y_1^*) = v^*$ for all rows in the support. > - **Common Mistake 4:** Mark loss of 2 marks when $x_i$ or $y_j$ are not normalized (sum should equal 1). > - **Common Mistake 5:** Not identifying the **support correctly** — only the lines meeting at the optimum point form the support. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Solve an $m \times 2$ Game **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** Solve the following $4 \times 2$ game using the graphical method. Determine the optimal strategies and the value of the game. **(7 Marks)** $$ A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 5 \\ 6 & 1 \\ 3 & 8 \\ 4 & 4 \end{pmatrix} $$ **(b)** A firm can launch a product using one of 4 marketing campaigns (A1, A2, A3, A4). A rival firm can respond with one of 2 promotional tactics (B1 = heavy discount, B2 = premium positioning). The expected profit (in ₹ lakhs) of the firm given the (campaign, tactic) pair is given by the matrix above. Determine the firm's optimal marketing strategy and the guaranteed minimum profit. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Write line equations** with $y_1$ = probability of B playing strategy 1: $$ \begin{aligned} E_1(y_1) &= 2 y_1 + 5(1 - y_1) = 5 - 3 y_1 \\ E_2(y_1) &= 6 y_1 + 1(1 - y_1) = 1 + 5 y_1 \\ E_3(y_1) &= 3 y_1 + 8(1 - y_1) = 8 - 5 y_1 \\ E_4(y_1) &= 4 y_1 + 4(1 - y_1) = 4 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Endpoints** | Line | $E_i(0)$ | $E_i(1)$ | |------|----------|----------| | $E_1$ | 5 | 2 | | $E_2$ | 1 | 6 | | $E_3$ | 8 | 3 | | $E_4$ | 4 | 4 | **Step 3: Find intersections** $E_2 = E_3$: $1 + 5y_1 = 8 - 5y_1 \Rightarrow 10y_1 = 7 \Rightarrow y_1 = 0.7$, $E = 4.5$ $E_3 = E_4$: $8 - 5y_1 = 4 \Rightarrow y_1 = 0.8$, $E = 4$ $E_1 = E_4$: $5 - 3y_1 = 4 \Rightarrow y_1 = 1/3 \approx 0.333$, $E = 4$ $E_1 = E_3$: $5 - 3y_1 = 8 - 5y_1 \Rightarrow 2y_1 = 3 \Rightarrow y_1 = 1.5$ (out of range) $E_1 = E_2$: $5 - 3y_1 = 1 + 5y_1 \Rightarrow 4 = 8y_1 \Rightarrow y_1 = 0.5$, $E = 3.5$ $E_2 = E_4$: $1 + 5y_1 = 4 \Rightarrow y_1 = 0.6$, $E = 4$ **Step 4: Determine Upper Envelope (sample values)** | $y_1$ | $E_1$ | $E_2$ | $E_3$ | $E_4$ | Upper Envelope | |-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|----------------| | 0.0 | 5 | 1 | 8 | 4 | 8 ($E_3$) | | 0.5 | 3.5 | 3.5 | 5.5 | 4 | 5.5 ($E_3$) | | 0.6 | 3.2 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 5 ($E_3$) | | 0.7 | 2.9 | 4.5 | 4.5 | 4 | 4.5 ($E_2, E_3$)| | 0.8 | 2.6 | 5 | 4 | 4 | 5 ($E_2$) | | 1.0 | 2 | 6 | 3 | 4 | 6 ($E_2$) | Upper envelope: $E_3$ for $y_1 \in [0, 0.7]$, then $E_2$ for $y_1 \in [0.7, 1]$. **Step 5: Lowest point** is at intersection of $E_2$ and $E_3$: $$ \boxed{y_1^* = 0.7, \quad y_2^* = 0.3, \quad v^* = 4.5} $$ **Step 6: A's support = {Row 2, Row 3}**, so $x_1 = 0$, $x_4 = 0$, $x_2 + x_3 = 1$. Let $x_2 = p$. Setting payoffs vs both columns equal to $v^*$: $$ 6 p + 3(1 - p) = 4.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 3 + 3p = 4.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p = 0.5 $$ Check column 2: $1(0.5) + 8(0.5) = 4.5$ ✓ $$ \boxed{x^* = (0,\; 0.5,\; 0.5,\; 0), \quad y^* = (0.7,\; 0.3), \quad v^* = 4.5} $$ #### Part (b) — 7 Marks The numerical payoff matrix matches Part (a). So the optimal strategy is identical: - The firm should allocate **equal budget (50% each)** to campaigns A2 and A3, and avoid A1 and A4. - The rival will play B1 with probability 0.7 and B2 with probability 0.3. - **Guaranteed minimum profit = ₹4.5 lakhs.** *(Award 3 marks for interpretation, 4 marks for numerical extraction.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Solve a $2 \times n$ Game **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** Solve the following $2 \times 4$ game graphically and find the optimal strategies and the value of the game. **(7 Marks)** $$ A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 6 & 2 & 5 \\ 3 & 1 & 7 & 4 \end{pmatrix} $$ **(b)** Two companies compete in a market. Company A (maximizer) has 2 production technologies (Tech-I, Tech-II). Company B (minimizer) has 4 pricing strategies (P1, P2, P3, P4). The profit matrix is as given above. Determine the equilibrium strategies. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Line equations** with $x_1$ = probability A plays Row 1: $$ \begin{aligned} C_1(x_1) &= 4 x_1 + 3(1 - x_1) = 3 + x_1 \\ C_2(x_1) &= 6 x_1 + 1(1 - x_1) = 1 + 5 x_1 \\ C_3(x_1) &= 2 x_1 + 7(1 - x_1) = 7 - 5 x_1 \\ C_4(x_1) &= 5 x_1 + 4(1 - x_1) = 4 + x_1 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Endpoints** | Line | $C_j(0)$ | $C_j(1)$ | |------|----------|----------| | $C_1$ | 3 | 4 | | $C_2$ | 1 | 6 | | $C_3$ | 7 | 2 | | $C_4$ | 4 | 5 | **Step 3: Find intersections** $C_1 = C_2$: $3 + x_1 = 1 + 5x_1 \Rightarrow x_1 = 0.5$, $C = 3.5$ $C_1 = C_3$: $3 + x_1 = 7 - 5x_1 \Rightarrow 6x_1 = 4 \Rightarrow x_1 = 2/3$, $C = 11/3 \approx 3.67$ $C_1 = C_4$: $3 + x_1 = 4 + x_1 \Rightarrow$ No solution (parallel) $C_2 = C_3$: $1 + 5x_1 = 7 - 5x_1 \Rightarrow x_1 = 0.6$, $C = 4$ $C_2 = C_4$: $1 + 5x_1 = 4 + x_1 \Rightarrow 4x_1 = 3 \Rightarrow x_1 = 0.75$, $C = 4.75$ $C_3 = C_4$: $7 - 5x_1 = 4 + x_1 \Rightarrow 6x_1 = 3 \Rightarrow x_1 = 0.5$, $C = 4.5$ **Step 4: Determine Lower Envelope** | $x_1$ | $C_1$ | $C_2$ | $C_3$ | $C_4$ | Lower Envelope | |-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|----------------| | 0.0 | 3 | 1 | 7 | 4 | 1 ($C_2$) | | 0.4 | 3.4 | 3 | 5 | 4.4 | 3 ($C_2$) | | 0.5 | 3.5 | 3.5 | 4.5 | 4.5 | 3.5 ($C_1, C_2$)| | 0.6 | 3.6 | 4 | 4 | 4.6 | 3.6 ($C_1$) | | 0.667 | 3.67 | 4.33 | 3.67 | 4.67 | 3.67 ($C_1, C_3$)| | 0.75 | 3.75 | 4.75 | 3.25 | 4.75 | 3.25 ($C_3$) | | 1.0 | 4 | 6 | 2 | 5 | 2 ($C_3$) | Lower envelope: $C_2$ on $[0, 0.5]$, $C_1$ on $[0.5, 2/3]$, $C_3$ on $[2/3, 1]$. **Step 5: Highest point** is at $x_1 = 0.5$, where $C_1 = C_2 = 3.5$. Wait — checking the highest point: the envelope rises from 1 to 3.5 (at 0.5), then declines. So: $$ \boxed{x_1^* = 0.5, \quad x_2^* = 0.5, \quad v^* = 3.5} $$ **Step 6: B's support = {Column 1, Column 2}**, so $y_3 = 0$, $y_4 = 0$, $y_1 + y_2 = 1$. Let $y_1 = q$. Setting payoffs vs both rows equal to $v^*$: $$ 4 q + 6(1 - q) = 3.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 6 - 2q = 3.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; q = 1.25 $$ This is **out of range**. This means our chosen vertex is actually **not feasible** at the boundary. Let us try the alternative: maybe the highest point is elsewhere. Re-examining: at $x_1 = 0.5$, $C_1 = C_2 = 3.5$, but $C_3 = C_4 = 4.5$ (above envelope). So both $C_1$ and $C_2$ touch the lower envelope at $x_1 = 0.5$. **Re-solve for B's strategy** by ensuring payoffs vs both rows equal $v^* = 3.5$: Row 1: $4 y_1 + 6 y_2 = 3.5$ Row 2: $3 y_1 + 1 y_2 = 3.5$ Constraint: $y_1 + y_2 = 1 \Rightarrow y_2 = 1 - y_1$ Substitute in Row 2: $3 y_1 + 1(1 - y_1) = 3.5 \Rightarrow 2 y_1 + 1 = 3.5 \Rightarrow y_1 = 1.25$ Since $y_1 > 1$, this is infeasible — meaning the vertex at $x_1 = 0.5$ is **NOT the maximin** of the lower envelope. We must look at endpoints. Re-examining the lower envelope values: | $x_1$ | Lower Envelope Value | |-------|----------------------| | 0 | 1 | | 0.5 | 3.5 | | 0.667 | 3.67 | | 0.75 | 3.25 | | 1 | 2 | The **maximum** of the lower envelope is at $x_1 = 2/3$, with $v^* = 11/3 \approx 3.67$. **Corrected Step 5:** $$ \boxed{x_1^* = 2/3, \quad x_2^* = 1/3, \quad v^* = 11/3} $$ **Corrected Step 6:** At $x_1^* = 2/3$, lines $C_1$ and $C_3$ meet. So **B's support = {Column 1, Column 3}**, $y_2 = 0$, $y_4 = 0$, $y_1 + y_3 = 1$. Let $y_1 = q$, $y_3 = 1 - q$. Row 1: $4 q + 2(1 - q) = 11/3 \Rightarrow 2 + 2q = 11/3 \Rightarrow 2q = 5/3 \Rightarrow q = 5/6$ Check Row 2: $3(5/6) + 7(1/6) = 15/6 + 7/6 = 22/6 = 11/3$ ✓ $$ \boxed{x^* = \left(\tfrac{2}{3},\; \tfrac{1}{3}\right), \quad y^* = \left(\tfrac{5}{6},\; 0,\; \tfrac{1}{6},\; 0\right), \quad v^* = \tfrac{11}{3} \approx 3.667} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Valuator Note:** Many students will mistakenly choose $x_1 = 0.5$ without checking feasibility of the resulting $y$ values. Always **check** that the $y_j$ obtained are non-negative and sum to 1. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks - Company A should use **Tech-I with probability 2/3** and **Tech-II with probability 1/3**. - Company B should play **P1 with probability 5/6** and **P3 with probability 1/6** (and avoid P2, P4). - **Equilibrium value = ₹11/3 ≈ ₹3.67 lakhs** (gain for A). *(4 marks for numerical extraction, 3 marks for managerial interpretation.)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam** ### Quick-Fire Facts - ✅ **m × 2 game**: Player A has $m$ strategies, B has 2 strategies. Plot **m lines** → form **UPPER envelope** → find **MINIMUM**. - ✅ **2 × n game**: Player A has 2 strategies, B has $n$ strategies. Plot **n lines** → form **LOWER envelope** → find **MAXIMUM**. - ✅ **Always plot $v$ (payoff) on the y-axis** and the probability on the x-axis. - ✅ **Endpoint check**: $y_1$ (or $x_1$) ranges only over $[0, 1]$. Ignore intersections outside this range. - ✅ **Support identification**: Only the lines meeting at the optimum point are in the player's support. - ✅ **Verification step**: Substitute the opponent's optimal strategy back into all rows (or columns) of the support; they must all yield $v^*$. ### Formula Flashcards - 📌 $E_i(y_1) = a_{i1} y_1 + a_{i2}(1 - y_1)$ → Line for each row of A in m×2 - 📌 $C_j(x_1) = a_{1j} x_1 + a_{2j}(1 - x_1)$ → Line for each column of B in 2×n - 📌 $v^* = \min_{y_1} \max_i E_i(y_1)$ → m×2 game value - 📌 $v^* = \max_{x_1} \min_j C_j(x_1)$ → 2×n game value - 📌 $\sum_i x_i = 1, \; \sum_j y_j = 1, \; x_i, y_j \ge 0$ → Probability constraints ### Common Pitfalls - ⚠️ Mixing up **upper** vs **lower** envelopes. - ⚠️ Forgetting that the player's strategy with **2 strategies** has its probabilities read directly from the graph; only the other player's strategy needs solving. - ⚠️ Not using **dominance** to reduce the matrix size before graphical plotting (a key time-saver in exams). - ⚠️ Reporting probabilities without normalization check (sum must be 1). ### KTU Mark Distribution Snapshot (Typical) - **Part A (3 marks)**: Concept / definition / comparison — expects crisp 1-page answer. - **Part B (14 marks)**: Numericals dominate (~10 marks numerical + ~4 marks interpretation or alternative method). > **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"M-twice UP-min" / "Twice-N DOWN-max"** — > *m × 2*: Upper envelope, take Minimum → B's strategy first. > *2 × n*: Lower envelope, take Maximum → A's strategy first.
